revision.text:
'''Kitsumoto Shrine''' is one of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|fr|Kujūkuōji|ja|九十九王子|lt=99 ōji Shrines}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. <!--subst:PAGENAME magic word should be substituted in the future when this is moved to its final pagename. This was added to a few pages not created on Feb 8 because they were very barren and this gives context to the fact that they are in a large group of pages all to be translated more or less as a group similar to the Ise Shrines--> ==Overview== == Kitsumoto Shrine == '''Kitsumoto Shrine''' (橘本神社) is a Shinto shrine located in {{ill|Kainan, Wakayama|ja|海南市}}, {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|ja|和歌山県}}, Japan. The shrine enshrines {{nihongo|Tajimamori|田道間守}} and {{nihongo|Kumanoza-no-Ōkami|熊野坐大神}}, and is revered as the deity of confectionery. It is designated as a {{ill|Prefectural Historic Site|ja|和歌山県指定史跡}} (since April 9, 1974), as the former site of {{nihongo|Tokosaka Ōji|所坂王子}}, one of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子}} shrines.<ref name="Kinen">{{Cite web|title=Prefectural Cultural Properties: Historic Sites|url=https://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/prefg/500700/mokuroku/kenkinenbutsu.html|publisher=Wakayama Prefecture Board of Education|accessdate=2021-07-25}}</ref>
== History == According to the ''Kojiki'' and ''Nihon Shoki'', Emperor Suinin ordered Tajimamori to find the legendary fruit of eternal youth. After enduring over ten years of hardship, he returned from the mythical {{nihongo|Tokoyo no Kuni|常世の国}} (often identified with ancient China) with the fruit known as {{nihongo|Tokijiku no Kagunokonomi|非時香菓}}, identified as the {{nihongo|tachibana|橘}}, an ancient citrus fruit. Unfortunately, the emperor had already passed away. In despair, Tajimamori offered the tachibana at the emperor’s tomb and died shortly after.<ref name="Mikan">{{Cite web|title=Mikan Festival|url=http://www.d1.dion.ne.jp/~konchi/kitumoto/menu/mikanmaturi.htm|publisher=Kitsumoto Shrine|accessdate=2015-02-14}}</ref><ref name="Kainanshi">{{Cite web|title=Kitsumoto Shrine (Origin of Mikan, Deity of Confectionery)|url=https://www.city.kainan.lg.jp/kakubusho/machizukuribu/norinsuisangakari/michinoeki/1543927025145.html|publisher=Kainan City|date=2021-03-01|accessdate=2021-07-25}}</ref>
It is said that the tachibana was first planted in Japan at the "Hill of Six Trees," the former site of the shrine to the north. The tachibana is considered the ancestor of modern mandarins, and this region is known as the birthplace of mikan cultivation.<ref name="Mikan"/>
The shrine’s founding date is unknown, but a roof plaque dated 1437 (Eikyō 9) still exists.<ref name="Rekishi">{{Cite web|title=Shrine History|url=http://www.d1.dion.ne.jp/~konchi/kitumoto/menu/framepage.htm|publisher=Kitsumoto Shrine|accessdate=2015-02-14}}</ref><ref name="Nikkeii">{{Cite web|title=Why Is the "Confectionery Deity" in Wakayama? (Mystery Cruise)|url=https://www.nikkei.com/article/DGXLASHD15H02_V10C14A8AA1P00/|publisher=Nikkei|date=2014-08-20|accessdate=2021-07-25}}</ref>
In 1158 (Hōgen 3), Emperor Shirakawa reportedly stayed at the shrine during his pilgrimage to Kumano and composed the poem: ''"Spending a night under the tachibana tree, I gaze at the moon over Mt. Irisa."''<ref name="Rekishi"/>
During the Edo period, Kishu Domain lord {{nihongo|Tokugawa Mitsusada|徳川光貞}} ordered the repair of the shrine in 1687 (Jōkyō 4). However, it later fell into decline, leaving only an old tachibana tree and a small shrine by the early Meiji era. Thanks to local efforts, the shrine was restored, and in November 1907, it was relocated to its current site on the former grounds of Tokosaka Ōji. At this time, two other local Ōji shrines—{{nihongo|Tōge-shita Ōji|搭下王子}} and {{nihongo|Kitsumoto Ōji|橘本王子}}—were also enshrined here.<ref name="Rekishi"/><ref name="Nikkeii"/>
== Festivals == The region is famous for mikan production, and the annual festival on October 10 is known as the ''Mikan Festival,'' commemorating Tajimamori’s introduction of citrus to Japan.<ref name="Mikan"/><ref name="Kainanshi"/>
Additionally, the ''Confectionery Festival'' (''Zenkoku Meika Hōkensai'') is held on the first Sunday of April. This event honors Tajimamori as the deity of sweets, as mikan were historically regarded as confections. Confectioners from across Japan offer their specialty sweets in prayer for prosperity.<ref>{{Cite web|title=Confectionery Festival|url=http://www.d1.dion.ne.jp/~konchi/kitumoto/menu/kashimaturi.htm|publisher=Kitsumoto Shrine|accessdate=2015-02-14}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|title=Kitsumoto Shrine’s Confectionery Festival|url=https://www.city.kainan.lg.jp/kanko/matsuri/1543928726429.html|publisher=Kainan City|date=2021-03-18|accessdate=2021-07-25}}</ref>
== Deities Enshrined == * {{nihongo|Tajimamori|田道間守}} – Deity of sweets and mikan cultivation * {{nihongo|Kumanoza-no-Ōkami|熊野坐大神}} – Associated with the Kumano faith
== Access == * Located in {{ill|Kainan, Wakayama|ja|海南市}}, accessible via local transportation routes.
== Gallery == <gallery> File:Kitsumoto Shrine Entrance.jpg|Entrance to Kitsumoto Shrine </gallery>
== See Also == * {{ill|Kujūku Ōji (Kainan)|ja|九十九王子 (海南市)}}

revision.text:
'''Kitsumoto Shrine''' is one of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|fr|Kujūkuōji|ja|九十九王子|lt=99 ōji Shrines}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. <!--subst:PAGENAME magic word should be substituted in the future when this is moved to its final pagename. This was added to a few pages not created on Feb 8 because they were very barren and this gives context to the fact that they are in a large group of pages all to be translated more or less as a group similar to the Ise Shrines--> ==Overview== == Kitsumoto Shrine == '''Kitsumoto Shrine''' (橘本神社) is a Shinto shrine located in {{ill|Kainan, Wakayama|ja|海南市}}, {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|ja|和歌山県}}, Japan. The shrine enshrines {{nihongo|Tajimamori|田道間守}} and {{nihongo|Kumanoza-no-Ōkami|熊野坐大神}}, and is revered as the deity of confectionery. It is designated as a {{ill|Prefectural Historic Site|ja|和歌山県指定史跡}} (since April 9, 1974), as the former site of {{nihongo|Tokosaka Ōji|所坂王子}}, one of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子}} shrines.<ref name="Kinen">{{Cite web|title=Prefectural Cultural Properties: Historic Sites|url=https://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/prefg/500700/mokuroku/kenkinenbutsu.html|publisher=Wakayama Prefecture Board of Education|accessdate=2021-07-25}}</ref>
== History == According to the ''Kojiki'' and ''Nihon Shoki'', Emperor Suinin ordered Tajimamori to find the legendary fruit of eternal youth. After enduring over ten years of hardship, he returned from the mythical {{nihongo|Tokoyo no Kuni|常世の国}} (often identified with ancient China) with the fruit known as {{nihongo|Tokijiku no Kagunokonomi|非時香菓}}, identified as the {{nihongo|tachibana|橘}}, an ancient citrus fruit. Unfortunately, the emperor had already passed away. In despair, Tajimamori offered the tachibana at the emperor’s tomb and died shortly after.<ref name="Mikan">{{Cite web|title=Mikan Festival|url=http://www.d1.dion.ne.jp/~konchi/kitumoto/menu/mikanmaturi.htm|publisher=Kitsumoto Shrine|accessdate=2015-02-14}}</ref><ref name="Kainanshi">{{Cite web|title=Kitsumoto Shrine (Origin of Mikan, Deity of Confectionery)|url=https://www.city.kainan.lg.jp/kakubusho/machizukuribu/norinsuisangakari/michinoeki/1543927025145.html|publisher=Kainan City|date=2021-03-01|accessdate=2021-07-25}}</ref>
It is said that the tachibana was first planted in Japan at the "Hill of Six Trees," the former site of the shrine to the north. The tachibana is considered the ancestor of modern mandarins, and this region is known as the birthplace of mikan cultivation.<ref name="Mikan"/>
The shrine’s founding date is unknown, but a roof plaque dated 1437 (Eikyō 9) still exists.<ref name="Rekishi">{{Cite web|title=Shrine History|url=http://www.d1.dion.ne.jp/~konchi/kitumoto/menu/framepage.htm|publisher=Kitsumoto Shrine|accessdate=2015-02-14}}</ref><ref name="Nikkeii">{{Cite web|title=Why Is the "Confectionery Deity" in Wakayama? (Mystery Cruise)|url=https://www.nikkei.com/article/DGXLASHD15H02_V10C14A8AA1P00/|publisher=Nikkei|date=2014-08-20|accessdate=2021-07-25}}</ref>
In 1158 (Hōgen 3), Emperor Shirakawa reportedly stayed at the shrine during his pilgrimage to Kumano and composed the poem: ''"Spending a night under the tachibana tree, I gaze at the moon over Mt. Irisa."''<ref name="Rekishi"/>
During the Edo period, Kishu Domain lord {{nihongo|Tokugawa Mitsusada|徳川光貞}} ordered the repair of the shrine in 1687 (Jōkyō 4). However, it later fell into decline, leaving only an old tachibana tree and a small shrine by the early Meiji era. Thanks to local efforts, the shrine was restored, and in November 1907, it was relocated to its current site on the former grounds of Tokosaka Ōji. At this time, two other local Ōji shrines—{{nihongo|Tōge-shita Ōji|搭下王子}} and {{nihongo|Kitsumoto Ōji|橘本王子}}—were also enshrined here.<ref name="Rekishi"/><ref name="Nikkeii"/>
== Festivals == The region is famous for mikan production, and the annual festival on October 10 is known as the ''Mikan Festival,'' commemorating Tajimamori’s introduction of citrus to Japan.<ref name="Mikan"/><ref name="Kainanshi"/>
Additionally, the ''Confectionery Festival'' (''Zenkoku Meika Hōkensai'') is held on the first Sunday of April. This event honors Tajimamori as the deity of sweets, as mikan were historically regarded as confections. Confectioners from across Japan offer their specialty sweets in prayer for prosperity.<ref>{{Cite web|title=Confectionery Festival|url=http://www.d1.dion.ne.jp/~konchi/kitumoto/menu/kashimaturi.htm|publisher=Kitsumoto Shrine|accessdate=2015-02-14}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|title=Kitsumoto Shrine’s Confectionery Festival|url=https://www.city.kainan.lg.jp/kanko/matsuri/1543928726429.html|publisher=Kainan City|date=2021-03-18|accessdate=2021-07-25}}</ref>
== Deities Enshrined == * {{nihongo|Tajimamori|田道間守}} – Deity of sweets and mikan cultivation * {{nihongo|Kumanoza-no-Ōkami|熊野坐大神}} – Associated with the Kumano faith
== Access == * Located in {{ill|Kainan, Wakayama|ja|海南市}}, accessible via local transportation routes.
== Gallery == <gallery> File:Kitsumoto Shrine Entrance.jpg|Entrance to Kitsumoto Shrine </gallery>

revision.comment: Copying from [[Category:Subst:MAINSPACE]] to [[Category:q-{{subst:NAMESPACE}}-q]] using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
'''Kitsumoto Shrine''' is one of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|fr|Kujūkuōji|ja|九十九王子|lt=99 ōji Shrines}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. <!--subst:PAGENAME magic word should be substituted in the future when this is moved to its final pagename. This was added to a few pages not created on Feb 8 because they were very barren and this gives context to the fact that they are in a large group of pages all to be translated more or less as a group similar to the Ise Shrines--> ==Overview== == Kitsumoto Shrine == '''Kitsumoto Shrine''' (橘本神社) is a Shinto shrine located in {{ill|Kainan, Wakayama|ja|海南市}}, {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|ja|和歌山県}}, Japan. The shrine enshrines {{nihongo|Tajimamori|田道間守}} and {{nihongo|Kumanoza-no-Ōkami|熊野坐大神}}, and is revered as the deity of confectionery. It is designated as a {{ill|Prefectural Historic Site|ja|和歌山県指定史跡}} (since April 9, 1974), as the former site of {{nihongo|Tokosaka Ōji|所坂王子}}, one of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子}} shrines.<ref name="Kinen">{{Cite web|title=Prefectural Cultural Properties: Historic Sites|url=https://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/prefg/500700/mokuroku/kenkinenbutsu.html|publisher=Wakayama Prefecture Board of Education|accessdate=2021-07-25}}</ref>
== History == According to the ''Kojiki'' and ''Nihon Shoki'', Emperor Suinin ordered Tajimamori to find the legendary fruit of eternal youth. After enduring over ten years of hardship, he returned from the mythical {{nihongo|Tokoyo no Kuni|常世の国}} (often identified with ancient China) with the fruit known as {{nihongo|Tokijiku no Kagunokonomi|非時香菓}}, identified as the {{nihongo|tachibana|橘}}, an ancient citrus fruit. Unfortunately, the emperor had already passed away. In despair, Tajimamori offered the tachibana at the emperor’s tomb and died shortly after.<ref name="Mikan">{{Cite web|title=Mikan Festival|url=http://www.d1.dion.ne.jp/~konchi/kitumoto/menu/mikanmaturi.htm|publisher=Kitsumoto Shrine|accessdate=2015-02-14}}</ref><ref name="Kainanshi">{{Cite web|title=Kitsumoto Shrine (Origin of Mikan, Deity of Confectionery)|url=https://www.city.kainan.lg.jp/kakubusho/machizukuribu/norinsuisangakari/michinoeki/1543927025145.html|publisher=Kainan City|date=2021-03-01|accessdate=2021-07-25}}</ref>
It is said that the tachibana was first planted in Japan at the "Hill of Six Trees," the former site of the shrine to the north. The tachibana is considered the ancestor of modern mandarins, and this region is known as the birthplace of mikan cultivation.<ref name="Mikan"/>
The shrine’s founding date is unknown, but a roof plaque dated 1437 (Eikyō 9) still exists.<ref name="Rekishi">{{Cite web|title=Shrine History|url=http://www.d1.dion.ne.jp/~konchi/kitumoto/menu/framepage.htm|publisher=Kitsumoto Shrine|accessdate=2015-02-14}}</ref><ref name="Nikkeii">{{Cite web|title=Why Is the "Confectionery Deity" in Wakayama? (Mystery Cruise)|url=https://www.nikkei.com/article/DGXLASHD15H02_V10C14A8AA1P00/|publisher=Nikkei|date=2014-08-20|accessdate=2021-07-25}}</ref>
In 1158 (Hōgen 3), Emperor Shirakawa reportedly stayed at the shrine during his pilgrimage to Kumano and composed the poem: ''"Spending a night under the tachibana tree, I gaze at the moon over Mt. Irisa."''<ref name="Rekishi"/>
During the Edo period, Kishu Domain lord {{nihongo|Tokugawa Mitsusada|徳川光貞}} ordered the repair of the shrine in 1687 (Jōkyō 4). However, it later fell into decline, leaving only an old tachibana tree and a small shrine by the early Meiji era. Thanks to local efforts, the shrine was restored, and in November 1907, it was relocated to its current site on the former grounds of Tokosaka Ōji. At this time, two other local Ōji shrines—{{nihongo|Tōge-shita Ōji|搭下王子}} and {{nihongo|Kitsumoto Ōji|橘本王子}}—were also enshrined here.<ref name="Rekishi"/><ref name="Nikkeii"/>
== Festivals == The region is famous for mikan production, and the annual festival on October 10 is known as the ''Mikan Festival,'' commemorating Tajimamori’s introduction of citrus to Japan.<ref name="Mikan"/><ref name="Kainanshi"/>
Additionally, the ''Confectionery Festival'' (''Zenkoku Meika Hōkensai'') is held on the first Sunday of April. This event honors Tajimamori as the deity of sweets, as mikan were historically regarded as confections. Confectioners from across Japan offer their specialty sweets in prayer for prosperity.<ref>{{Cite web|title=Confectionery Festival|url=http://www.d1.dion.ne.jp/~konchi/kitumoto/menu/kashimaturi.htm|publisher=Kitsumoto Shrine|accessdate=2015-02-14}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|title=Kitsumoto Shrine’s Confectionery Festival|url=https://www.city.kainan.lg.jp/kanko/matsuri/1543928726429.html|publisher=Kainan City|date=2021-03-18|accessdate=2021-07-25}}</ref>
== Deities Enshrined == * {{nihongo|Tajimamori|田道間守}} – Deity of sweets and mikan cultivation * {{nihongo|Kumanoza-no-Ōkami|熊野坐大神}} – Associated with the Kumano faith
== Access == * Located in {{ill|Kainan, Wakayama|ja|海南市}}, accessible via local transportation routes.
== Gallery == <gallery> File:Kitsumoto Shrine Entrance.jpg|Entrance to Kitsumoto Shrine </gallery>
== See Also == * {{ill|Kujūku Ōji (Kainan)|ja|九十九王子 (海南市)}}
== References == {{Reflist}}
== External Links == * {{Wayback|url=http://www.d1.dion.ne.jp/~konchi/kitumoto/ |title=Kitsumoto Shrine (Official Site)|date=2004-02-16}} * [https://wakayama-jinjacho.or.jp/jdb/sys/user/GetWjtTbl.php?JinjyaNo=2021 Kitsumoto Shrine – Wakayama Prefecture Shrine Office]
{{Shinto-stub}}

revision.text:
'''Kitsumoto Shrine''' is one of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|fr|Kujūkuōji|ja|九十九王子|lt=99 ōji Shrines}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. <!--subst:PAGENAME magic word should be substituted in the future when this is moved to its final pagename. This was added to a few pages not created on Feb 8 because they were very barren and this gives context to the fact that they are in a large group of pages all to be translated more or less as a group similar to the Ise Shrines--> ==Overview== == Kitsumoto Shrine == '''Kitsumoto Shrine''' (橘本神社) is a Shinto shrine located in {{ill|Kainan, Wakayama|ja|海南市}}, {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|ja|和歌山県}}, Japan. The shrine enshrines {{nihongo|Tajimamori|田道間守}} and {{nihongo|Kumanoza-no-Ōkami|熊野坐大神}}, and is revered as the deity of confectionery. It is designated as a {{ill|Prefectural Historic Site|ja|和歌山県指定史跡}} (since April 9, 1974), as the former site of {{nihongo|Tokosaka Ōji|所坂王子}}, one of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子}} shrines.<ref name="Kinen">{{Cite web|title=Prefectural Cultural Properties: Historic Sites|url=https://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/prefg/500700/mokuroku/kenkinenbutsu.html|publisher=Wakayama Prefecture Board of Education|accessdate=2021-07-25}}</ref>
== History == According to the ''Kojiki'' and ''Nihon Shoki'', Emperor Suinin ordered Tajimamori to find the legendary fruit of eternal youth. After enduring over ten years of hardship, he returned from the mythical {{nihongo|Tokoyo no Kuni|常世の国}} (often identified with ancient China) with the fruit known as {{nihongo|Tokijiku no Kagunokonomi|非時香菓}}, identified as the {{nihongo|tachibana|橘}}, an ancient citrus fruit. Unfortunately, the emperor had already passed away. In despair, Tajimamori offered the tachibana at the emperor’s tomb and died shortly after.<ref name="Mikan">{{Cite web|title=Mikan Festival|url=http://www.d1.dion.ne.jp/~konchi/kitumoto/menu/mikanmaturi.htm|publisher=Kitsumoto Shrine|accessdate=2015-02-14}}</ref><ref name="Kainanshi">{{Cite web|title=Kitsumoto Shrine (Origin of Mikan, Deity of Confectionery)|url=https://www.city.kainan.lg.jp/kakubusho/machizukuribu/norinsuisangakari/michinoeki/1543927025145.html|publisher=Kainan City|date=2021-03-01|accessdate=2021-07-25}}</ref>
It is said that the tachibana was first planted in Japan at the "Hill of Six Trees," the former site of the shrine to the north. The tachibana is considered the ancestor of modern mandarins, and this region is known as the birthplace of mikan cultivation.<ref name="Mikan"/>
The shrine’s founding date is unknown, but a roof plaque dated 1437 (Eikyō 9) still exists.<ref name="Rekishi">{{Cite web|title=Shrine History|url=http://www.d1.dion.ne.jp/~konchi/kitumoto/menu/framepage.htm|publisher=Kitsumoto Shrine|accessdate=2015-02-14}}</ref><ref name="Nikkeii">{{Cite web|title=Why Is the "Confectionery Deity" in Wakayama? (Mystery Cruise)|url=https://www.nikkei.com/article/DGXLASHD15H02_V10C14A8AA1P00/|publisher=Nikkei|date=2014-08-20|accessdate=2021-07-25}}</ref>
In 1158 (Hōgen 3), Emperor Shirakawa reportedly stayed at the shrine during his pilgrimage to Kumano and composed the poem: ''"Spending a night under the tachibana tree, I gaze at the moon over Mt. Irisa."''<ref name="Rekishi"/>
During the Edo period, Kishu Domain lord {{nihongo|Tokugawa Mitsusada|徳川光貞}} ordered the repair of the shrine in 1687 (Jōkyō 4). However, it later fell into decline, leaving only an old tachibana tree and a small shrine by the early Meiji era. Thanks to local efforts, the shrine was restored, and in November 1907, it was relocated to its current site on the former grounds of Tokosaka Ōji. At this time, two other local Ōji shrines—{{nihongo|Tōge-shita Ōji|搭下王子}} and {{nihongo|Kitsumoto Ōji|橘本王子}}—were also enshrined here.<ref name="Rekishi"/><ref name="Nikkeii"/>
== Festivals == The region is famous for mikan production, and the annual festival on October 10 is known as the ''Mikan Festival,'' commemorating Tajimamori’s introduction of citrus to Japan.<ref name="Mikan"/><ref name="Kainanshi"/>
Additionally, the ''Confectionery Festival'' (''Zenkoku Meika Hōkensai'') is held on the first Sunday of April. This event honors Tajimamori as the deity of sweets, as mikan were historically regarded as confections. Confectioners from across Japan offer their specialty sweets in prayer for prosperity.<ref>{{Cite web|title=Confectionery Festival|url=http://www.d1.dion.ne.jp/~konchi/kitumoto/menu/kashimaturi.htm|publisher=Kitsumoto Shrine|accessdate=2015-02-14}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|title=Kitsumoto Shrine’s Confectionery Festival|url=https://www.city.kainan.lg.jp/kanko/matsuri/1543928726429.html|publisher=Kainan City|date=2021-03-18|accessdate=2021-07-25}}</ref>
== Deities Enshrined == * {{nihongo|Tajimamori|田道間守}} – Deity of sweets and mikan cultivation * {{nihongo|Kumanoza-no-Ōkami|熊野坐大神}} – Associated with the Kumano faith
== Access == * Located in {{ill|Kainan, Wakayama|ja|海南市}}, accessible via local transportation routes.
== Gallery == <gallery> File:Kitsumoto Shrine Entrance.jpg|Entrance to Kitsumoto Shrine </gallery>
== See Also == * {{ill|Kujūku Ōji (Kainan)|ja|九十九王子 (海南市)}}
== References == {{Reflist}}
== External Links == * {{Wayback|url=http://www.d1.dion.ne.jp/~konchi/kitumoto/ |title=Kitsumoto Shrine (Official Site)|date=2004-02-16}} * [https://wakayama-jinjacho.or.jp/jdb/sys/user/GetWjtTbl.php?JinjyaNo=2021 Kitsumoto Shrine – Wakayama Prefecture Shrine Office]
{{Shinto-stub}}

revision.comment: mainspace to namespace
revision.text:
'''Kitsumoto Shrine''' is one of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|fr|Kujūkuōji|ja|九十九王子|lt=99 ōji Shrines}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. <!--subst:PAGENAME magic word should be substituted in the future when this is moved to its final pagename. This was added to a few pages not created on Feb 8 because they were very barren and this gives context to the fact that they are in a large group of pages all to be translated more or less as a group similar to the Ise Shrines--> ==Overview== == Kitsumoto Shrine == '''Kitsumoto Shrine''' (橘本神社) is a Shinto shrine located in {{ill|Kainan, Wakayama|ja|海南市}}, {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|ja|和歌山県}}, Japan. The shrine enshrines {{nihongo|Tajimamori|田道間守}} and {{nihongo|Kumanoza-no-Ōkami|熊野坐大神}}, and is revered as the deity of confectionery. It is designated as a {{ill|Prefectural Historic Site|ja|和歌山県指定史跡}} (since April 9, 1974), as the former site of {{nihongo|Tokosaka Ōji|所坂王子}}, one of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子}} shrines.<ref name="Kinen">{{Cite web|title=Prefectural Cultural Properties: Historic Sites|url=https://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/prefg/500700/mokuroku/kenkinenbutsu.html|publisher=Wakayama Prefecture Board of Education|accessdate=2021-07-25}}</ref>
== History == According to the ''Kojiki'' and ''Nihon Shoki'', Emperor Suinin ordered Tajimamori to find the legendary fruit of eternal youth. After enduring over ten years of hardship, he returned from the mythical {{nihongo|Tokoyo no Kuni|常世の国}} (often identified with ancient China) with the fruit known as {{nihongo|Tokijiku no Kagunokonomi|非時香菓}}, identified as the {{nihongo|tachibana|橘}}, an ancient citrus fruit. Unfortunately, the emperor had already passed away. In despair, Tajimamori offered the tachibana at the emperor’s tomb and died shortly after.<ref name="Mikan">{{Cite web|title=Mikan Festival|url=http://www.d1.dion.ne.jp/~konchi/kitumoto/menu/mikanmaturi.htm|publisher=Kitsumoto Shrine|accessdate=2015-02-14}}</ref><ref name="Kainanshi">{{Cite web|title=Kitsumoto Shrine (Origin of Mikan, Deity of Confectionery)|url=https://www.city.kainan.lg.jp/kakubusho/machizukuribu/norinsuisangakari/michinoeki/1543927025145.html|publisher=Kainan City|date=2021-03-01|accessdate=2021-07-25}}</ref>
It is said that the tachibana was first planted in Japan at the "Hill of Six Trees," the former site of the shrine to the north. The tachibana is considered the ancestor of modern mandarins, and this region is known as the birthplace of mikan cultivation.<ref name="Mikan"/>
The shrine’s founding date is unknown, but a roof plaque dated 1437 (Eikyō 9) still exists.<ref name="Rekishi">{{Cite web|title=Shrine History|url=http://www.d1.dion.ne.jp/~konchi/kitumoto/menu/framepage.htm|publisher=Kitsumoto Shrine|accessdate=2015-02-14}}</ref><ref name="Nikkeii">{{Cite web|title=Why Is the "Confectionery Deity" in Wakayama? (Mystery Cruise)|url=https://www.nikkei.com/article/DGXLASHD15H02_V10C14A8AA1P00/|publisher=Nikkei|date=2014-08-20|accessdate=2021-07-25}}</ref>
In 1158 (Hōgen 3), Emperor Shirakawa reportedly stayed at the shrine during his pilgrimage to Kumano and composed the poem: ''"Spending a night under the tachibana tree, I gaze at the moon over Mt. Irisa."''<ref name="Rekishi"/>
During the Edo period, Kishu Domain lord {{nihongo|Tokugawa Mitsusada|徳川光貞}} ordered the repair of the shrine in 1687 (Jōkyō 4). However, it later fell into decline, leaving only an old tachibana tree and a small shrine by the early Meiji era. Thanks to local efforts, the shrine was restored, and in November 1907, it was relocated to its current site on the former grounds of Tokosaka Ōji. At this time, two other local Ōji shrines—{{nihongo|Tōge-shita Ōji|搭下王子}} and {{nihongo|Kitsumoto Ōji|橘本王子}}—were also enshrined here.<ref name="Rekishi"/><ref name="Nikkeii"/>
== Festivals == The region is famous for mikan production, and the annual festival on October 10 is known as the ''Mikan Festival,'' commemorating Tajimamori’s introduction of citrus to Japan.<ref name="Mikan"/><ref name="Kainanshi"/>
Additionally, the ''Confectionery Festival'' (''Zenkoku Meika Hōkensai'') is held on the first Sunday of April. This event honors Tajimamori as the deity of sweets, as mikan were historically regarded as confections. Confectioners from across Japan offer their specialty sweets in prayer for prosperity.<ref>{{Cite web|title=Confectionery Festival|url=http://www.d1.dion.ne.jp/~konchi/kitumoto/menu/kashimaturi.htm|publisher=Kitsumoto Shrine|accessdate=2015-02-14}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|title=Kitsumoto Shrine’s Confectionery Festival|url=https://www.city.kainan.lg.jp/kanko/matsuri/1543928726429.html|publisher=Kainan City|date=2021-03-18|accessdate=2021-07-25}}</ref>
== Deities Enshrined == * {{nihongo|Tajimamori|田道間守}} – Deity of sweets and mikan cultivation * {{nihongo|Kumanoza-no-Ōkami|熊野坐大神}} – Associated with the Kumano faith
== Access == * Located in {{ill|Kainan, Wakayama|ja|海南市}}, accessible via local transportation routes.
== Gallery == <gallery> File:Kitsumoto Shrine Entrance.jpg|Entrance to Kitsumoto Shrine </gallery>
== See Also == * {{ill|Kujūku Ōji (Kainan)|ja|九十九王子 (海南市)}}
== References == {{Reflist}}
== External Links == * {{Wayback|url=http://www.d1.dion.ne.jp/~konchi/kitumoto/ |title=Kitsumoto Shrine (Official Site)|date=2004-02-16}} * [https://wakayama-jinjacho.or.jp/jdb/sys/user/GetWjtTbl.php?JinjyaNo=2021 Kitsumoto Shrine – Wakayama Prefecture Shrine Office]
{{Shinto-stub}}

revision.comment: making it so there is only one of this template on every page in [[Category:Kujūku Ōji template forks]]
revision.text:
'''Kitsumoto Shrine''' is one of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|fr|Kujūkuōji|ja|九十九王子|lt=99 ōji Shrines}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. <!--subst:PAGENAME magic word should be substituted in the future when this is moved to its final pagename. This was added to a few pages not created on Feb 8 because they were very barren and this gives context to the fact that they are in a large group of pages all to be translated more or less as a group similar to the Ise Shrines--> ==Overview== == Kitsumoto Shrine == '''Kitsumoto Shrine''' (橘本神社) is a Shinto shrine located in {{ill|Kainan, Wakayama|ja|海南市}}, {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|ja|和歌山県}}, Japan. The shrine enshrines {{nihongo|Tajimamori|田道間守}} and {{nihongo|Kumanoza-no-Ōkami|熊野坐大神}}, and is revered as the deity of confectionery. It is designated as a {{ill|Prefectural Historic Site|ja|和歌山県指定史跡}} (since April 9, 1974), as the former site of {{nihongo|Tokosaka Ōji|所坂王子}}, one of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子}} shrines.<ref name="Kinen">{{Cite web|title=Prefectural Cultural Properties: Historic Sites|url=https://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/prefg/500700/mokuroku/kenkinenbutsu.html|publisher=Wakayama Prefecture Board of Education|accessdate=2021-07-25}}</ref>
== History == According to the ''Kojiki'' and ''Nihon Shoki'', Emperor Suinin ordered Tajimamori to find the legendary fruit of eternal youth. After enduring over ten years of hardship, he returned from the mythical {{nihongo|Tokoyo no Kuni|常世の国}} (often identified with ancient China) with the fruit known as {{nihongo|Tokijiku no Kagunokonomi|非時香菓}}, identified as the {{nihongo|tachibana|橘}}, an ancient citrus fruit. Unfortunately, the emperor had already passed away. In despair, Tajimamori offered the tachibana at the emperor’s tomb and died shortly after.<ref name="Mikan">{{Cite web|title=Mikan Festival|url=http://www.d1.dion.ne.jp/~konchi/kitumoto/menu/mikanmaturi.htm|publisher=Kitsumoto Shrine|accessdate=2015-02-14}}</ref><ref name="Kainanshi">{{Cite web|title=Kitsumoto Shrine (Origin of Mikan, Deity of Confectionery)|url=https://www.city.kainan.lg.jp/kakubusho/machizukuribu/norinsuisangakari/michinoeki/1543927025145.html|publisher=Kainan City|date=2021-03-01|accessdate=2021-07-25}}</ref>
It is said that the tachibana was first planted in Japan at the "Hill of Six Trees," the former site of the shrine to the north. The tachibana is considered the ancestor of modern mandarins, and this region is known as the birthplace of mikan cultivation.<ref name="Mikan"/>
The shrine’s founding date is unknown, but a roof plaque dated 1437 (Eikyō 9) still exists.<ref name="Rekishi">{{Cite web|title=Shrine History|url=http://www.d1.dion.ne.jp/~konchi/kitumoto/menu/framepage.htm|publisher=Kitsumoto Shrine|accessdate=2015-02-14}}</ref><ref name="Nikkeii">{{Cite web|title=Why Is the "Confectionery Deity" in Wakayama? (Mystery Cruise)|url=https://www.nikkei.com/article/DGXLASHD15H02_V10C14A8AA1P00/|publisher=Nikkei|date=2014-08-20|accessdate=2021-07-25}}</ref>
In 1158 (Hōgen 3), Emperor Shirakawa reportedly stayed at the shrine during his pilgrimage to Kumano and composed the poem: ''"Spending a night under the tachibana tree, I gaze at the moon over Mt. Irisa."''<ref name="Rekishi"/>
During the Edo period, Kishu Domain lord {{nihongo|Tokugawa Mitsusada|徳川光貞}} ordered the repair of the shrine in 1687 (Jōkyō 4). However, it later fell into decline, leaving only an old tachibana tree and a small shrine by the early Meiji era. Thanks to local efforts, the shrine was restored, and in November 1907, it was relocated to its current site on the former grounds of Tokosaka Ōji. At this time, two other local Ōji shrines—{{nihongo|Tōge-shita Ōji|搭下王子}} and {{nihongo|Kitsumoto Ōji|橘本王子}}—were also enshrined here.<ref name="Rekishi"/><ref name="Nikkeii"/>
== Festivals == The region is famous for mikan production, and the annual festival on October 10 is known as the ''Mikan Festival,'' commemorating Tajimamori’s introduction of citrus to Japan.<ref name="Mikan"/><ref name="Kainanshi"/>
Additionally, the ''Confectionery Festival'' (''Zenkoku Meika Hōkensai'') is held on the first Sunday of April. This event honors Tajimamori as the deity of sweets, as mikan were historically regarded as confections. Confectioners from across Japan offer their specialty sweets in prayer for prosperity.<ref>{{Cite web|title=Confectionery Festival|url=http://www.d1.dion.ne.jp/~konchi/kitumoto/menu/kashimaturi.htm|publisher=Kitsumoto Shrine|accessdate=2015-02-14}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|title=Kitsumoto Shrine’s Confectionery Festival|url=https://www.city.kainan.lg.jp/kanko/matsuri/1543928726429.html|publisher=Kainan City|date=2021-03-18|accessdate=2021-07-25}}</ref>
== Deities Enshrined == * {{nihongo|Tajimamori|田道間守}} – Deity of sweets and mikan cultivation * {{nihongo|Kumanoza-no-Ōkami|熊野坐大神}} – Associated with the Kumano faith
== Access == * Located in {{ill|Kainan, Wakayama|ja|海南市}}, accessible via local transportation routes.
== Gallery == <gallery> File:Kitsumoto Shrine Entrance.jpg|Entrance to Kitsumoto Shrine </gallery>
== See Also == * {{ill|Kujūku Ōji (Kainan)|ja|九十九王子 (海南市)}}
== References == {{Reflist}}
== External Links == * {{Wayback|url=http://www.d1.dion.ne.jp/~konchi/kitumoto/ |title=Kitsumoto Shrine (Official Site)|date=2004-02-16}} * [https://wakayama-jinjacho.or.jp/jdb/sys/user/GetWjtTbl.php?JinjyaNo=2021 Kitsumoto Shrine – Wakayama Prefecture Shrine Office]
{{Shinto-stub}}

revision.comment: Removing from [[Category:subst:NAMESPACE]] on using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
'''Kitsumoto Shrine''' is one of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|fr|Kujūkuōji|ja|九十九王子|lt=99 ōji Shrines}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. <!--subst:PAGENAME magic word should be substituted in the future when this is moved to its final pagename. This was added to a few pages not created on Feb 8 because they were very barren and this gives context to the fact that they are in a large group of pages all to be translated more or less as a group similar to the Ise Shrines--> ==Overview== == Kitsumoto Shrine == '''Kitsumoto Shrine''' (橘本神社) is a Shinto shrine located in {{ill|Kainan, Wakayama|ja|海南市}}, {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|ja|和歌山県}}, Japan. The shrine enshrines {{nihongo|Tajimamori|田道間守}} and {{nihongo|Kumanoza-no-Ōkami|熊野坐大神}}, and is revered as the deity of confectionery. It is designated as a {{ill|Prefectural Historic Site|ja|和歌山県指定史跡}} (since April 9, 1974), as the former site of {{nihongo|Tokosaka Ōji|所坂王子}}, one of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子}} shrines.<ref name="Kinen">{{Cite web|title=Prefectural Cultural Properties: Historic Sites|url=https://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/prefg/500700/mokuroku/kenkinenbutsu.html|publisher=Wakayama Prefecture Board of Education|accessdate=2021-07-25}}</ref>
== History == According to the ''Kojiki'' and ''Nihon Shoki'', Emperor Suinin ordered Tajimamori to find the legendary fruit of eternal youth. After enduring over ten years of hardship, he returned from the mythical {{nihongo|Tokoyo no Kuni|常世の国}} (often identified with ancient China) with the fruit known as {{nihongo|Tokijiku no Kagunokonomi|非時香菓}}, identified as the {{nihongo|tachibana|橘}}, an ancient citrus fruit. Unfortunately, the emperor had already passed away. In despair, Tajimamori offered the tachibana at the emperor’s tomb and died shortly after.<ref name="Mikan">{{Cite web|title=Mikan Festival|url=http://www.d1.dion.ne.jp/~konchi/kitumoto/menu/mikanmaturi.htm|publisher=Kitsumoto Shrine|accessdate=2015-02-14}}</ref><ref name="Kainanshi">{{Cite web|title=Kitsumoto Shrine (Origin of Mikan, Deity of Confectionery)|url=https://www.city.kainan.lg.jp/kakubusho/machizukuribu/norinsuisangakari/michinoeki/1543927025145.html|publisher=Kainan City|date=2021-03-01|accessdate=2021-07-25}}</ref>
It is said that the tachibana was first planted in Japan at the "Hill of Six Trees," the former site of the shrine to the north. The tachibana is considered the ancestor of modern mandarins, and this region is known as the birthplace of mikan cultivation.<ref name="Mikan"/>
The shrine’s founding date is unknown, but a roof plaque dated 1437 (Eikyō 9) still exists.<ref name="Rekishi">{{Cite web|title=Shrine History|url=http://www.d1.dion.ne.jp/~konchi/kitumoto/menu/framepage.htm|publisher=Kitsumoto Shrine|accessdate=2015-02-14}}</ref><ref name="Nikkeii">{{Cite web|title=Why Is the "Confectionery Deity" in Wakayama? (Mystery Cruise)|url=https://www.nikkei.com/article/DGXLASHD15H02_V10C14A8AA1P00/|publisher=Nikkei|date=2014-08-20|accessdate=2021-07-25}}</ref>
In 1158 (Hōgen 3), Emperor Shirakawa reportedly stayed at the shrine during his pilgrimage to Kumano and composed the poem: ''"Spending a night under the tachibana tree, I gaze at the moon over Mt. Irisa."''<ref name="Rekishi"/>
During the Edo period, Kishu Domain lord {{nihongo|Tokugawa Mitsusada|徳川光貞}} ordered the repair of the shrine in 1687 (Jōkyō 4). However, it later fell into decline, leaving only an old tachibana tree and a small shrine by the early Meiji era. Thanks to local efforts, the shrine was restored, and in November 1907, it was relocated to its current site on the former grounds of Tokosaka Ōji. At this time, two other local Ōji shrines—{{nihongo|Tōge-shita Ōji|搭下王子}} and {{nihongo|Kitsumoto Ōji|橘本王子}}—were also enshrined here.<ref name="Rekishi"/><ref name="Nikkeii"/>
== Festivals == The region is famous for mikan production, and the annual festival on October 10 is known as the ''Mikan Festival,'' commemorating Tajimamori’s introduction of citrus to Japan.<ref name="Mikan"/><ref name="Kainanshi"/>
Additionally, the ''Confectionery Festival'' (''Zenkoku Meika Hōkensai'') is held on the first Sunday of April. This event honors Tajimamori as the deity of sweets, as mikan were historically regarded as confections. Confectioners from across Japan offer their specialty sweets in prayer for prosperity.<ref>{{Cite web|title=Confectionery Festival|url=http://www.d1.dion.ne.jp/~konchi/kitumoto/menu/kashimaturi.htm|publisher=Kitsumoto Shrine|accessdate=2015-02-14}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|title=Kitsumoto Shrine’s Confectionery Festival|url=https://www.city.kainan.lg.jp/kanko/matsuri/1543928726429.html|publisher=Kainan City|date=2021-03-18|accessdate=2021-07-25}}</ref>
== Deities Enshrined == * {{nihongo|Tajimamori|田道間守}} – Deity of sweets and mikan cultivation * {{nihongo|Kumanoza-no-Ōkami|熊野坐大神}} – Associated with the Kumano faith
== Access == * Located in {{ill|Kainan, Wakayama|ja|海南市}}, accessible via local transportation routes.
== Gallery == <gallery> File:Kitsumoto Shrine Entrance.jpg|Entrance to Kitsumoto Shrine </gallery>
== See Also == * {{ill|Kujūku Ōji (Kainan)|ja|九十九王子 (海南市)}}
== References == {{Reflist}}
== External Links == * {{Wayback|url=http://www.d1.dion.ne.jp/~konchi/kitumoto/ |title=Kitsumoto Shrine (Official Site)|date=2004-02-16}} * [https://wakayama-jinjacho.or.jp/jdb/sys/user/GetWjtTbl.php?JinjyaNo=2021 Kitsumoto Shrine – Wakayama Prefecture Shrine Office]

revision.comment: putting pagename at the end
revision.text:
'''Kitsumoto Shrine''' is one of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|fr|Kujūkuōji|ja|九十九王子|lt=99 ōji Shrines}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. <!--subst:PAGENAME magic word should be substituted in the future when this is moved to its final pagename. This was added to a few pages not created on Feb 8 because they were very barren and this gives context to the fact that they are in a large group of pages all to be translated more or less as a group similar to the Ise Shrines--> ==Overview== == Kitsumoto Shrine == '''Kitsumoto Shrine''' (橘本神社) is a Shinto shrine located in {{ill|Kainan, Wakayama|ja|海南市}}, {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|ja|和歌山県}}, Japan. The shrine enshrines {{nihongo|Tajimamori|田道間守}} and {{nihongo|Kumanoza-no-Ōkami|熊野坐大神}}, and is revered as the deity of confectionery. It is designated as a {{ill|Prefectural Historic Site|ja|和歌山県指定史跡}} (since April 9, 1974), as the former site of {{nihongo|Tokosaka Ōji|所坂王子}}, one of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子}} shrines.<ref name="Kinen">{{Cite web|title=Prefectural Cultural Properties: Historic Sites|url=https://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/prefg/500700/mokuroku/kenkinenbutsu.html|publisher=Wakayama Prefecture Board of Education|accessdate=2021-07-25}}</ref>
== History == According to the ''Kojiki'' and ''Nihon Shoki'', Emperor Suinin ordered Tajimamori to find the legendary fruit of eternal youth. After enduring over ten years of hardship, he returned from the mythical {{nihongo|Tokoyo no Kuni|常世の国}} (often identified with ancient China) with the fruit known as {{nihongo|Tokijiku no Kagunokonomi|非時香菓}}, identified as the {{nihongo|tachibana|橘}}, an ancient citrus fruit. Unfortunately, the emperor had already passed away. In despair, Tajimamori offered the tachibana at the emperor’s tomb and died shortly after.<ref name="Mikan">{{Cite web|title=Mikan Festival|url=http://www.d1.dion.ne.jp/~konchi/kitumoto/menu/mikanmaturi.htm|publisher=Kitsumoto Shrine|accessdate=2015-02-14}}</ref><ref name="Kainanshi">{{Cite web|title=Kitsumoto Shrine (Origin of Mikan, Deity of Confectionery)|url=https://www.city.kainan.lg.jp/kakubusho/machizukuribu/norinsuisangakari/michinoeki/1543927025145.html|publisher=Kainan City|date=2021-03-01|accessdate=2021-07-25}}</ref>
It is said that the tachibana was first planted in Japan at the "Hill of Six Trees," the former site of the shrine to the north. The tachibana is considered the ancestor of modern mandarins, and this region is known as the birthplace of mikan cultivation.<ref name="Mikan"/>
The shrine’s founding date is unknown, but a roof plaque dated 1437 (Eikyō 9) still exists.<ref name="Rekishi">{{Cite web|title=Shrine History|url=http://www.d1.dion.ne.jp/~konchi/kitumoto/menu/framepage.htm|publisher=Kitsumoto Shrine|accessdate=2015-02-14}}</ref><ref name="Nikkeii">{{Cite web|title=Why Is the "Confectionery Deity" in Wakayama? (Mystery Cruise)|url=https://www.nikkei.com/article/DGXLASHD15H02_V10C14A8AA1P00/|publisher=Nikkei|date=2014-08-20|accessdate=2021-07-25}}</ref>
In 1158 (Hōgen 3), Emperor Shirakawa reportedly stayed at the shrine during his pilgrimage to Kumano and composed the poem: ''"Spending a night under the tachibana tree, I gaze at the moon over Mt. Irisa."''<ref name="Rekishi"/>
During the Edo period, Kishu Domain lord {{nihongo|Tokugawa Mitsusada|徳川光貞}} ordered the repair of the shrine in 1687 (Jōkyō 4). However, it later fell into decline, leaving only an old tachibana tree and a small shrine by the early Meiji era. Thanks to local efforts, the shrine was restored, and in November 1907, it was relocated to its current site on the former grounds of Tokosaka Ōji. At this time, two other local Ōji shrines—{{nihongo|Tōge-shita Ōji|搭下王子}} and {{nihongo|Kitsumoto Ōji|橘本王子}}—were also enshrined here.<ref name="Rekishi"/><ref name="Nikkeii"/>
== Festivals == The region is famous for mikan production, and the annual festival on October 10 is known as the ''Mikan Festival,'' commemorating Tajimamori’s introduction of citrus to Japan.<ref name="Mikan"/><ref name="Kainanshi"/>
Additionally, the ''Confectionery Festival'' (''Zenkoku Meika Hōkensai'') is held on the first Sunday of April. This event honors Tajimamori as the deity of sweets, as mikan were historically regarded as confections. Confectioners from across Japan offer their specialty sweets in prayer for prosperity.<ref>{{Cite web|title=Confectionery Festival|url=http://www.d1.dion.ne.jp/~konchi/kitumoto/menu/kashimaturi.htm|publisher=Kitsumoto Shrine|accessdate=2015-02-14}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|title=Kitsumoto Shrine’s Confectionery Festival|url=https://www.city.kainan.lg.jp/kanko/matsuri/1543928726429.html|publisher=Kainan City|date=2021-03-18|accessdate=2021-07-25}}</ref>
== Deities Enshrined == * {{nihongo|Tajimamori|田道間守}} – Deity of sweets and mikan cultivation * {{nihongo|Kumanoza-no-Ōkami|熊野坐大神}} – Associated with the Kumano faith
== Access == * Located in {{ill|Kainan, Wakayama|ja|海南市}}, accessible via local transportation routes.
== Gallery == <gallery> File:Kitsumoto Shrine Entrance.jpg|Entrance to Kitsumoto Shrine </gallery>
== See Also == * {{ill|Kujūku Ōji (Kainan)|ja|九十九王子 (海南市)}}
== References == {{Reflist}}
== External Links == * {{Wayback|url=http://www.d1.dion.ne.jp/~konchi/kitumoto/ |title=Kitsumoto Shrine (Official Site)|date=2004-02-16}} * [https://wakayama-jinjacho.or.jp/jdb/sys/user/GetWjtTbl.php?JinjyaNo=2021 Kitsumoto Shrine – Wakayama Prefecture Shrine Office]

revision.comment: Adding [[Category:11]][[ja:{{subst:PAGENAME}}]][[Category:11]] on using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
'''Kitsumoto Shrine''' is one of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|fr|Kujūkuōji|ja|九十九王子|lt=99 ōji Shrines}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. <!--subst:PAGENAME magic word should be substituted in the future when this is moved to its final pagename. This was added to a few pages not created on Feb 8 because they were very barren and this gives context to the fact that they are in a large group of pages all to be translated more or less as a group similar to the Ise Shrines--> ==Overview== == Kitsumoto Shrine == '''Kitsumoto Shrine''' (橘本神社) is a Shinto shrine located in {{ill|Kainan, Wakayama|ja|海南市}}, {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|ja|和歌山県}}, Japan. The shrine enshrines {{nihongo|Tajimamori|田道間守}} and {{nihongo|Kumanoza-no-Ōkami|熊野坐大神}}, and is revered as the deity of confectionery. It is designated as a {{ill|Prefectural Historic Site|ja|和歌山県指定史跡}} (since April 9, 1974), as the former site of {{nihongo|Tokosaka Ōji|所坂王子}}, one of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子}} shrines.<ref name="Kinen">{{Cite web|title=Prefectural Cultural Properties: Historic Sites|url=https://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/prefg/500700/mokuroku/kenkinenbutsu.html|publisher=Wakayama Prefecture Board of Education|accessdate=2021-07-25}}</ref>
== History == According to the ''Kojiki'' and ''Nihon Shoki'', Emperor Suinin ordered Tajimamori to find the legendary fruit of eternal youth. After enduring over ten years of hardship, he returned from the mythical {{nihongo|Tokoyo no Kuni|常世の国}} (often identified with ancient China) with the fruit known as {{nihongo|Tokijiku no Kagunokonomi|非時香菓}}, identified as the {{nihongo|tachibana|橘}}, an ancient citrus fruit. Unfortunately, the emperor had already passed away. In despair, Tajimamori offered the tachibana at the emperor’s tomb and died shortly after.<ref name="Mikan">{{Cite web|title=Mikan Festival|url=http://www.d1.dion.ne.jp/~konchi/kitumoto/menu/mikanmaturi.htm|publisher=Kitsumoto Shrine|accessdate=2015-02-14}}</ref><ref name="Kainanshi">{{Cite web|title=Kitsumoto Shrine (Origin of Mikan, Deity of Confectionery)|url=https://www.city.kainan.lg.jp/kakubusho/machizukuribu/norinsuisangakari/michinoeki/1543927025145.html|publisher=Kainan City|date=2021-03-01|accessdate=2021-07-25}}</ref>
It is said that the tachibana was first planted in Japan at the "Hill of Six Trees," the former site of the shrine to the north. The tachibana is considered the ancestor of modern mandarins, and this region is known as the birthplace of mikan cultivation.<ref name="Mikan"/>
The shrine’s founding date is unknown, but a roof plaque dated 1437 (Eikyō 9) still exists.<ref name="Rekishi">{{Cite web|title=Shrine History|url=http://www.d1.dion.ne.jp/~konchi/kitumoto/menu/framepage.htm|publisher=Kitsumoto Shrine|accessdate=2015-02-14}}</ref><ref name="Nikkeii">{{Cite web|title=Why Is the "Confectionery Deity" in Wakayama? (Mystery Cruise)|url=https://www.nikkei.com/article/DGXLASHD15H02_V10C14A8AA1P00/|publisher=Nikkei|date=2014-08-20|accessdate=2021-07-25}}</ref>
In 1158 (Hōgen 3), Emperor Shirakawa reportedly stayed at the shrine during his pilgrimage to Kumano and composed the poem: ''"Spending a night under the tachibana tree, I gaze at the moon over Mt. Irisa."''<ref name="Rekishi"/>
During the Edo period, Kishu Domain lord {{nihongo|Tokugawa Mitsusada|徳川光貞}} ordered the repair of the shrine in 1687 (Jōkyō 4). However, it later fell into decline, leaving only an old tachibana tree and a small shrine by the early Meiji era. Thanks to local efforts, the shrine was restored, and in November 1907, it was relocated to its current site on the former grounds of Tokosaka Ōji. At this time, two other local Ōji shrines—{{nihongo|Tōge-shita Ōji|搭下王子}} and {{nihongo|Kitsumoto Ōji|橘本王子}}—were also enshrined here.<ref name="Rekishi"/><ref name="Nikkeii"/>
== Festivals == The region is famous for mikan production, and the annual festival on October 10 is known as the ''Mikan Festival,'' commemorating Tajimamori’s introduction of citrus to Japan.<ref name="Mikan"/><ref name="Kainanshi"/>
Additionally, the ''Confectionery Festival'' (''Zenkoku Meika Hōkensai'') is held on the first Sunday of April. This event honors Tajimamori as the deity of sweets, as mikan were historically regarded as confections. Confectioners from across Japan offer their specialty sweets in prayer for prosperity.<ref>{{Cite web|title=Confectionery Festival|url=http://www.d1.dion.ne.jp/~konchi/kitumoto/menu/kashimaturi.htm|publisher=Kitsumoto Shrine|accessdate=2015-02-14}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|title=Kitsumoto Shrine’s Confectionery Festival|url=https://www.city.kainan.lg.jp/kanko/matsuri/1543928726429.html|publisher=Kainan City|date=2021-03-18|accessdate=2021-07-25}}</ref>
== Deities Enshrined == * {{nihongo|Tajimamori|田道間守}} – Deity of sweets and mikan cultivation * {{nihongo|Kumanoza-no-Ōkami|熊野坐大神}} – Associated with the Kumano faith
== Access == * Located in {{ill|Kainan, Wakayama|ja|海南市}}, accessible via local transportation routes.
== Gallery == <gallery> File:Kitsumoto Shrine Entrance.jpg|Entrance to Kitsumoto Shrine </gallery>
== See Also == * {{ill|Kujūku Ōji (Kainan)|ja|九十九王子 (海南市)}}
== References == {{Reflist}}
== External Links == * {{Wayback|url=http://www.d1.dion.ne.jp/~konchi/kitumoto/ |title=Kitsumoto Shrine (Official Site)|date=2004-02-16}} * [https://wakayama-jinjacho.or.jp/jdb/sys/user/GetWjtTbl.php?JinjyaNo=2021 Kitsumoto Shrine – Wakayama Prefecture Shrine Office]

revision.text:
'''Kitsumoto Shrine''' is one of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|fr|Kujūkuōji|ja|九十九王子|lt=99 ōji Shrines}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. <!--subst:PAGENAME magic word should be substituted in the future when this is moved to its final pagename. This was added to a few pages not created on Feb 8 because they were very barren and this gives context to the fact that they are in a large group of pages all to be translated more or less as a group similar to the Ise Shrines--> ==Overview== == Kitsumoto Shrine == '''Kitsumoto Shrine''' (橘本神社) is a Shinto shrine located in {{ill|Kainan, Wakayama|ja|海南市}}, {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|ja|和歌山県}}, Japan. The shrine enshrines {{nihongo|Tajimamori|田道間守}} and {{nihongo|Kumanoza-no-Ōkami|熊野坐大神}}, and is revered as the deity of confectionery. It is designated as a {{ill|Prefectural Historic Site|ja|和歌山県指定史跡}} (since April 9, 1974), as the former site of {{nihongo|Tokosaka Ōji|所坂王子}}, one of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji}} shrines.<ref name="Kinen">{{Cite web|title=Prefectural Cultural Properties: Historic Sites|url=https://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/prefg/500700/mokuroku/kenkinenbutsu.html|publisher=Wakayama Prefecture Board of Education|accessdate=2021-07-25}}</ref>
== History == According to the ''Kojiki'' and ''Nihon Shoki'', Emperor Suinin ordered Tajimamori to find the legendary fruit of eternal youth. After enduring over ten years of hardship, he returned from the mythical {{nihongo|Tokoyo no Kuni|常世の国}} (often identified with ancient China) with the fruit known as {{nihongo|Tokijiku no Kagunokonomi|非時香菓}}, identified as the {{nihongo|tachibana|橘}}, an ancient citrus fruit. Unfortunately, the emperor had already passed away. In despair, Tajimamori offered the tachibana at the emperor’s tomb and died shortly after.<ref name="Mikan">{{Cite web|title=Mikan Festival|url=http://www.d1.dion.ne.jp/~konchi/kitumoto/menu/mikanmaturi.htm|publisher=Kitsumoto Shrine|accessdate=2015-02-14}}</ref><ref name="Kainanshi">{{Cite web|title=Kitsumoto Shrine (Origin of Mikan, Deity of Confectionery)|url=https://www.city.kainan.lg.jp/kakubusho/machizukuribu/norinsuisangakari/michinoeki/1543927025145.html|publisher=Kainan City|date=2021-03-01|accessdate=2021-07-25}}</ref>
It is said that the tachibana was first planted in Japan at the "Hill of Six Trees," the former site of the shrine to the north. The tachibana is considered the ancestor of modern mandarins, and this region is known as the birthplace of mikan cultivation.<ref name="Mikan"/>
The shrine’s founding date is unknown, but a roof plaque dated 1437 (Eikyō 9) still exists.<ref name="Rekishi">{{Cite web|title=Shrine History|url=http://www.d1.dion.ne.jp/~konchi/kitumoto/menu/framepage.htm|publisher=Kitsumoto Shrine|accessdate=2015-02-14}}</ref><ref name="Nikkeii">{{Cite web|title=Why Is the "Confectionery Deity" in Wakayama? (Mystery Cruise)|url=https://www.nikkei.com/article/DGXLASHD15H02_V10C14A8AA1P00/|publisher=Nikkei|date=2014-08-20|accessdate=2021-07-25}}</ref>
In 1158 (Hōgen 3), Emperor Shirakawa reportedly stayed at the shrine during his pilgrimage to Kumano and composed the poem: ''"Spending a night under the tachibana tree, I gaze at the moon over Mt. Irisa."''<ref name="Rekishi"/>
During the Edo period, Kishu Domain lord {{nihongo|Tokugawa Mitsusada|徳川光貞}} ordered the repair of the shrine in 1687 (Jōkyō 4). However, it later fell into decline, leaving only an old tachibana tree and a small shrine by the early Meiji era. Thanks to local efforts, the shrine was restored, and in November 1907, it was relocated to its current site on the former grounds of Tokosaka Ōji. At this time, two other local Ōji shrines—{{nihongo|Tōge-shita Ōji|搭下王子}} and {{nihongo|Kitsumoto Ōji|橘本王子}}—were also enshrined here.<ref name="Rekishi"/><ref name="Nikkeii"/>
== Festivals == The region is famous for mikan production, and the annual festival on October 10 is known as the ''Mikan Festival,'' commemorating Tajimamori’s introduction of citrus to Japan.<ref name="Mikan"/><ref name="Kainanshi"/>
Additionally, the ''Confectionery Festival'' (''Zenkoku Meika Hōkensai'') is held on the first Sunday of April. This event honors Tajimamori as the deity of sweets, as mikan were historically regarded as confections. Confectioners from across Japan offer their specialty sweets in prayer for prosperity.<ref>{{Cite web|title=Confectionery Festival|url=http://www.d1.dion.ne.jp/~konchi/kitumoto/menu/kashimaturi.htm|publisher=Kitsumoto Shrine|accessdate=2015-02-14}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|title=Kitsumoto Shrine’s Confectionery Festival|url=https://www.city.kainan.lg.jp/kanko/matsuri/1543928726429.html|publisher=Kainan City|date=2021-03-18|accessdate=2021-07-25}}</ref>
== Deities Enshrined == * {{nihongo|Tajimamori|田道間守}} – Deity of sweets and mikan cultivation * {{nihongo|Kumanoza-no-Ōkami|熊野坐大神}} – Associated with the Kumano faith
== Access == * Located in {{ill|Kainan, Wakayama|ja|海南市}}, accessible via local transportation routes.
== Gallery == <gallery> File:Kitsumoto Shrine Entrance.jpg|Entrance to Kitsumoto Shrine </gallery>
== See Also == * {{ill|Kujūku Ōji (Kainan)|ja|九十九王子 (海南市)}}
== References == {{Reflist}}

revision.text:
'''Kitsumoto Shrine''' is one of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|fr|Kujūkuōji|ja|九十九王子|lt=99 ōji Shrines}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. <!--subst:PAGENAME magic word should be substituted in the future when this is moved to its final pagename. This was added to a few pages not created on Feb 8 because they were very barren and this gives context to the fact that they are in a large group of pages all to be translated more or less as a group similar to the Ise Shrines--> == Kitsumoto Shrine == '''Kitsumoto Shrine''' (橘本神社) is a Shinto shrine located in {{ill|Kainan, Wakayama|ja|海南市}}, {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|ja|和歌山県}}, Japan. The shrine enshrines {{nihongo|Tajimamori|田道間守}} and {{nihongo|Kumanoza-no-Ōkami|熊野坐大神}}, and is revered as the deity of confectionery. It is designated as a {{ill|Prefectural Historic Site|ja|和歌山県指定史跡}} (since April 9, 1974), as the former site of {{nihongo|Tokosaka Ōji|所坂王子}}, one of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji}} shrines.<ref name="Kinen">{{Cite web|title=Prefectural Cultural Properties: Historic Sites|url=https://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/prefg/500700/mokuroku/kenkinenbutsu.html|publisher=Wakayama Prefecture Board of Education|accessdate=2021-07-25}}</ref>
== History == According to the ''Kojiki'' and ''Nihon Shoki'', Emperor Suinin ordered Tajimamori to find the legendary fruit of eternal youth. After enduring over ten years of hardship, he returned from the mythical {{nihongo|Tokoyo no Kuni|常世の国}} (often identified with ancient China) with the fruit known as {{nihongo|Tokijiku no Kagunokonomi|非時香菓}}, identified as the {{nihongo|tachibana|橘}}, an ancient citrus fruit. Unfortunately, the emperor had already passed away. In despair, Tajimamori offered the tachibana at the emperor’s tomb and died shortly after.<ref name="Mikan">{{Cite web|title=Mikan Festival|url=http://www.d1.dion.ne.jp/~konchi/kitumoto/menu/mikanmaturi.htm|publisher=Kitsumoto Shrine|accessdate=2015-02-14}}</ref><ref name="Kainanshi">{{Cite web|title=Kitsumoto Shrine (Origin of Mikan, Deity of Confectionery)|url=https://www.city.kainan.lg.jp/kakubusho/machizukuribu/norinsuisangakari/michinoeki/1543927025145.html|publisher=Kainan City|date=2021-03-01|accessdate=2021-07-25}}</ref>
It is said that the tachibana was first planted in Japan at the "Hill of Six Trees," the former site of the shrine to the north. The tachibana is considered the ancestor of modern mandarins, and this region is known as the birthplace of mikan cultivation.<ref name="Mikan"/>
The shrine’s founding date is unknown, but a roof plaque dated 1437 (Eikyō 9) still exists.<ref name="Rekishi">{{Cite web|title=Shrine History|url=http://www.d1.dion.ne.jp/~konchi/kitumoto/menu/framepage.htm|publisher=Kitsumoto Shrine|accessdate=2015-02-14}}</ref><ref name="Nikkeii">{{Cite web|title=Why Is the "Confectionery Deity" in Wakayama? (Mystery Cruise)|url=https://www.nikkei.com/article/DGXLASHD15H02_V10C14A8AA1P00/|publisher=Nikkei|date=2014-08-20|accessdate=2021-07-25}}</ref>
In 1158 (Hōgen 3), Emperor Shirakawa reportedly stayed at the shrine during his pilgrimage to Kumano and composed the poem: ''"Spending a night under the tachibana tree, I gaze at the moon over Mt. Irisa."''<ref name="Rekishi"/>
During the Edo period, Kishu Domain lord {{nihongo|Tokugawa Mitsusada|徳川光貞}} ordered the repair of the shrine in 1687 (Jōkyō 4). However, it later fell into decline, leaving only an old tachibana tree and a small shrine by the early Meiji era. Thanks to local efforts, the shrine was restored, and in November 1907, it was relocated to its current site on the former grounds of Tokosaka Ōji. At this time, two other local Ōji shrines—{{nihongo|Tōge-shita Ōji|搭下王子}} and {{nihongo|Kitsumoto Ōji|橘本王子}}—were also enshrined here.<ref name="Rekishi"/><ref name="Nikkeii"/>
== Festivals == The region is famous for mikan production, and the annual festival on October 10 is known as the ''Mikan Festival,'' commemorating Tajimamori’s introduction of citrus to Japan.<ref name="Mikan"/><ref name="Kainanshi"/>
Additionally, the ''Confectionery Festival'' (''Zenkoku Meika Hōkensai'') is held on the first Sunday of April. This event honors Tajimamori as the deity of sweets, as mikan were historically regarded as confections. Confectioners from across Japan offer their specialty sweets in prayer for prosperity.<ref>{{Cite web|title=Confectionery Festival|url=http://www.d1.dion.ne.jp/~konchi/kitumoto/menu/kashimaturi.htm|publisher=Kitsumoto Shrine|accessdate=2015-02-14}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|title=Kitsumoto Shrine’s Confectionery Festival|url=https://www.city.kainan.lg.jp/kanko/matsuri/1543928726429.html|publisher=Kainan City|date=2021-03-18|accessdate=2021-07-25}}</ref>
== Deities Enshrined == * {{nihongo|Tajimamori|田道間守}} – Deity of sweets and mikan cultivation * {{nihongo|Kumanoza-no-Ōkami|熊野坐大神}} – Associated with the Kumano faith
== Access == * Located in {{ill|Kainan, Wakayama|ja|海南市}}, accessible via local transportation routes.
== Gallery == <gallery> File:Kitsumoto Shrine Entrance.jpg|Entrance to Kitsumoto Shrine </gallery>
== See Also == * {{ill|Kujūku Ōji (Kainan)|ja|九十九王子 (海南市)}}

revision.comment: Removing from [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]] on using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
'''Kitsumoto Shrine''' is one of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|fr|Kujūkuōji|ja|九十九王子|lt=99 ōji Shrines}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. <!--subst:PAGENAME magic word should be substituted in the future when this is moved to its final pagename. This was added to a few pages not created on Feb 8 because they were very barren and this gives context to the fact that they are in a large group of pages all to be translated more or less as a group similar to the Ise Shrines--> == Kitsumoto Shrine == '''Kitsumoto Shrine''' (橘本神社) is a Shinto shrine located in {{ill|Kainan, Wakayama|ja|海南市}}, {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|ja|和歌山県}}, Japan. The shrine enshrines {{nihongo|Tajimamori|田道間守}} and {{nihongo|Kumanoza-no-Ōkami|熊野坐大神}}, and is revered as the deity of confectionery. It is designated as a {{ill|Prefectural Historic Site|ja|和歌山県指定史跡}} (since April 9, 1974), as the former site of {{nihongo|Tokosaka Ōji|所坂王子}}, one of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji}} shrines.<ref name="Kinen">{{Cite web|title=Prefectural Cultural Properties: Historic Sites|url=https://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/prefg/500700/mokuroku/kenkinenbutsu.html|publisher=Wakayama Prefecture Board of Education|accessdate=2021-07-25}}</ref>
== History == According to the ''Kojiki'' and ''Nihon Shoki'', Emperor Suinin ordered Tajimamori to find the legendary fruit of eternal youth. After enduring over ten years of hardship, he returned from the mythical {{nihongo|Tokoyo no Kuni|常世の国}} (often identified with ancient China) with the fruit known as {{nihongo|Tokijiku no Kagunokonomi|非時香菓}}, identified as the {{nihongo|tachibana|橘}}, an ancient citrus fruit. Unfortunately, the emperor had already passed away. In despair, Tajimamori offered the tachibana at the emperor’s tomb and died shortly after.<ref name="Mikan">{{Cite web|title=Mikan Festival|url=http://www.d1.dion.ne.jp/~konchi/kitumoto/menu/mikanmaturi.htm|publisher=Kitsumoto Shrine|accessdate=2015-02-14}}</ref><ref name="Kainanshi">{{Cite web|title=Kitsumoto Shrine (Origin of Mikan, Deity of Confectionery)|url=https://www.city.kainan.lg.jp/kakubusho/machizukuribu/norinsuisangakari/michinoeki/1543927025145.html|publisher=Kainan City|date=2021-03-01|accessdate=2021-07-25}}</ref>
It is said that the tachibana was first planted in Japan at the "Hill of Six Trees," the former site of the shrine to the north. The tachibana is considered the ancestor of modern mandarins, and this region is known as the birthplace of mikan cultivation.<ref name="Mikan"/>
The shrine’s founding date is unknown, but a roof plaque dated 1437 (Eikyō 9) still exists.<ref name="Rekishi">{{Cite web|title=Shrine History|url=http://www.d1.dion.ne.jp/~konchi/kitumoto/menu/framepage.htm|publisher=Kitsumoto Shrine|accessdate=2015-02-14}}</ref><ref name="Nikkeii">{{Cite web|title=Why Is the "Confectionery Deity" in Wakayama? (Mystery Cruise)|url=https://www.nikkei.com/article/DGXLASHD15H02_V10C14A8AA1P00/|publisher=Nikkei|date=2014-08-20|accessdate=2021-07-25}}</ref>
In 1158 (Hōgen 3), Emperor Shirakawa reportedly stayed at the shrine during his pilgrimage to Kumano and composed the poem: ''"Spending a night under the tachibana tree, I gaze at the moon over Mt. Irisa."''<ref name="Rekishi"/>
During the Edo period, Kishu Domain lord {{nihongo|Tokugawa Mitsusada|徳川光貞}} ordered the repair of the shrine in 1687 (Jōkyō 4). However, it later fell into decline, leaving only an old tachibana tree and a small shrine by the early Meiji era. Thanks to local efforts, the shrine was restored, and in November 1907, it was relocated to its current site on the former grounds of Tokosaka Ōji. At this time, two other local Ōji shrines—{{nihongo|Tōge-shita Ōji|搭下王子}} and {{nihongo|Kitsumoto Ōji|橘本王子}}—were also enshrined here.<ref name="Rekishi"/><ref name="Nikkeii"/>
== Festivals == The region is famous for mikan production, and the annual festival on October 10 is known as the ''Mikan Festival,'' commemorating Tajimamori’s introduction of citrus to Japan.<ref name="Mikan"/><ref name="Kainanshi"/>
Additionally, the ''Confectionery Festival'' (''Zenkoku Meika Hōkensai'') is held on the first Sunday of April. This event honors Tajimamori as the deity of sweets, as mikan were historically regarded as confections. Confectioners from across Japan offer their specialty sweets in prayer for prosperity.<ref>{{Cite web|title=Confectionery Festival|url=http://www.d1.dion.ne.jp/~konchi/kitumoto/menu/kashimaturi.htm|publisher=Kitsumoto Shrine|accessdate=2015-02-14}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|title=Kitsumoto Shrine’s Confectionery Festival|url=https://www.city.kainan.lg.jp/kanko/matsuri/1543928726429.html|publisher=Kainan City|date=2021-03-18|accessdate=2021-07-25}}</ref>
== Deities Enshrined == * {{nihongo|Tajimamori|田道間守}} – Deity of sweets and mikan cultivation * {{nihongo|Kumanoza-no-Ōkami|熊野坐大神}} – Associated with the Kumano faith
== Access == * Located in {{ill|Kainan, Wakayama|ja|海南市}}, accessible via local transportation routes.
== Gallery == <gallery> File:Kitsumoto Shrine Entrance.jpg|Entrance to Kitsumoto Shrine </gallery>
== See Also == * {{ill|Kujūku Ōji (Kainan)|ja|九十九王子 (海南市)}}
== References == {{Reflist}}
== External Links == * {{Wayback|url=http://www.d1.dion.ne.jp/~konchi/kitumoto/ |title=Kitsumoto Shrine (Official Site)|date=2004-02-16}} * [https://wakayama-jinjacho.or.jp/jdb/sys/user/GetWjtTbl.php?JinjyaNo=2021 Kitsumoto Shrine – Wakayama Prefecture Shrine Office]

revision.comment: Copying from [[Category:Kujūku Ōji]] to [[Category:Completion tier 1]] using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
'''Kitsumoto Shrine''' is one of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|fr|Kujūkuōji|ja|九十九王子|lt=99 ōji Shrines}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. <!--subst:PAGENAME magic word should be substituted in the future when this is moved to its final pagename. This was added to a few pages not created on Feb 8 because they were very barren and this gives context to the fact that they are in a large group of pages all to be translated more or less as a group similar to the Ise Shrines--> == Kitsumoto Shrine == '''Kitsumoto Shrine''' (橘本神社) is a Shinto shrine located in {{ill|Kainan, Wakayama|ja|海南市}}, {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|ja|和歌山県}}, Japan. The shrine enshrines {{nihongo|Tajimamori|田道間守}} and {{nihongo|Kumanoza-no-Ōkami|熊野坐大神}}, and is revered as the deity of confectionery. It is designated as a {{ill|Prefectural Historic Site|ja|和歌山県指定史跡}} (since April 9, 1974), as the former site of {{nihongo|Tokosaka Ōji|所坂王子}}, one of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji}} shrines.<ref name="Kinen">{{Cite web|title=Prefectural Cultural Properties: Historic Sites|url=https://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/prefg/500700/mokuroku/kenkinenbutsu.html|publisher=Wakayama Prefecture Board of Education|accessdate=2021-07-25}}</ref>
== History == According to the ''Kojiki'' and ''Nihon Shoki'', Emperor Suinin ordered Tajimamori to find the legendary fruit of eternal youth. After enduring over ten years of hardship, he returned from the mythical {{nihongo|Tokoyo no Kuni|常世の国}} (often identified with ancient China) with the fruit known as {{nihongo|Tokijiku no Kagunokonomi|非時香菓}}, identified as the {{nihongo|tachibana|橘}}, an ancient citrus fruit. Unfortunately, the emperor had already passed away. In despair, Tajimamori offered the tachibana at the emperor’s tomb and died shortly after.<ref name="Mikan">{{Cite web|title=Mikan Festival|url=http://www.d1.dion.ne.jp/~konchi/kitumoto/menu/mikanmaturi.htm|publisher=Kitsumoto Shrine|accessdate=2015-02-14}}</ref><ref name="Kainanshi">{{Cite web|title=Kitsumoto Shrine (Origin of Mikan, Deity of Confectionery)|url=https://www.city.kainan.lg.jp/kakubusho/machizukuribu/norinsuisangakari/michinoeki/1543927025145.html|publisher=Kainan City|date=2021-03-01|accessdate=2021-07-25}}</ref>
It is said that the tachibana was first planted in Japan at the "Hill of Six Trees," the former site of the shrine to the north. The tachibana is considered the ancestor of modern mandarins, and this region is known as the birthplace of mikan cultivation.<ref name="Mikan"/>
The shrine’s founding date is unknown, but a roof plaque dated 1437 (Eikyō 9) still exists.<ref name="Rekishi">{{Cite web|title=Shrine History|url=http://www.d1.dion.ne.jp/~konchi/kitumoto/menu/framepage.htm|publisher=Kitsumoto Shrine|accessdate=2015-02-14}}</ref><ref name="Nikkeii">{{Cite web|title=Why Is the "Confectionery Deity" in Wakayama? (Mystery Cruise)|url=https://www.nikkei.com/article/DGXLASHD15H02_V10C14A8AA1P00/|publisher=Nikkei|date=2014-08-20|accessdate=2021-07-25}}</ref>
In 1158 (Hōgen 3), Emperor Shirakawa reportedly stayed at the shrine during his pilgrimage to Kumano and composed the poem: ''"Spending a night under the tachibana tree, I gaze at the moon over Mt. Irisa."''<ref name="Rekishi"/>
During the Edo period, Kishu Domain lord {{nihongo|Tokugawa Mitsusada|徳川光貞}} ordered the repair of the shrine in 1687 (Jōkyō 4). However, it later fell into decline, leaving only an old tachibana tree and a small shrine by the early Meiji era. Thanks to local efforts, the shrine was restored, and in November 1907, it was relocated to its current site on the former grounds of Tokosaka Ōji. At this time, two other local Ōji shrines—{{nihongo|Tōge-shita Ōji|搭下王子}} and {{nihongo|Kitsumoto Ōji|橘本王子}}—were also enshrined here.<ref name="Rekishi"/><ref name="Nikkeii"/>
== Festivals == The region is famous for mikan production, and the annual festival on October 10 is known as the ''Mikan Festival,'' commemorating Tajimamori’s introduction of citrus to Japan.<ref name="Mikan"/><ref name="Kainanshi"/>
Additionally, the ''Confectionery Festival'' (''Zenkoku Meika Hōkensai'') is held on the first Sunday of April. This event honors Tajimamori as the deity of sweets, as mikan were historically regarded as confections. Confectioners from across Japan offer their specialty sweets in prayer for prosperity.<ref>{{Cite web|title=Confectionery Festival|url=http://www.d1.dion.ne.jp/~konchi/kitumoto/menu/kashimaturi.htm|publisher=Kitsumoto Shrine|accessdate=2015-02-14}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|title=Kitsumoto Shrine’s Confectionery Festival|url=https://www.city.kainan.lg.jp/kanko/matsuri/1543928726429.html|publisher=Kainan City|date=2021-03-18|accessdate=2021-07-25}}</ref>
== Deities Enshrined == * {{nihongo|Tajimamori|田道間守}} – Deity of sweets and mikan cultivation * {{nihongo|Kumanoza-no-Ōkami|熊野坐大神}} – Associated with the Kumano faith
== Access == * Located in {{ill|Kainan, Wakayama|ja|海南市}}, accessible via local transportation routes.
== Gallery == <gallery> File:Kitsumoto Shrine Entrance.jpg|Entrance to Kitsumoto Shrine </gallery>
== See Also == * {{ill|Kujūku Ōji (Kainan)|ja|九十九王子 (海南市)}}
== References == {{Reflist}}
== External Links == * {{Wayback|url=http://www.d1.dion.ne.jp/~konchi/kitumoto/ |title=Kitsumoto Shrine (Official Site)|date=2004-02-16}} * [https://wakayama-jinjacho.or.jp/jdb/sys/user/GetWjtTbl.php?JinjyaNo=2021 Kitsumoto Shrine – Wakayama Prefecture Shrine Office]

revision.comment: Removing from [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
'''Kitsumoto Shrine''' is one of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|fr|Kujūkuōji|ja|九十九王子|lt=99 ōji Shrines}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. <!--subst:PAGENAME magic word should be substituted in the future when this is moved to its final pagename. This was added to a few pages not created on Feb 8 because they were very barren and this gives context to the fact that they are in a large group of pages all to be translated more or less as a group similar to the Ise Shrines--> == Kitsumoto Shrine == '''Kitsumoto Shrine''' (橘本神社) is a Shinto shrine located in {{ill|Kainan, Wakayama|ja|海南市}}, {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|ja|和歌山県}}, Japan. The shrine enshrines {{nihongo|Tajimamori|田道間守}} and {{nihongo|Kumanoza-no-Ōkami|熊野坐大神}}, and is revered as the deity of confectionery. It is designated as a {{ill|Prefectural Historic Site|ja|和歌山県指定史跡}} (since April 9, 1974), as the former site of {{nihongo|Tokosaka Ōji|所坂王子}}, one of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji}} shrines.<ref name="Kinen">{{Cite web|title=Prefectural Cultural Properties: Historic Sites|url=https://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/prefg/500700/mokuroku/kenkinenbutsu.html|publisher=Wakayama Prefecture Board of Education|accessdate=2021-07-25}}</ref>
== History == According to the ''Kojiki'' and ''Nihon Shoki'', Emperor Suinin ordered Tajimamori to find the legendary fruit of eternal youth. After enduring over ten years of hardship, he returned from the mythical {{nihongo|Tokoyo no Kuni|常世の国}} (often identified with ancient China) with the fruit known as {{nihongo|Tokijiku no Kagunokonomi|非時香菓}}, identified as the {{nihongo|tachibana|橘}}, an ancient citrus fruit. Unfortunately, the emperor had already passed away. In despair, Tajimamori offered the tachibana at the emperor’s tomb and died shortly after.<ref name="Mikan">{{Cite web|title=Mikan Festival|url=http://www.d1.dion.ne.jp/~konchi/kitumoto/menu/mikanmaturi.htm|publisher=Kitsumoto Shrine|accessdate=2015-02-14}}</ref><ref name="Kainanshi">{{Cite web|title=Kitsumoto Shrine (Origin of Mikan, Deity of Confectionery)|url=https://www.city.kainan.lg.jp/kakubusho/machizukuribu/norinsuisangakari/michinoeki/1543927025145.html|publisher=Kainan City|date=2021-03-01|accessdate=2021-07-25}}</ref>
It is said that the tachibana was first planted in Japan at the "Hill of Six Trees," the former site of the shrine to the north. The tachibana is considered the ancestor of modern mandarins, and this region is known as the birthplace of mikan cultivation.<ref name="Mikan"/>
The shrine’s founding date is unknown, but a roof plaque dated 1437 (Eikyō 9) still exists.<ref name="Rekishi">{{Cite web|title=Shrine History|url=http://www.d1.dion.ne.jp/~konchi/kitumoto/menu/framepage.htm|publisher=Kitsumoto Shrine|accessdate=2015-02-14}}</ref><ref name="Nikkeii">{{Cite web|title=Why Is the "Confectionery Deity" in Wakayama? (Mystery Cruise)|url=https://www.nikkei.com/article/DGXLASHD15H02_V10C14A8AA1P00/|publisher=Nikkei|date=2014-08-20|accessdate=2021-07-25}}</ref>
In 1158 (Hōgen 3), Emperor Shirakawa reportedly stayed at the shrine during his pilgrimage to Kumano and composed the poem: ''"Spending a night under the tachibana tree, I gaze at the moon over Mt. Irisa."''<ref name="Rekishi"/>
During the Edo period, Kishu Domain lord {{nihongo|Tokugawa Mitsusada|徳川光貞}} ordered the repair of the shrine in 1687 (Jōkyō 4). However, it later fell into decline, leaving only an old tachibana tree and a small shrine by the early Meiji era. Thanks to local efforts, the shrine was restored, and in November 1907, it was relocated to its current site on the former grounds of Tokosaka Ōji. At this time, two other local Ōji shrines—{{nihongo|Tōge-shita Ōji|搭下王子}} and {{nihongo|Kitsumoto Ōji|橘本王子}}—were also enshrined here.<ref name="Rekishi"/><ref name="Nikkeii"/>
== Festivals == The region is famous for mikan production, and the annual festival on October 10 is known as the ''Mikan Festival,'' commemorating Tajimamori’s introduction of citrus to Japan.<ref name="Mikan"/><ref name="Kainanshi"/>
Additionally, the ''Confectionery Festival'' (''Zenkoku Meika Hōkensai'') is held on the first Sunday of April. This event honors Tajimamori as the deity of sweets, as mikan were historically regarded as confections. Confectioners from across Japan offer their specialty sweets in prayer for prosperity.<ref>{{Cite web|title=Confectionery Festival|url=http://www.d1.dion.ne.jp/~konchi/kitumoto/menu/kashimaturi.htm|publisher=Kitsumoto Shrine|accessdate=2015-02-14}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|title=Kitsumoto Shrine’s Confectionery Festival|url=https://www.city.kainan.lg.jp/kanko/matsuri/1543928726429.html|publisher=Kainan City|date=2021-03-18|accessdate=2021-07-25}}</ref>
== Deities Enshrined == * {{nihongo|Tajimamori|田道間守}} – Deity of sweets and mikan cultivation * {{nihongo|Kumanoza-no-Ōkami|熊野坐大神}} – Associated with the Kumano faith
== Access == * Located in {{ill|Kainan, Wakayama|ja|海南市}}, accessible via local transportation routes.
== Gallery == <gallery> File:Kitsumoto Shrine Entrance.jpg|Entrance to Kitsumoto Shrine </gallery>
== See Also == * {{ill|Kujūku Ōji (Kainan)|ja|九十九王子 (海南市)}}
== References == {{Reflist}}
== External Links == * {{Wayback|url=http://www.d1.dion.ne.jp/~konchi/kitumoto/ |title=Kitsumoto Shrine (Official Site)|date=2004-02-16}} * [https://wakayama-jinjacho.or.jp/jdb/sys/user/GetWjtTbl.php?JinjyaNo=2021 Kitsumoto Shrine – Wakayama Prefecture Shrine Office]
{{Shinto-stub}}
