revision.text:
{{神社 | 名称=橘本神社 | 画像=[[File:Kitsumoto Shrine Entrance.jpg|250px]] | 所在地=[[和歌山県]][[海南市]]下津町橘本779 | 位置={{Coord|34|7|27.6|N|135|11|44.7|E|region:JP-30_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | 祭神=[[田道間守]]、熊野坐大神 | 例祭=[[10月10日]] | 地図 = {{Location map|Japan Wakayama Prefecture|label=橘本神社|float=center|relief=1|width=256}} }} '''Kitsumoto Shrine (橘本神社)''' is one of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|fr|Kujūkuōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|lt=99 ōji Shrines|12=draft}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. It is a Shinto shrine located in {{ill|Kainan, Wakayama|ja|海南市|12=draft}}, {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|ja|和歌山県|12=draft}}, Japan. The shrine enshrines {{nihongo|[[Tajimamori]]|田道間守}} and {{nihongo|Kumanoza-no-Ōkami|熊野坐大神}}, and is revered as the deity of confectionery. It is designated as a {{ill|Prefectural Historic Site|ja|和歌山県指定史跡|12=draft}} (since April 9, 1974), as the former site of {{nihongo|Tokosaka Ōji|所坂王子}}, one of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|12=draft}} shrines.<ref name="Kinen">{{Cite web|title=Prefectural Cultural Properties: Historic Sites|url=https://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/prefg/500700/mokuroku/kenkinenbutsu.html|publisher=Wakayama Prefecture Board of Education|accessdate=2021-07-25}}</ref>
== History == According to the ''Kojiki'' and ''Nihon Shoki'', Emperor Suinin ordered Tajimamori to find the legendary fruit of eternal youth. After enduring over ten years of hardship, he returned from the mythical {{nihongo|Tokoyo no Kuni|常世の国}} (often identified with ancient China) with the fruit known as {{nihongo|Tokijiku no Kagunokonomi|非時香菓}}, identified as the {{nihongo|tachibana|橘}}, an ancient citrus fruit. Unfortunately, the emperor had already passed away. In despair, Tajimamori offered the tachibana at the emperor’s tomb and died shortly after.<ref name="Mikan">{{Cite web|title=Mikan Festival|url=http://www.d1.dion.ne.jp/~konchi/kitumoto/menu/mikanmaturi.htm|publisher=Kitsumoto Shrine|accessdate=2015-02-14}}</ref><ref name="Kainanshi">{{Cite web|title=Kitsumoto Shrine (Origin of Mikan, Deity of Confectionery)|url=https://www.city.kainan.lg.jp/kakubusho/machizukuribu/norinsuisangakari/michinoeki/1543927025145.html|publisher=Kainan City|date=2021-03-01|accessdate=2021-07-25}}</ref>
It is said that the tachibana was first planted in Japan at the "Hill of Six Trees," the former site of the shrine to the north. The tachibana is considered the ancestor of modern mandarins, and this region is known as the birthplace of mikan cultivation.<ref name="Mikan"/>
The shrine’s founding date is unknown, but a roof plaque dated 1437 (Eikyō 9) still exists.<ref name="Rekishi">{{Cite web|title=Shrine History|url=http://www.d1.dion.ne.jp/~konchi/kitumoto/menu/framepage.htm|publisher=Kitsumoto Shrine|accessdate=2015-02-14}}</ref><ref name="Nikkeii">{{Cite web|title=Why Is the "Confectionery Deity" in Wakayama? (Mystery Cruise)|url=https://www.nikkei.com/article/DGXLASHD15H02_V10C14A8AA1P00/|publisher=Nikkei|date=2014-08-20|accessdate=2021-07-25}}</ref>
In 1158 (Hōgen 3), Emperor Shirakawa reportedly stayed at the shrine during his pilgrimage to Kumano and composed the poem: ''"Spending a night under the tachibana tree, I gaze at the moon over Mt. Irisa."''<ref name="Rekishi"/>
During the Edo period, Kishu Domain lord {{nihongo|Tokugawa Mitsusada|徳川光貞}} ordered the repair of the shrine in 1687 (Jōkyō 4). However, it later fell into decline, leaving only an old tachibana tree and a small shrine by the early Meiji era. Thanks to local efforts, the shrine was restored, and in November 1907, it was relocated to its current site on the former grounds of Tokosaka Ōji. At this time, two other local Ōji shrines—{{nihongo|Tōge-shita Ōji|搭下王子}} and {{nihongo|Kitsumoto Ōji|橘本王子}}—were also enshrined here.<ref name="Rekishi"/><ref name="Nikkeii"/>
== Festivals == The region is famous for mikan production, and the annual festival on October 10 is known as the ''Mikan Festival,'' commemorating Tajimamori’s introduction of citrus to Japan.<ref name="Mikan"/><ref name="Kainanshi"/>
Additionally, the ''Confectionery Festival'' (''Zenkoku Meika Hōkensai'') is held on the first Sunday of April. This event honors Tajimamori as the deity of sweets, as mikan were historically regarded as confections. Confectioners from across Japan offer their specialty sweets in prayer for prosperity.<ref>{{Cite web|title=Confectionery Festival|url=http://www.d1.dion.ne.jp/~konchi/kitumoto/menu/kashimaturi.htm|publisher=Kitsumoto Shrine|accessdate=2015-02-14}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|title=Kitsumoto Shrine’s Confectionery Festival|url=https://www.city.kainan.lg.jp/kanko/matsuri/1543928726429.html|publisher=Kainan City|date=2021-03-18|accessdate=2021-07-25}}</ref>
== Deities Enshrined == * {{nihongo|Tajimamori|田道間守}} – Deity of sweets and mikan cultivation * {{nihongo|Kumanoza-no-Ōkami|熊野坐大神}} – Associated with the Kumano faith
== Access == * Located in {{ill|Kainan, Wakayama|ja|海南市|12=draft}}, accessible via local transportation routes.
== Gallery == <gallery> File:Kitsumoto Shrine Entrance.jpg|Entrance to Kitsumoto Shrine </gallery>
== See Also == * {{ill|Kujūku Ōji (Kainan)|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji (海南市)|1=List of Kujūku Ōji (Kainan City)|12=draft}}
== References == {{Reflist}}
== External Links == * {{Wayback|url=http://www.d1.dion.ne.jp/~konchi/kitumoto/ |title=Kitsumoto Shrine (Official Site)|date=2004-02-16}} * [https://wakayama-jinjacho.or.jp/jdb/sys/user/GetWjtTbl.php?JinjyaNo=2021 Kitsumoto Shrine – Wakayama Prefecture Shrine Office]

revision.comment: Removing from [[Category:Qq]] on using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
{{神社 | 名称=橘本神社 | 画像=[[File:Kitsumoto Shrine Entrance.jpg|250px]] | 所在地=[[和歌山県]][[海南市]]下津町橘本779 | 位置={{Coord|34|7|27.6|N|135|11|44.7|E|region:JP-30_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | 祭神=[[田道間守]]、熊野坐大神 | 例祭=[[10月10日]] | 地図 = {{Location map|Japan Wakayama Prefecture|label=橘本神社|float=center|relief=1|width=256}} }} '''Kitsumoto Shrine (橘本神社)''' is one of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|fr|Kujūkuōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|lt=99 ōji Shrines|12=draft}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. It is a Shinto shrine located in {{ill|Kainan, Wakayama|ja|海南市|12=draft}}, {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|ja|和歌山県|12=draft}}, Japan. The shrine enshrines {{nihongo|[[Tajimamori]]|田道間守}} and {{nihongo|Kumanoza-no-Ōkami|熊野坐大神}}, and is revered as the deity of confectionery. It is designated as a {{ill|Prefectural Historic Site|ja|和歌山県指定史跡|12=draft}} (since April 9, 1974), as the former site of {{nihongo|Tokosaka Ōji|所坂王子}}, one of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|12=draft}} shrines.<ref name="Kinen">{{Cite web|title=Prefectural Cultural Properties: Historic Sites|url=https://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/prefg/500700/mokuroku/kenkinenbutsu.html|publisher=Wakayama Prefecture Board of Education|accessdate=2021-07-25}}</ref>
== History == According to the ''Kojiki'' and ''Nihon Shoki'', Emperor Suinin ordered Tajimamori to find the legendary fruit of eternal youth. After enduring over ten years of hardship, he returned from the mythical {{nihongo|Tokoyo no Kuni|常世の国}} (often identified with ancient China) with the fruit known as {{nihongo|Tokijiku no Kagunokonomi|非時香菓}}, identified as the {{nihongo|tachibana|橘}}, an ancient citrus fruit. Unfortunately, the emperor had already passed away. In despair, Tajimamori offered the tachibana at the emperor’s tomb and died shortly after.<ref name="Mikan">{{Cite web|title=Mikan Festival|url=http://www.d1.dion.ne.jp/~konchi/kitumoto/menu/mikanmaturi.htm|publisher=Kitsumoto Shrine|accessdate=2015-02-14}}</ref><ref name="Kainanshi">{{Cite web|title=Kitsumoto Shrine (Origin of Mikan, Deity of Confectionery)|url=https://www.city.kainan.lg.jp/kakubusho/machizukuribu/norinsuisangakari/michinoeki/1543927025145.html|publisher=Kainan City|date=2021-03-01|accessdate=2021-07-25}}</ref>
It is said that the tachibana was first planted in Japan at the "Hill of Six Trees," the former site of the shrine to the north. The tachibana is considered the ancestor of modern mandarins, and this region is known as the birthplace of mikan cultivation.<ref name="Mikan"/>
The shrine’s founding date is unknown, but a roof plaque dated 1437 (Eikyō 9) still exists.<ref name="Rekishi">{{Cite web|title=Shrine History|url=http://www.d1.dion.ne.jp/~konchi/kitumoto/menu/framepage.htm|publisher=Kitsumoto Shrine|accessdate=2015-02-14}}</ref><ref name="Nikkeii">{{Cite web|title=Why Is the "Confectionery Deity" in Wakayama? (Mystery Cruise)|url=https://www.nikkei.com/article/DGXLASHD15H02_V10C14A8AA1P00/|publisher=Nikkei|date=2014-08-20|accessdate=2021-07-25}}</ref>
In 1158 (Hōgen 3), Emperor Shirakawa reportedly stayed at the shrine during his pilgrimage to Kumano and composed the poem: ''"Spending a night under the tachibana tree, I gaze at the moon over Mt. Irisa."''<ref name="Rekishi"/>
During the Edo period, Kishu Domain lord {{nihongo|Tokugawa Mitsusada|徳川光貞}} ordered the repair of the shrine in 1687 (Jōkyō 4). However, it later fell into decline, leaving only an old tachibana tree and a small shrine by the early Meiji era. Thanks to local efforts, the shrine was restored, and in November 1907, it was relocated to its current site on the former grounds of Tokosaka Ōji. At this time, two other local Ōji shrines—{{nihongo|Tōge-shita Ōji|搭下王子}} and {{nihongo|Kitsumoto Ōji|橘本王子}}—were also enshrined here.<ref name="Rekishi"/><ref name="Nikkeii"/>
== Festivals == The region is famous for mikan production, and the annual festival on October 10 is known as the ''Mikan Festival,'' commemorating Tajimamori’s introduction of citrus to Japan.<ref name="Mikan"/><ref name="Kainanshi"/>
Additionally, the ''Confectionery Festival'' (''Zenkoku Meika Hōkensai'') is held on the first Sunday of April. This event honors Tajimamori as the deity of sweets, as mikan were historically regarded as confections. Confectioners from across Japan offer their specialty sweets in prayer for prosperity.<ref>{{Cite web|title=Confectionery Festival|url=http://www.d1.dion.ne.jp/~konchi/kitumoto/menu/kashimaturi.htm|publisher=Kitsumoto Shrine|accessdate=2015-02-14}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|title=Kitsumoto Shrine’s Confectionery Festival|url=https://www.city.kainan.lg.jp/kanko/matsuri/1543928726429.html|publisher=Kainan City|date=2021-03-18|accessdate=2021-07-25}}</ref>
== Deities Enshrined == * {{nihongo|Tajimamori|田道間守}} – Deity of sweets and mikan cultivation * {{nihongo|Kumanoza-no-Ōkami|熊野坐大神}} – Associated with the Kumano faith
== Access == * Located in {{ill|Kainan, Wakayama|ja|海南市|12=draft}}, accessible via local transportation routes.
== Gallery == <gallery> File:Kitsumoto Shrine Entrance.jpg|Entrance to Kitsumoto Shrine </gallery>
== See Also == * {{ill|Kujūku Ōji (Kainan)|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji (海南市)|1=List of Kujūku Ōji (Kainan City)|12=draft}}
== References == {{Reflist}}
== External Links == * {{Wayback|url=http://www.d1.dion.ne.jp/~konchi/kitumoto/ |title=Kitsumoto Shrine (Official Site)|date=2004-02-16}} * [https://wakayama-jinjacho.or.jp/jdb/sys/user/GetWjtTbl.php?JinjyaNo=2021 Kitsumoto Shrine – Wakayama Prefecture Shrine Office]

revision.text:
{{神社 | 名称=橘本神社 | 画像=[[File:Kitsumoto Shrine Entrance.jpg|250px]] | 所在地=[[和歌山県]][[海南市]]下津町橘本779 | 位置={{Coord|34|7|27.6|N|135|11|44.7|E|region:JP-30_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | 祭神=[[田道間守]]、熊野坐大神 | 例祭=[[10月10日]] | 地図 = {{Location map|Japan Wakayama Prefecture|label=橘本神社|float=center|relief=1|width=256}} }} '''橘本神社'''（きつもとじんじゃ）は、[[和歌山県]][[海南市]]にある[[神社]]である。菓祖・[[田道間守]]および熊野坐大神を祀り、[[菓子]]の神として崇敬される。熊野[[九十九王子]]の中の一つ、所坂王子の旧址として県指定史跡（1974年〈昭和49年〉4月9日指定）<ref name="Kinen">{{Cite web|和書|publisher=和歌山県教育委員会|url=https://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/prefg/500700/mokuroku/kenkinenbutsu.html|title=県指定文化財・記念物|accessdate=2021-07-25}}</ref>。
== 歴史 == 「記紀」によれば第十一代垂仁天皇が田道間守に不老長寿の霊菓を探すことを命じ、[[常世の国]]に渡り十余年の辛苦の末に非時香菓(ときじくのかぐのこのみ)「[[橘]]」を持ち帰る(現在の中国から持ち帰ったといわれている)が、天皇は既に崩御されていた。田道間守は落胆悲涙し、その陵に橘を捧げて命絶えたとされている。その橘が日本で最初に移植されたのが現在地北の橘本神社旧社地「六本樹の丘」である。橘は現在のミカンの原種で、改良されミカン発祥の地といわれている。<ref name="Mikan">{{Cite web|和書|url=http://www.d1.dion.ne.jp/~konchi/kitumoto/menu/mikanmaturi.htm|title=みかん祭|publisher=[http://www.d1.dion.ne.jp/~konchi/kitumoto/ 橘本神社]|accessdate=2015-02-14}}</ref><ref name="Kainanshi">{{Cite web|和書|publisher=海南市|date=2021-03-01|url=https://www.city.kainan.lg.jp/kakubusho/machizukuribu/norinsuisangakari/michinoeki/1543927025145.html|title=橘本神社（みかん発祥の地、お菓子の神様）|accessdate=2021-07-25}}</ref>。
創建の年代は不詳であるが、[[永享]]9年（[[1437年]]）と記された神社の棟札が残っている<ref name="Rekishi">{{Cite web|和書|url=http://www.d1.dion.ne.jp/~konchi/kitumoto/menu/framepage.htm|title=由緒由来（橘本神社公式ウェブサイト）|publisher=橘本神社|accessdate=2015-02-14}}</ref><ref name="Nikkeii">{{Cite web|和書|publisher=[[日本経済新聞社]]|date=2014-08-20|url=https://www.nikkei.com/article/DGXLASHD15H02_V10C14A8AA1P00/|title= 「お菓子の神様」なぜ和歌山に（謎解きクルーズ） ミカンの原種タチバナの木、元祖伝わる 毎年の祭り、各社が看板商品|website=[[日本経済新聞]]|accessdate=2021-07-25}}</ref>。
[[保元]]3年（[[1158年]]）、[[白河天皇|白河法皇]]が熊野行幸の際に当社で一夜を明かし「橘の本に一夜のかりねして入佐の山の月を見るかな」と詠んだ<ref name="Rekishi"/>。
[[貞享]]4年（[[1687年]]）、[[紀州藩]]主[[徳川光貞]]が社殿の修繕を行ったという記録があるが、その後廃頽し、明治初年には橘の老木と小祠だけが残されていた。里人の尽力により再興し、[[明治]]40年（[[1907年]]）11月、現在地の所坂王子（[[九十九王子]]の一つ）跡地に社殿を造営して遷座し、所坂王子と近隣の[[九十九王子 (海南市)#藤白塔下王子|搭下王子]]・[[九十九王子 (海南市)#橘本王子|橘本王子]]を合祀した<ref name="Rekishi"/><ref name="Nikkeii"/>。
== 祭事 == 一帯はみかんの産地であり、[[10月10日]]の例祭は「みかん祭」と呼ばれ、祭神の田道間守が日本にみかんをもたらしたことに因む祭事である<ref name="Mikan"/><ref name="Kainanshi"/>。
また、[[4月]]第1[[日曜日]]に催行される全国銘菓奉献祭（菓子祭）は、その昔みかんが菓子として好まれたことにちなんで菓子屋の商売繁盛を祈願する祭であり、全国の菓子屋から各種の菓子が供えられる<ref>{{Cite web|和書|url=http://www.d1.dion.ne.jp/~konchi/kitumoto/menu/kashimaturi.htm|title=菓子祭・全国銘菓奉献祭|publisher=[http://www.d1.dion.ne.jp/~konchi/kitumoto/ 橘本神社]|accessdate=2015-02-14}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|和書|publisher=海南市|date=2021-03-18|url=https://www.city.kainan.lg.jp/kanko/matsuri/1543928726429.html|title=橘本神社の菓子祭|accessdate=2021-07-25}}</ref>。
== 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist}}
== 関連項目 == * [[九十九王子 (海南市)]]
== 外部リンク == * {{Wayback|url=http://www.d1.dion.ne.jp/~konchi/kitumoto/ |title=橘本神社 |date=20040216001702}}（公式サイト） * [https://wakayama-jinjacho.or.jp/jdb/sys/user/GetWjtTbl.php?JinjyaNo=2021 橘本神社] - 和歌山県神社庁
{{神道 横}} {{DEFAULTSORT:きつもとしんしや}} [[Category:和歌山県の神社]] [[Category:九十九王子]] [[Category:海南市の建築物]] [[Category:海南市の歴史]] [[Category:和歌山県指定史跡]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:橘本神社]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11544292]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:34, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11544292]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 04:14, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building | 名称=橘本神社 | 画像=[[File:Kitsumoto Shrine Entrance.jpg|250px]] | 所在地=[[和歌山県]][[海南市]]下津町橘本779 | 位置={{Coord|34|7|27.6|N|135|11|44.7|E|region:JP-30_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | 祭神=[[田道間守]]、熊野坐大神 | 例祭=[[10月10日]] | 地図 = {{Location map|Japan Wakayama Prefecture|label=橘本神社|float=center|relief=1|width=256}} | image=File:Kitsumoto Shrine Entrance.jpg | prefecture={{ill|Kainan|qid=Q841309}} | coordinates={{Coord|34.12433333333333|N|135.19575|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} }} '''Kitsumoto Shrine (橘本神社)''' is one of the {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|fr|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=99 ōji Shrines|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. It is a Shinto shrine located in [[:en:Kainan, Wakayama|Kainan, Wakayama]], [[:en:Wakayama Prefecture|Wakayama Prefecture]], Japan. The shrine enshrines {{nihongo|[[:en:Tajimamori|Tajimamori]]|田道間守}} and {{nihongo|Kumanoza-no-Ōkami|熊野坐大神}}, and is revered as the deity of confectionery. It is designated as a {{ill|Prefectural Historic Site|ja|和歌山県指定文化財一覧|1=List of Wakayama Prefecture designated cultural properties|2=ja|3=和歌山県指定文化財一覧|lt=Prefectural Historic Site|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prefectural Historic Site|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11417412}} (since April 9, 1974), as the former site of {{nihongo|Tokosaka Ōji|所坂王子}}, one of the {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}} shrines.<ref name="Kinen">{{Cite web|title=Prefectural Cultural Properties: Historic Sites|url=https://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/prefg/500700/mokuroku/kenkinenbutsu.html|publisher=Wakayama Prefecture Board of Education|accessdate=2021-07-25}}</ref>
== History == According to the ''Kojiki'' and ''Nihon Shoki'', Emperor Suinin ordered Tajimamori to find the legendary fruit of eternal youth. After enduring over ten years of hardship, he returned from the mythical {{nihongo|Tokoyo no Kuni|常世の国}} (often identified with ancient China) with the fruit known as {{nihongo|Tokijiku no Kagunokonomi|非時香菓}}, identified as the {{nihongo|tachibana|橘}}, an ancient citrus fruit. Unfortunately, the emperor had already passed away. In despair, Tajimamori offered the tachibana at the emperor’s tomb and died shortly after.<ref name="Mikan">{{Cite web|title=Mikan Festival|url=http://www.d1.dion.ne.jp/~konchi/kitumoto/menu/mikanmaturi.htm|publisher=Kitsumoto Shrine|accessdate=2015-02-14}}</ref><ref name="Kainanshi">{{Cite web|title=Kitsumoto Shrine (Origin of Mikan, Deity of Confectionery)|url=https://www.city.kainan.lg.jp/kakubusho/machizukuribu/norinsuisangakari/michinoeki/1543927025145.html|publisher=Kainan City|date=2021-03-01|accessdate=2021-07-25}}</ref>
It is said that the tachibana was first planted in Japan at the "Hill of Six Trees," the former site of the shrine to the north. The tachibana is considered the ancestor of modern mandarins, and this region is known as the birthplace of mikan cultivation.<ref name="Mikan"/>
The shrine’s founding date is unknown, but a roof plaque dated 1437 (Eikyō 9) still exists.<ref name="Rekishi">{{Cite web|title=Shrine History|url=http://www.d1.dion.ne.jp/~konchi/kitumoto/menu/framepage.htm|publisher=Kitsumoto Shrine|accessdate=2015-02-14}}</ref><ref name="Nikkeii">{{Cite web|title=Why Is the "Confectionery Deity" in Wakayama? (Mystery Cruise)|url=https://www.nikkei.com/article/DGXLASHD15H02_V10C14A8AA1P00/|publisher=Nikkei|date=2014-08-20|accessdate=2021-07-25}}</ref>
In 1158 (Hōgen 3), Emperor Shirakawa reportedly stayed at the shrine during his pilgrimage to Kumano and composed the poem: ''"Spending a night under the tachibana tree, I gaze at the moon over Mt. Irisa."''<ref name="Rekishi"/>
During the Edo period, Kishu Domain lord {{nihongo|Tokugawa Mitsusada|徳川光貞}} ordered the repair of the shrine in 1687 (Jōkyō 4). However, it later fell into decline, leaving only an old tachibana tree and a small shrine by the early Meiji era. Thanks to local efforts, the shrine was restored, and in November 1907, it was relocated to its current site on the former grounds of Tokosaka Ōji. At this time, two other local Ōji shrines—{{nihongo|Tōge-shita Ōji|搭下王子}} and {{nihongo|Kitsumoto Ōji|橘本王子}}—were also enshrined here.<ref name="Rekishi"/><ref name="Nikkeii"/>
== Festivals == The region is famous for mikan production, and the annual festival on October 10 is known as the ''Mikan Festival,'' commemorating Tajimamori’s introduction of citrus to Japan.<ref name="Mikan"/><ref name="Kainanshi"/>
Additionally, the ''Confectionery Festival'' (''Zenkoku Meika Hōkensai'') is held on the first Sunday of April. This event honors Tajimamori as the deity of sweets, as mikan were historically regarded as confections. Confectioners from across Japan offer their specialty sweets in prayer for prosperity.<ref>{{Cite web|title=Confectionery Festival|url=http://www.d1.dion.ne.jp/~konchi/kitumoto/menu/kashimaturi.htm|publisher=Kitsumoto Shrine|accessdate=2015-02-14}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|title=Kitsumoto Shrine’s Confectionery Festival|url=https://www.city.kainan.lg.jp/kanko/matsuri/1543928726429.html|publisher=Kainan City|date=2021-03-18|accessdate=2021-07-25}}</ref>
== Deities Enshrined == * {{nihongo|Tajimamori|田道間守}} – Deity of sweets and mikan cultivation * {{nihongo|Kumanoza-no-Ōkami|熊野坐大神}} – Associated with the Kumano faith
== Access == * Located in [[:en:Kainan, Wakayama|Kainan, Wakayama]], accessible via local transportation routes.
== Gallery == <gallery> File:Kitsumoto Shrine Entrance.jpg|Entrance to Kitsumoto Shrine </gallery>
== See Also == * {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines (Kainan)|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines (海南市)|1=List of Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines (Kainan City)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines (Kainan)|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:橘本神社]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11544292]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:34, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11544292]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 04:14, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building | 名称=橘本神社 | 画像=[[File:Kitsumoto Shrine Entrance.jpg|250px]] | 所在地=[[和歌山県]][[海南市]]下津町橘本779 | 位置={{Coord|34|7|27.6|N|135|11|44.7|E|region:JP-30_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | 祭神=[[田道間守]]、熊野坐大神 | 例祭=[[10月10日]] | 地図 = {{Location map|Japan Wakayama Prefecture|label=橘本神社|float=center|relief=1|width=256}} | image=File:Kitsumoto Shrine Entrance.jpg | prefecture={{ill|Kainan|WD=Q841309}} | coordinates={{Coord|34.12433333333333|N|135.19575|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} }} '''Kitsumoto Shrine (橘本神社)''' is one of the {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|fr|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=99 ōji Shrines|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. It is a Shinto shrine located in [[:en:Kainan, Wakayama|Kainan, Wakayama]], [[:en:Wakayama Prefecture|Wakayama Prefecture]], Japan. The shrine enshrines {{nihongo|[[:en:Tajimamori|Tajimamori]]|田道間守}} and {{nihongo|Kumanoza-no-Ōkami|熊野坐大神}}, and is revered as the deity of confectionery. It is designated as a {{ill|Prefectural Historic Site|ja|和歌山県指定文化財一覧|1=List of Wakayama Prefecture designated cultural properties|2=ja|3=和歌山県指定文化財一覧|lt=Prefectural Historic Site|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prefectural Historic Site|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11417412}} (since April 9, 1974), as the former site of {{nihongo|Tokosaka Ōji|所坂王子}}, one of the {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}} shrines.<ref name="Kinen">{{Cite web|title=Prefectural Cultural Properties: Historic Sites|url=https://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/prefg/500700/mokuroku/kenkinenbutsu.html|publisher=Wakayama Prefecture Board of Education|accessdate=2021-07-25}}</ref>
== History == According to the ''Kojiki'' and ''Nihon Shoki'', Emperor Suinin ordered Tajimamori to find the legendary fruit of eternal youth. After enduring over ten years of hardship, he returned from the mythical {{nihongo|Tokoyo no Kuni|常世の国}} (often identified with ancient China) with the fruit known as {{nihongo|Tokijiku no Kagunokonomi|非時香菓}}, identified as the {{nihongo|tachibana|橘}}, an ancient citrus fruit. Unfortunately, the emperor had already passed away. In despair, Tajimamori offered the tachibana at the emperor’s tomb and died shortly after.<ref name="Mikan">{{Cite web|title=Mikan Festival|url=http://www.d1.dion.ne.jp/~konchi/kitumoto/menu/mikanmaturi.htm|publisher=Kitsumoto Shrine|accessdate=2015-02-14}}</ref><ref name="Kainanshi">{{Cite web|title=Kitsumoto Shrine (Origin of Mikan, Deity of Confectionery)|url=https://www.city.kainan.lg.jp/kakubusho/machizukuribu/norinsuisangakari/michinoeki/1543927025145.html|publisher=Kainan City|date=2021-03-01|accessdate=2021-07-25}}</ref>
It is said that the tachibana was first planted in Japan at the "Hill of Six Trees," the former site of the shrine to the north. The tachibana is considered the ancestor of modern mandarins, and this region is known as the birthplace of mikan cultivation.<ref name="Mikan"/>
The shrine’s founding date is unknown, but a roof plaque dated 1437 (Eikyō 9) still exists.<ref name="Rekishi">{{Cite web|title=Shrine History|url=http://www.d1.dion.ne.jp/~konchi/kitumoto/menu/framepage.htm|publisher=Kitsumoto Shrine|accessdate=2015-02-14}}</ref><ref name="Nikkeii">{{Cite web|title=Why Is the "Confectionery Deity" in Wakayama? (Mystery Cruise)|url=https://www.nikkei.com/article/DGXLASHD15H02_V10C14A8AA1P00/|publisher=Nikkei|date=2014-08-20|accessdate=2021-07-25}}</ref>
In 1158 (Hōgen 3), Emperor Shirakawa reportedly stayed at the shrine during his pilgrimage to Kumano and composed the poem: ''"Spending a night under the tachibana tree, I gaze at the moon over Mt. Irisa."''<ref name="Rekishi"/>
During the Edo period, Kishu Domain lord {{nihongo|Tokugawa Mitsusada|徳川光貞}} ordered the repair of the shrine in 1687 (Jōkyō 4). However, it later fell into decline, leaving only an old tachibana tree and a small shrine by the early Meiji era. Thanks to local efforts, the shrine was restored, and in November 1907, it was relocated to its current site on the former grounds of Tokosaka Ōji. At this time, two other local Ōji shrines—{{nihongo|Tōge-shita Ōji|搭下王子}} and {{nihongo|Kitsumoto Ōji|橘本王子}}—were also enshrined here.<ref name="Rekishi"/><ref name="Nikkeii"/>
== Festivals == The region is famous for mikan production, and the annual festival on October 10 is known as the ''Mikan Festival,'' commemorating Tajimamori’s introduction of citrus to Japan.<ref name="Mikan"/><ref name="Kainanshi"/>
Additionally, the ''Confectionery Festival'' (''Zenkoku Meika Hōkensai'') is held on the first Sunday of April. This event honors Tajimamori as the deity of sweets, as mikan were historically regarded as confections. Confectioners from across Japan offer their specialty sweets in prayer for prosperity.<ref>{{Cite web|title=Confectionery Festival|url=http://www.d1.dion.ne.jp/~konchi/kitumoto/menu/kashimaturi.htm|publisher=Kitsumoto Shrine|accessdate=2015-02-14}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|title=Kitsumoto Shrine’s Confectionery Festival|url=https://www.city.kainan.lg.jp/kanko/matsuri/1543928726429.html|publisher=Kainan City|date=2021-03-18|accessdate=2021-07-25}}</ref>
== Deities Enshrined == * {{nihongo|Tajimamori|田道間守}} – Deity of sweets and mikan cultivation * {{nihongo|Kumanoza-no-Ōkami|熊野坐大神}} – Associated with the Kumano faith
== Access == * Located in [[:en:Kainan, Wakayama|Kainan, Wakayama]], accessible via local transportation routes.
== Gallery == <gallery> File:Kitsumoto Shrine Entrance.jpg|Entrance to Kitsumoto Shrine </gallery>
== See Also == * {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines (Kainan)|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines (海南市)|1=List of Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines (Kainan City)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines (Kainan)|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:橘本神社]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11544292]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:34, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11544292]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 04:14, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building | 名称=橘本神社 | 画像=[[File:Kitsumoto Shrine Entrance.jpg|250px]] | 所在地=[[和歌山県]][[海南市]]下津町橘本779 | 位置={{Coord|34|7|27.6|N|135|11|44.7|E|region:JP-30_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | 祭神=[[田道間守]]、熊野坐大神 | 例祭=[[10月10日]] | 地図 = {{Location map|Japan Wakayama Prefecture|label=橘本神社|float=center|relief=1|width=256}} | image=File:Kitsumoto Shrine Entrance.jpg | prefecture={{ill|Kainan|WD=Q841309}} | coordinates={{Coord|34.12433333333333|N|135.19575|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} }} '''Kitsumoto Shrine (橘本神社)''' is one of the {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|fr|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=99 ōji Shrines|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. It is a Shinto shrine located in [[:en:Kainan, Wakayama|Kainan, Wakayama]], [[:en:Wakayama Prefecture|Wakayama Prefecture]], Japan. The shrine enshrines {{nihongo|[[:en:Tajimamori|Tajimamori]]|田道間守}} and {{nihongo|Kumanoza-no-Ōkami|熊野坐大神}}, and is revered as the deity of confectionery. It is designated as a {{ill|Prefectural Historic Site|ja|和歌山県指定文化財一覧|1=List of Wakayama Prefecture designated cultural properties|2=ja|3=和歌山県指定文化財一覧|lt=Prefectural Historic Site|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prefectural Historic Site|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11417412}} (since April 9, 1974), as the former site of {{nihongo|Tokosaka Ōji|所坂王子}}, one of the {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}} shrines.<ref name="Kinen">{{Cite web|title=Prefectural Cultural Properties: Historic Sites|url=https://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/prefg/500700/mokuroku/kenkinenbutsu.html|publisher=Wakayama Prefecture Board of Education|accessdate=2021-07-25}}</ref>
== History == According to the ''Kojiki'' and ''Nihon Shoki'', Emperor Suinin ordered Tajimamori to find the legendary fruit of eternal youth. After enduring over ten years of hardship, he returned from the mythical {{nihongo|Tokoyo no Kuni|常世の国}} (often identified with ancient China) with the fruit known as {{nihongo|Tokijiku no Kagunokonomi|非時香菓}}, identified as the {{nihongo|tachibana|橘}}, an ancient citrus fruit. Unfortunately, the emperor had already passed away. In despair, Tajimamori offered the tachibana at the emperor’s tomb and died shortly after.<ref name="Mikan">{{Cite web|title=Mikan Festival|url=http://www.d1.dion.ne.jp/~konchi/kitumoto/menu/mikanmaturi.htm|publisher=Kitsumoto Shrine|accessdate=2015-02-14}}</ref><ref name="Kainanshi">{{Cite web|title=Kitsumoto Shrine (Origin of Mikan, Deity of Confectionery)|url=https://www.city.kainan.lg.jp/kakubusho/machizukuribu/norinsuisangakari/michinoeki/1543927025145.html|publisher=Kainan City|date=2021-03-01|accessdate=2021-07-25}}</ref>
It is said that the tachibana was first planted in Japan at the "Hill of Six Trees," the former site of the shrine to the north. The tachibana is considered the ancestor of modern mandarins, and this region is known as the birthplace of mikan cultivation.<ref name="Mikan"/>
The shrine’s founding date is unknown, but a roof plaque dated 1437 (Eikyō 9) still exists.<ref name="Rekishi">{{Cite web|title=Shrine History|url=http://www.d1.dion.ne.jp/~konchi/kitumoto/menu/framepage.htm|publisher=Kitsumoto Shrine|accessdate=2015-02-14}}</ref><ref name="Nikkeii">{{Cite web|title=Why Is the "Confectionery Deity" in Wakayama? (Mystery Cruise)|url=https://www.nikkei.com/article/DGXLASHD15H02_V10C14A8AA1P00/|publisher=Nikkei|date=2014-08-20|accessdate=2021-07-25}}</ref>
In 1158 (Hōgen 3), Emperor Shirakawa reportedly stayed at the shrine during his pilgrimage to Kumano and composed the poem: ''"Spending a night under the tachibana tree, I gaze at the moon over Mt. Irisa."''<ref name="Rekishi"/>
During the Edo period, Kishu Domain lord {{nihongo|Tokugawa Mitsusada|徳川光貞}} ordered the repair of the shrine in 1687 (Jōkyō 4). However, it later fell into decline, leaving only an old tachibana tree and a small shrine by the early Meiji era. Thanks to local efforts, the shrine was restored, and in November 1907, it was relocated to its current site on the former grounds of Tokosaka Ōji. At this time, two other local Ōji shrines—{{nihongo|Tōge-shita Ōji|搭下王子}} and {{nihongo|Kitsumoto Ōji|橘本王子}}—were also enshrined here.<ref name="Rekishi"/><ref name="Nikkeii"/>
== Festivals == The region is famous for mikan production, and the annual festival on October 10 is known as the ''Mikan Festival,'' commemorating Tajimamori’s introduction of citrus to Japan.<ref name="Mikan"/><ref name="Kainanshi"/>
Additionally, the ''Confectionery Festival'' (''Zenkoku Meika Hōkensai'') is held on the first Sunday of April. This event honors Tajimamori as the deity of sweets, as mikan were historically regarded as confections. Confectioners from across Japan offer their specialty sweets in prayer for prosperity.<ref>{{Cite web|title=Confectionery Festival|url=http://www.d1.dion.ne.jp/~konchi/kitumoto/menu/kashimaturi.htm|publisher=Kitsumoto Shrine|accessdate=2015-02-14}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|title=Kitsumoto Shrine’s Confectionery Festival|url=https://www.city.kainan.lg.jp/kanko/matsuri/1543928726429.html|publisher=Kainan City|date=2021-03-18|accessdate=2021-07-25}}</ref>
== Deities Enshrined == * {{nihongo|Tajimamori|田道間守}} – Deity of sweets and mikan cultivation * {{nihongo|Kumanoza-no-Ōkami|熊野坐大神}} – Associated with the Kumano faith
== Access == * Located in [[:en:Kainan, Wakayama|Kainan, Wakayama]], accessible via local transportation routes.
== Gallery == <gallery> File:Kitsumoto Shrine Entrance.jpg|Entrance to Kitsumoto Shrine </gallery>
== See Also == * {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines (Kainan)|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines (海南市)|1=List of Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines (Kainan City)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines (Kainan)|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:橘本神社]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11544292]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:34, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11544292]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 04:14, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building | 名称=橘本神社 | 画像=[[File:Kitsumoto Shrine Entrance.jpg|250px]] | 所在地=[[和歌山県]][[海南市]]下津町橘本779 | 位置={{Coord|34|7|27.6|N|135|11|44.7|E|region:JP-30_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | 祭神=[[田道間守]]、熊野坐大神 | 例祭=[[10月10日]] | 地図 = {{Location map|Japan Wakayama Prefecture|label=橘本神社|float=center|relief=1|width=256}} | image=File:Kitsumoto Shrine Entrance.jpg | prefecture={{ill|Kainan|WD=Q841309}} | coordinates={{Coord|34.12433333333333|N|135.19575|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} }} '''Kitsumoto Shrine (橘本神社)''' is one of the {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|fr|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=99 ōji Shrines|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. It is a Shinto shrine located in [[:en:Kainan, Wakayama|Kainan, Wakayama]], [[:en:Wakayama Prefecture|Wakayama Prefecture]], Japan. The shrine enshrines {{nihongo|[[:en:Tajimamori|Tajimamori]]|田道間守}} and {{nihongo|Kumanoza-no-Ōkami|熊野坐大神}}, and is revered as the deity of confectionery. It is designated as a {{ill|Prefectural Historic Site|ja|和歌山県指定文化財一覧|1=List of Wakayama Prefecture designated cultural properties|2=ja|3=和歌山県指定文化財一覧|lt=Prefectural Historic Site|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prefectural Historic Site|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11417412}} (since April 9, 1974), as the former site of {{nihongo|Tokosaka Ōji|所坂王子}}, one of the {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}} shrines.<ref name="Kinen">{{Cite web|title=Prefectural Cultural Properties: Historic Sites|url=https://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/prefg/500700/mokuroku/kenkinenbutsu.html|publisher=Wakayama Prefecture Board of Education|accessdate=2021-07-25}}</ref>
== History == According to the ''Kojiki'' and ''Nihon Shoki'', Emperor Suinin ordered Tajimamori to find the legendary fruit of eternal youth. After enduring over ten years of hardship, he returned from the mythical {{nihongo|Tokoyo no Kuni|常世の国}} (often identified with ancient China) with the fruit known as {{nihongo|Tokijiku no Kagunokonomi|非時香菓}}, identified as the {{nihongo|tachibana|橘}}, an ancient citrus fruit. Unfortunately, the emperor had already passed away. In despair, Tajimamori offered the tachibana at the emperor’s tomb and died shortly after.<ref name="Mikan">{{Cite web|title=Mikan Festival|url=http://www.d1.dion.ne.jp/~konchi/kitumoto/menu/mikanmaturi.htm|publisher=Kitsumoto Shrine|accessdate=2015-02-14}}</ref><ref name="Kainanshi">{{Cite web|title=Kitsumoto Shrine (Origin of Mikan, Deity of Confectionery)|url=https://www.city.kainan.lg.jp/kakubusho/machizukuribu/norinsuisangakari/michinoeki/1543927025145.html|publisher=Kainan City|date=2021-03-01|accessdate=2021-07-25}}</ref>
It is said that the tachibana was first planted in Japan at the "Hill of Six Trees," the former site of the shrine to the north. The tachibana is considered the ancestor of modern mandarins, and this region is known as the birthplace of mikan cultivation.<ref name="Mikan"/>
The shrine’s founding date is unknown, but a roof plaque dated 1437 (Eikyō 9) still exists.<ref name="Rekishi">{{Cite web|title=Shrine History|url=http://www.d1.dion.ne.jp/~konchi/kitumoto/menu/framepage.htm|publisher=Kitsumoto Shrine|accessdate=2015-02-14}}</ref><ref name="Nikkeii">{{Cite web|title=Why Is the "Confectionery Deity" in Wakayama? (Mystery Cruise)|url=https://www.nikkei.com/article/DGXLASHD15H02_V10C14A8AA1P00/|publisher=Nikkei|date=2014-08-20|accessdate=2021-07-25}}</ref>
In 1158 (Hōgen 3), Emperor Shirakawa reportedly stayed at the shrine during his pilgrimage to Kumano and composed the poem: ''"Spending a night under the tachibana tree, I gaze at the moon over Mt. Irisa."''<ref name="Rekishi"/>
During the Edo period, Kishu Domain lord {{nihongo|Tokugawa Mitsusada|徳川光貞}} ordered the repair of the shrine in 1687 (Jōkyō 4). However, it later fell into decline, leaving only an old tachibana tree and a small shrine by the early Meiji era. Thanks to local efforts, the shrine was restored, and in November 1907, it was relocated to its current site on the former grounds of Tokosaka Ōji. At this time, two other local Ōji shrines—{{nihongo|Tōge-shita Ōji|搭下王子}} and {{nihongo|Kitsumoto Ōji|橘本王子}}—were also enshrined here.<ref name="Rekishi"/><ref name="Nikkeii"/>
== Festivals == The region is famous for mikan production, and the annual festival on October 10 is known as the ''Mikan Festival,'' commemorating Tajimamori’s introduction of citrus to Japan.<ref name="Mikan"/><ref name="Kainanshi"/>
Additionally, the ''Confectionery Festival'' (''Zenkoku Meika Hōkensai'') is held on the first Sunday of April. This event honors Tajimamori as the deity of sweets, as mikan were historically regarded as confections. Confectioners from across Japan offer their specialty sweets in prayer for prosperity.<ref>{{Cite web|title=Confectionery Festival|url=http://www.d1.dion.ne.jp/~konchi/kitumoto/menu/kashimaturi.htm|publisher=Kitsumoto Shrine|accessdate=2015-02-14}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|title=Kitsumoto Shrine’s Confectionery Festival|url=https://www.city.kainan.lg.jp/kanko/matsuri/1543928726429.html|publisher=Kainan City|date=2021-03-18|accessdate=2021-07-25}}</ref>
== Deities Enshrined == * {{nihongo|Tajimamori|田道間守}} – Deity of sweets and mikan cultivation * {{nihongo|Kumanoza-no-Ōkami|熊野坐大神}} – Associated with the Kumano faith
== Access == * Located in [[:en:Kainan, Wakayama|Kainan, Wakayama]], accessible via local transportation routes.
== Gallery == <gallery> File:Kitsumoto Shrine Entrance.jpg|Entrance to Kitsumoto Shrine </gallery>
== See Also == * {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines (Kainan)|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines (海南市)|1=List of Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines (Kainan City)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines (Kainan)|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:橘本神社]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11544292]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:34, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11544292]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 04:14, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building | 名称=橘本神社 | 画像=[[File:Kitsumoto Shrine Entrance.jpg|250px]] | 所在地=[[和歌山県]][[海南市]]下津町橘本779 | 位置={{Coord|34|7|27.6|N|135|11|44.7|E|region:JP-30_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | 祭神=[[田道間守]]、熊野坐大神 | 例祭=[[10月10日]] | 地図 = {{Location map|Japan Wakayama Prefecture|label=橘本神社|float=center|relief=1|width=256}} | image=File:Kitsumoto Shrine Entrance.jpg | prefecture={{ill|Kainan|WD=Q841309}} | coordinates={{Coord|34.12433333333333|N|135.19575|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} }} '''Kitsumoto Shrine (橘本神社)''' is one of the {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|fr|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=99 ōji Shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. It is a Shinto shrine located in [[:en:Kainan, Wakayama|Kainan, Wakayama]], [[:en:Wakayama Prefecture|Wakayama Prefecture]], Japan. The shrine enshrines {{nihongo|[[:en:Tajimamori|Tajimamori]]|田道間守}} and {{nihongo|Kumanoza-no-Ōkami|熊野坐大神}}, and is revered as the deity of confectionery. It is designated as a {{ill|Prefectural Historic Site|ja|和歌山県指定文化財一覧|1=List of Wakayama Prefecture designated cultural properties|2=ja|3=和歌山県指定文化財一覧|lt=Prefectural Historic Site|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prefectural Historic Site|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11417412}} (since April 9, 1974), as the former site of {{nihongo|Tokosaka Ōji|所坂王子}}, one of the {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}} shrines.<ref name="Kinen">{{Cite web|title=Prefectural Cultural Properties: Historic Sites|url=https://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/prefg/500700/mokuroku/kenkinenbutsu.html|publisher=Wakayama Prefecture Board of Education|accessdate=2021-07-25}}</ref>
== History == According to the ''Kojiki'' and ''Nihon Shoki'', Emperor Suinin ordered Tajimamori to find the legendary fruit of eternal youth. After enduring over ten years of hardship, he returned from the mythical {{nihongo|Tokoyo no Kuni|常世の国}} (often identified with ancient China) with the fruit known as {{nihongo|Tokijiku no Kagunokonomi|非時香菓}}, identified as the {{nihongo|tachibana|橘}}, an ancient citrus fruit. Unfortunately, the emperor had already passed away. In despair, Tajimamori offered the tachibana at the emperor’s tomb and died shortly after.<ref name="Mikan">{{Cite web|title=Mikan Festival|url=http://www.d1.dion.ne.jp/~konchi/kitumoto/menu/mikanmaturi.htm|publisher=Kitsumoto Shrine|accessdate=2015-02-14}}</ref><ref name="Kainanshi">{{Cite web|title=Kitsumoto Shrine (Origin of Mikan, Deity of Confectionery)|url=https://www.city.kainan.lg.jp/kakubusho/machizukuribu/norinsuisangakari/michinoeki/1543927025145.html|publisher=Kainan City|date=2021-03-01|accessdate=2021-07-25}}</ref>
It is said that the tachibana was first planted in Japan at the "Hill of Six Trees," the former site of the shrine to the north. The tachibana is considered the ancestor of modern mandarins, and this region is known as the birthplace of mikan cultivation.<ref name="Mikan"/>
The shrine’s founding date is unknown, but a roof plaque dated 1437 (Eikyō 9) still exists.<ref name="Rekishi">{{Cite web|title=Shrine History|url=http://www.d1.dion.ne.jp/~konchi/kitumoto/menu/framepage.htm|publisher=Kitsumoto Shrine|accessdate=2015-02-14}}</ref><ref name="Nikkeii">{{Cite web|title=Why Is the "Confectionery Deity" in Wakayama? (Mystery Cruise)|url=https://www.nikkei.com/article/DGXLASHD15H02_V10C14A8AA1P00/|publisher=Nikkei|date=2014-08-20|accessdate=2021-07-25}}</ref>
In 1158 (Hōgen 3), Emperor Shirakawa reportedly stayed at the shrine during his pilgrimage to Kumano and composed the poem: ''"Spending a night under the tachibana tree, I gaze at the moon over Mt. Irisa."''<ref name="Rekishi"/>
During the Edo period, Kishu Domain lord {{nihongo|Tokugawa Mitsusada|徳川光貞}} ordered the repair of the shrine in 1687 (Jōkyō 4). However, it later fell into decline, leaving only an old tachibana tree and a small shrine by the early Meiji era. Thanks to local efforts, the shrine was restored, and in November 1907, it was relocated to its current site on the former grounds of Tokosaka Ōji. At this time, two other local Ōji shrines—{{nihongo|Tōge-shita Ōji|搭下王子}} and {{nihongo|Kitsumoto Ōji|橘本王子}}—were also enshrined here.<ref name="Rekishi"/><ref name="Nikkeii"/>
== Festivals == The region is famous for mikan production, and the annual festival on October 10 is known as the ''Mikan Festival,'' commemorating Tajimamori’s introduction of citrus to Japan.<ref name="Mikan"/><ref name="Kainanshi"/>
Additionally, the ''Confectionery Festival'' (''Zenkoku Meika Hōkensai'') is held on the first Sunday of April. This event honors Tajimamori as the deity of sweets, as mikan were historically regarded as confections. Confectioners from across Japan offer their specialty sweets in prayer for prosperity.<ref>{{Cite web|title=Confectionery Festival|url=http://www.d1.dion.ne.jp/~konchi/kitumoto/menu/kashimaturi.htm|publisher=Kitsumoto Shrine|accessdate=2015-02-14}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|title=Kitsumoto Shrine’s Confectionery Festival|url=https://www.city.kainan.lg.jp/kanko/matsuri/1543928726429.html|publisher=Kainan City|date=2021-03-18|accessdate=2021-07-25}}</ref>
== Deities Enshrined == * {{nihongo|Tajimamori|田道間守}} – Deity of sweets and mikan cultivation * {{nihongo|Kumanoza-no-Ōkami|熊野坐大神}} – Associated with the Kumano faith
== Access == * Located in [[:en:Kainan, Wakayama|Kainan, Wakayama]], accessible via local transportation routes.
== Gallery == <gallery> File:Kitsumoto Shrine Entrance.jpg|Entrance to Kitsumoto Shrine </gallery>
== See Also == * {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines (Kainan)|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines (海南市)|1=List of Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines (Kainan City)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines (Kainan)|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:橘本神社]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11544292]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:34, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11544292]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 04:14, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building | 名称=橘本神社 | 画像=[[File:Kitsumoto Shrine Entrance.jpg|250px]] | 所在地=[[和歌山県]][[海南市]]下津町橘本779 | 位置={{Coord|34|7|27.6|N|135|11|44.7|E|region:JP-30_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | 祭神=[[田道間守]]、熊野坐大神 | 例祭=[[10月10日]] | 地図 = {{Location map|Japan Wakayama Prefecture|label=橘本神社|float=center|relief=1|width=256}} | image=File:Kitsumoto Shrine Entrance.jpg | prefecture={{ill|Kainan|WD=Q841309}} | coordinates={{Coord|34.12433333333333|N|135.19575|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} }} '''Kitsumoto Shrine (橘本神社)''' is one of the {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|fr|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=99 ōji Shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. It is a Shinto shrine located in [[:en:Kainan, Wakayama|Kainan, Wakayama]], [[:en:Wakayama Prefecture|Wakayama Prefecture]], Japan. The shrine enshrines {{nihongo|[[:en:Tajimamori|Tajimamori]]|田道間守}} and {{nihongo|Kumanoza-no-Ōkami|熊野坐大神}}, and is revered as the deity of confectionery. It is designated as a {{ill|Prefectural Historic Site|ja|和歌山県指定文化財一覧|1=List of Wakayama Prefecture designated cultural properties|2=ja|3=和歌山県指定文化財一覧|lt=Prefectural Historic Site|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prefectural Historic Site|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11417412}} (since April 9, 1974), as the former site of {{nihongo|Tokosaka Ōji|所坂王子}}, one of the {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}} shrines.<ref name="Kinen">{{Cite web|title=Prefectural Cultural Properties: Historic Sites|url=https://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/prefg/500700/mokuroku/kenkinenbutsu.html|publisher=Wakayama Prefecture Board of Education|accessdate=2021-07-25}}</ref>
== History == According to the ''Kojiki'' and ''Nihon Shoki'', Emperor Suinin ordered Tajimamori to find the legendary fruit of eternal youth. After enduring over ten years of hardship, he returned from the mythical {{nihongo|Tokoyo no Kuni|常世の国}} (often identified with ancient China) with the fruit known as {{nihongo|Tokijiku no Kagunokonomi|非時香菓}}, identified as the {{nihongo|tachibana|橘}}, an ancient citrus fruit. Unfortunately, the emperor had already passed away. In despair, Tajimamori offered the tachibana at the emperor’s tomb and died shortly after.<ref name="Mikan">{{Cite web|title=Mikan Festival|url=http://www.d1.dion.ne.jp/~konchi/kitumoto/menu/mikanmaturi.htm|publisher=Kitsumoto Shrine|accessdate=2015-02-14}}</ref><ref name="Kainanshi">{{Cite web|title=Kitsumoto Shrine (Origin of Mikan, Deity of Confectionery)|url=https://www.city.kainan.lg.jp/kakubusho/machizukuribu/norinsuisangakari/michinoeki/1543927025145.html|publisher=Kainan City|date=2021-03-01|accessdate=2021-07-25}}</ref>
It is said that the tachibana was first planted in Japan at the "Hill of Six Trees," the former site of the shrine to the north. The tachibana is considered the ancestor of modern mandarins, and this region is known as the birthplace of mikan cultivation.<ref name="Mikan"/>
The shrine’s founding date is unknown, but a roof plaque dated 1437 (Eikyō 9) still exists.<ref name="Rekishi">{{Cite web|title=Shrine History|url=http://www.d1.dion.ne.jp/~konchi/kitumoto/menu/framepage.htm|publisher=Kitsumoto Shrine|accessdate=2015-02-14}}</ref><ref name="Nikkeii">{{Cite web|title=Why Is the "Confectionery Deity" in Wakayama? (Mystery Cruise)|url=https://www.nikkei.com/article/DGXLASHD15H02_V10C14A8AA1P00/|publisher=Nikkei|date=2014-08-20|accessdate=2021-07-25}}</ref>
In 1158 (Hōgen 3), Emperor Shirakawa reportedly stayed at the shrine during his pilgrimage to Kumano and composed the poem: ''"Spending a night under the tachibana tree, I gaze at the moon over Mt. Irisa."''<ref name="Rekishi"/>
During the Edo period, Kishu Domain lord {{nihongo|Tokugawa Mitsusada|徳川光貞}} ordered the repair of the shrine in 1687 (Jōkyō 4). However, it later fell into decline, leaving only an old tachibana tree and a small shrine by the early Meiji era. Thanks to local efforts, the shrine was restored, and in November 1907, it was relocated to its current site on the former grounds of Tokosaka Ōji. At this time, two other local Ōji shrines—{{nihongo|Tōge-shita Ōji|搭下王子}} and {{nihongo|Kitsumoto Ōji|橘本王子}}—were also enshrined here.<ref name="Rekishi"/><ref name="Nikkeii"/>
== Festivals == The region is famous for mikan production, and the annual festival on October 10 is known as the ''Mikan Festival,'' commemorating Tajimamori’s introduction of citrus to Japan.<ref name="Mikan"/><ref name="Kainanshi"/>
Additionally, the ''Confectionery Festival'' (''Zenkoku Meika Hōkensai'') is held on the first Sunday of April. This event honors Tajimamori as the deity of sweets, as mikan were historically regarded as confections. Confectioners from across Japan offer their specialty sweets in prayer for prosperity.<ref>{{Cite web|title=Confectionery Festival|url=http://www.d1.dion.ne.jp/~konchi/kitumoto/menu/kashimaturi.htm|publisher=Kitsumoto Shrine|accessdate=2015-02-14}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|title=Kitsumoto Shrine’s Confectionery Festival|url=https://www.city.kainan.lg.jp/kanko/matsuri/1543928726429.html|publisher=Kainan City|date=2021-03-18|accessdate=2021-07-25}}</ref>
== Deities Enshrined == * {{nihongo|Tajimamori|田道間守}} – Deity of sweets and mikan cultivation * {{nihongo|Kumanoza-no-Ōkami|熊野坐大神}} – Associated with the Kumano faith
== Access == * Located in [[:en:Kainan, Wakayama|Kainan, Wakayama]], accessible via local transportation routes.
== Gallery == <gallery> File:Kitsumoto Shrine Entrance.jpg|Entrance to Kitsumoto Shrine </gallery>
== See Also == * {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines (Kainan)|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines (海南市)|1=List of Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines (Kainan City)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines (Kainan)|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:橘本神社]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11544292]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:34, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11544292]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 04:14, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building | 名称=橘本神社 | 画像=[[File:Kitsumoto Shrine Entrance.jpg|250px]] | 所在地=[[和歌山県]][[海南市]]下津町橘本779 | 位置={{Coord|34|7|27.6|N|135|11|44.7|E|region:JP-30_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | 祭神=[[田道間守]]、熊野坐大神 | 例祭=[[10月10日]] | 地図 = {{Location map|Japan Wakayama Prefecture|label=橘本神社|float=center|relief=1|width=256}} | image=File:Kitsumoto Shrine Entrance.jpg | prefecture={{ill|Kainan|WD=Q841309}} | coordinates={{Coord|34.12433333333333|N|135.19575|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} }} '''Kitsumoto Shrine (橘本神社)''' is one of the {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|fr|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=99 ōji Shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. It is a Shinto shrine located in [[:en:Kainan, Wakayama|Kainan, Wakayama]], [[:en:Wakayama Prefecture|Wakayama Prefecture]], Japan. The shrine enshrines {{nihongo|[[:en:Tajimamori|Tajimamori]]|田道間守}} and {{nihongo|Kumanoza-no-Ōkami|熊野坐大神}}, and is revered as the deity of confectionery. It is designated as a {{ill|Prefectural Historic Site|ja|和歌山県指定文化財一覧|1=List of Wakayama Prefecture designated cultural properties|2=ja|3=和歌山県指定文化財一覧|lt=Prefectural Historic Site|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prefectural Historic Site|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11417412}} (since April 9, 1974), as the former site of {{nihongo|Tokosaka Ōji|所坂王子}}, one of the {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}} shrines.<ref name="Kinen">{{Cite web|title=Prefectural Cultural Properties: Historic Sites|url=https://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/prefg/500700/mokuroku/kenkinenbutsu.html|publisher=Wakayama Prefecture Board of Education|accessdate=2021-07-25}}</ref>
== History == According to the ''Kojiki'' and ''Nihon Shoki'', Emperor Suinin ordered Tajimamori to find the legendary fruit of eternal youth. After enduring over ten years of hardship, he returned from the mythical {{nihongo|Tokoyo no Kuni|常世の国}} (often identified with ancient China) with the fruit known as {{nihongo|Tokijiku no Kagunokonomi|非時香菓}}, identified as the {{nihongo|tachibana|橘}}, an ancient citrus fruit. Unfortunately, the emperor had already passed away. In despair, Tajimamori offered the tachibana at the emperor’s tomb and died shortly after.<ref name="Mikan">{{Cite web|title=Mikan Festival|url=http://www.d1.dion.ne.jp/~konchi/kitumoto/menu/mikanmaturi.htm|publisher=Kitsumoto Shrine|accessdate=2015-02-14}}</ref><ref name="Kainanshi">{{Cite web|title=Kitsumoto Shrine (Origin of Mikan, Deity of Confectionery)|url=https://www.city.kainan.lg.jp/kakubusho/machizukuribu/norinsuisangakari/michinoeki/1543927025145.html|publisher=Kainan City|date=2021-03-01|accessdate=2021-07-25}}</ref>
It is said that the tachibana was first planted in Japan at the "Hill of Six Trees," the former site of the shrine to the north. The tachibana is considered the ancestor of modern mandarins, and this region is known as the birthplace of mikan cultivation.<ref name="Mikan"/>
The shrine’s founding date is unknown, but a roof plaque dated 1437 (Eikyō 9) still exists.<ref name="Rekishi">{{Cite web|title=Shrine History|url=http://www.d1.dion.ne.jp/~konchi/kitumoto/menu/framepage.htm|publisher=Kitsumoto Shrine|accessdate=2015-02-14}}</ref><ref name="Nikkeii">{{Cite web|title=Why Is the "Confectionery Deity" in Wakayama? (Mystery Cruise)|url=https://www.nikkei.com/article/DGXLASHD15H02_V10C14A8AA1P00/|publisher=Nikkei|date=2014-08-20|accessdate=2021-07-25}}</ref>
In 1158 (Hōgen 3), Emperor Shirakawa reportedly stayed at the shrine during his pilgrimage to Kumano and composed the poem: ''"Spending a night under the tachibana tree, I gaze at the moon over Mt. Irisa."''<ref name="Rekishi"/>
During the Edo period, Kishu Domain lord {{nihongo|Tokugawa Mitsusada|徳川光貞}} ordered the repair of the shrine in 1687 (Jōkyō 4). However, it later fell into decline, leaving only an old tachibana tree and a small shrine by the early Meiji era. Thanks to local efforts, the shrine was restored, and in November 1907, it was relocated to its current site on the former grounds of Tokosaka Ōji. At this time, two other local Ōji shrines—{{nihongo|Tōge-shita Ōji|搭下王子}} and {{nihongo|Kitsumoto Ōji|橘本王子}}—were also enshrined here.<ref name="Rekishi"/><ref name="Nikkeii"/>
== Festivals == The region is famous for mikan production, and the annual festival on October 10 is known as the ''Mikan Festival,'' commemorating Tajimamori’s introduction of citrus to Japan.<ref name="Mikan"/><ref name="Kainanshi"/>
Additionally, the ''Confectionery Festival'' (''Zenkoku Meika Hōkensai'') is held on the first Sunday of April. This event honors Tajimamori as the deity of sweets, as mikan were historically regarded as confections. Confectioners from across Japan offer their specialty sweets in prayer for prosperity.<ref>{{Cite web|title=Confectionery Festival|url=http://www.d1.dion.ne.jp/~konchi/kitumoto/menu/kashimaturi.htm|publisher=Kitsumoto Shrine|accessdate=2015-02-14}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|title=Kitsumoto Shrine’s Confectionery Festival|url=https://www.city.kainan.lg.jp/kanko/matsuri/1543928726429.html|publisher=Kainan City|date=2021-03-18|accessdate=2021-07-25}}</ref>
== Deities Enshrined == * {{nihongo|Tajimamori|田道間守}} – Deity of sweets and mikan cultivation * {{nihongo|Kumanoza-no-Ōkami|熊野坐大神}} – Associated with the Kumano faith
== Access == * Located in [[:en:Kainan, Wakayama|Kainan, Wakayama]], accessible via local transportation routes.
== Gallery == <gallery> File:Kitsumoto Shrine Entrance.jpg|Entrance to Kitsumoto Shrine </gallery>
== See Also == * {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines (Kainan)|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines (海南市)|1=List of Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines (Kainan City)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines (Kainan)|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:橘本神社]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11544292]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:34, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11544292]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 04:14, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building | 名称=橘本神社 | 画像=[[File:Kitsumoto Shrine Entrance.jpg|250px]] | 所在地=[[和歌山県]][[海南市]]下津町橘本779 | 位置={{Coord|34|7|27.6|N|135|11|44.7|E|region:JP-30_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | 祭神=[[田道間守]]、熊野坐大神 | 例祭=[[10月10日]] | 地図 = {{Location map|Japan Wakayama Prefecture|label=橘本神社|float=center|relief=1|width=256}} | image=File:Kitsumoto Shrine Entrance.jpg | prefecture={{ill|Kainan|WD=Q841309}} | coordinates={{Coord|34.12433333333333|N|135.19575|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} }} '''Kitsumoto Shrine (橘本神社)''' is one of the {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|fr|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=99 ōji Shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. It is a Shinto shrine located in [[:en:Kainan, Wakayama|Kainan, Wakayama]], [[:en:Wakayama Prefecture|Wakayama Prefecture]], Japan. The shrine enshrines {{nihongo|[[:en:Tajimamori|Tajimamori]]|田道間守}} and {{nihongo|Kumanoza-no-Ōkami|熊野坐大神}}, and is revered as the deity of confectionery. It is designated as a {{ill|Prefectural Historic Site|ja|和歌山県指定文化財一覧|1=List of Wakayama Prefecture designated cultural properties|2=ja|3=和歌山県指定文化財一覧|lt=Prefectural Historic Site|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prefectural Historic Site|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11417412}} (since April 9, 1974), as the former site of {{nihongo|Tokosaka Ōji|所坂王子}}, one of the {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}} shrines.<ref name="Kinen">{{Cite web|title=Prefectural Cultural Properties: Historic Sites|url=https://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/prefg/500700/mokuroku/kenkinenbutsu.html|publisher=Wakayama Prefecture Board of Education|accessdate=2021-07-25}}</ref>
== History == According to the ''Kojiki'' and ''Nihon Shoki'', Emperor Suinin ordered Tajimamori to find the legendary fruit of eternal youth. After enduring over ten years of hardship, he returned from the mythical {{nihongo|Tokoyo no Kuni|常世の国}} (often identified with ancient China) with the fruit known as {{nihongo|Tokijiku no Kagunokonomi|非時香菓}}, identified as the {{nihongo|tachibana|橘}}, an ancient citrus fruit. Unfortunately, the emperor had already passed away. In despair, Tajimamori offered the tachibana at the emperor’s tomb and died shortly after.<ref name="Mikan">{{Cite web|title=Mikan Festival|url=http://www.d1.dion.ne.jp/~konchi/kitumoto/menu/mikanmaturi.htm|publisher=Kitsumoto Shrine|accessdate=2015-02-14}}</ref><ref name="Kainanshi">{{Cite web|title=Kitsumoto Shrine (Origin of Mikan, Deity of Confectionery)|url=https://www.city.kainan.lg.jp/kakubusho/machizukuribu/norinsuisangakari/michinoeki/1543927025145.html|publisher=Kainan City|date=2021-03-01|accessdate=2021-07-25}}</ref>
It is said that the tachibana was first planted in Japan at the "Hill of Six Trees," the former site of the shrine to the north. The tachibana is considered the ancestor of modern mandarins, and this region is known as the birthplace of mikan cultivation.<ref name="Mikan"/>
The shrine’s founding date is unknown, but a roof plaque dated 1437 (Eikyō 9) still exists.<ref name="Rekishi">{{Cite web|title=Shrine History|url=http://www.d1.dion.ne.jp/~konchi/kitumoto/menu/framepage.htm|publisher=Kitsumoto Shrine|accessdate=2015-02-14}}</ref><ref name="Nikkeii">{{Cite web|title=Why Is the "Confectionery Deity" in Wakayama? (Mystery Cruise)|url=https://www.nikkei.com/article/DGXLASHD15H02_V10C14A8AA1P00/|publisher=Nikkei|date=2014-08-20|accessdate=2021-07-25}}</ref>
In 1158 (Hōgen 3), Emperor Shirakawa reportedly stayed at the shrine during his pilgrimage to Kumano and composed the poem: ''"Spending a night under the tachibana tree, I gaze at the moon over Mt. Irisa."''<ref name="Rekishi"/>
During the Edo period, Kishu Domain lord {{nihongo|Tokugawa Mitsusada|徳川光貞}} ordered the repair of the shrine in 1687 (Jōkyō 4). However, it later fell into decline, leaving only an old tachibana tree and a small shrine by the early Meiji era. Thanks to local efforts, the shrine was restored, and in November 1907, it was relocated to its current site on the former grounds of Tokosaka Ōji. At this time, two other local Ōji shrines—{{nihongo|Tōge-shita Ōji|搭下王子}} and {{nihongo|Kitsumoto Ōji|橘本王子}}—were also enshrined here.<ref name="Rekishi"/><ref name="Nikkeii"/>
== Festivals == The region is famous for mikan production, and the annual festival on October 10 is known as the ''Mikan Festival,'' commemorating Tajimamori’s introduction of citrus to Japan.<ref name="Mikan"/><ref name="Kainanshi"/>
Additionally, the ''Confectionery Festival'' (''Zenkoku Meika Hōkensai'') is held on the first Sunday of April. This event honors Tajimamori as the deity of sweets, as mikan were historically regarded as confections. Confectioners from across Japan offer their specialty sweets in prayer for prosperity.<ref>{{Cite web|title=Confectionery Festival|url=http://www.d1.dion.ne.jp/~konchi/kitumoto/menu/kashimaturi.htm|publisher=Kitsumoto Shrine|accessdate=2015-02-14}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|title=Kitsumoto Shrine’s Confectionery Festival|url=https://www.city.kainan.lg.jp/kanko/matsuri/1543928726429.html|publisher=Kainan City|date=2021-03-18|accessdate=2021-07-25}}</ref>
== Deities Enshrined == * {{nihongo|Tajimamori|田道間守}} – Deity of sweets and mikan cultivation * {{nihongo|Kumanoza-no-Ōkami|熊野坐大神}} – Associated with the Kumano faith
== Access == * Located in [[:en:Kainan, Wakayama|Kainan, Wakayama]], accessible via local transportation routes.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:橘本神社]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11544292]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:34, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11544292]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 04:14, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building | 名称=橘本神社 | 画像=[[File:Kitsumoto Shrine Entrance.jpg|250px]] | 所在地=[[和歌山県]][[海南市]]下津町橘本779 | 位置={{Coord|34|7|27.6|N|135|11|44.7|E|region:JP-30_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | 祭神=[[田道間守]]、熊野坐大神 | 例祭=[[10月10日]] | 地図 = {{Location map|Japan Wakayama Prefecture|label=橘本神社|float=center|relief=1|width=256}} | image=File:Kitsumoto Shrine Entrance.jpg | prefecture={{ill|Kainan|WD=Q841309}} | coordinates={{Coord|34.12433333333333|N|135.19575|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} }} '''Kitsumoto Shrine (橘本神社)''' is one of the {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|fr|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=99 ōji Shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. It is a Shinto shrine located in [[:en:Kainan, Wakayama|Kainan, Wakayama]], [[:en:Wakayama Prefecture|Wakayama Prefecture]], Japan. The shrine enshrines {{nihongo|[[:en:Tajimamori|Tajimamori]]|田道間守}} and {{nihongo|Kumanoza-no-Ōkami|熊野坐大神}}, and is revered as the deity of confectionery. It is designated as a {{ill|Prefectural Historic Site|ja|和歌山県指定文化財一覧|1=List of Wakayama Prefecture designated cultural properties|2=ja|3=和歌山県指定文化財一覧|lt=Prefectural Historic Site|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prefectural Historic Site|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11417412}} (since April 9, 1974), as the former site of {{nihongo|Tokosaka Ōji|所坂王子}}, one of the {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}} shrines.<ref name="Kinen">{{Cite web|title=Prefectural Cultural Properties: Historic Sites|url=https://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/prefg/500700/mokuroku/kenkinenbutsu.html|publisher=Wakayama Prefecture Board of Education|accessdate=2021-07-25}}</ref>
== History == According to the ''Kojiki'' and ''Nihon Shoki'', Emperor Suinin ordered Tajimamori to find the legendary fruit of eternal youth. After enduring over ten years of hardship, he returned from the mythical {{nihongo|Tokoyo no Kuni|常世の国}} (often identified with ancient China) with the fruit known as {{nihongo|Tokijiku no Kagunokonomi|非時香菓}}, identified as the {{nihongo|tachibana|橘}}, an ancient citrus fruit. Unfortunately, the emperor had already passed away. In despair, Tajimamori offered the tachibana at the emperor’s tomb and died shortly after.<ref name="Mikan">{{Cite web|title=Mikan Festival|url=http://www.d1.dion.ne.jp/~konchi/kitumoto/menu/mikanmaturi.htm|publisher=Kitsumoto Shrine|accessdate=2015-02-14}}</ref><ref name="Kainanshi">{{Cite web|title=Kitsumoto Shrine (Origin of Mikan, Deity of Confectionery)|url=https://www.city.kainan.lg.jp/kakubusho/machizukuribu/norinsuisangakari/michinoeki/1543927025145.html|publisher=Kainan City|date=2021-03-01|accessdate=2021-07-25}}</ref>
It is said that the tachibana was first planted in Japan at the "Hill of Six Trees," the former site of the shrine to the north. The tachibana is considered the ancestor of modern mandarins, and this region is known as the birthplace of mikan cultivation.<ref name="Mikan"/>
The shrine’s founding date is unknown, but a roof plaque dated 1437 (Eikyō 9) still exists.<ref name="Rekishi">{{Cite web|title=Shrine History|url=http://www.d1.dion.ne.jp/~konchi/kitumoto/menu/framepage.htm|publisher=Kitsumoto Shrine|accessdate=2015-02-14}}</ref><ref name="Nikkeii">{{Cite web|title=Why Is the "Confectionery Deity" in Wakayama? (Mystery Cruise)|url=https://www.nikkei.com/article/DGXLASHD15H02_V10C14A8AA1P00/|publisher=Nikkei|date=2014-08-20|accessdate=2021-07-25}}</ref>
In 1158 (Hōgen 3), Emperor Shirakawa reportedly stayed at the shrine during his pilgrimage to Kumano and composed the poem: ''"Spending a night under the tachibana tree, I gaze at the moon over Mt. Irisa."''<ref name="Rekishi"/>
During the Edo period, Kishu Domain lord {{nihongo|Tokugawa Mitsusada|徳川光貞}} ordered the repair of the shrine in 1687 (Jōkyō 4). However, it later fell into decline, leaving only an old tachibana tree and a small shrine by the early Meiji era. Thanks to local efforts, the shrine was restored, and in November 1907, it was relocated to its current site on the former grounds of Tokosaka Ōji. At this time, two other local Ōji shrines—{{nihongo|Tōge-shita Ōji|搭下王子}} and {{nihongo|Kitsumoto Ōji|橘本王子}}—were also enshrined here.<ref name="Rekishi"/><ref name="Nikkeii"/>
== Festivals == The region is famous for mikan production, and the annual festival on October 10 is known as the ''Mikan Festival,'' commemorating Tajimamori’s introduction of citrus to Japan.<ref name="Mikan"/><ref name="Kainanshi"/>
Additionally, the ''Confectionery Festival'' (''Zenkoku Meika Hōkensai'') is held on the first Sunday of April. This event honors Tajimamori as the deity of sweets, as mikan were historically regarded as confections. Confectioners from across Japan offer their specialty sweets in prayer for prosperity.<ref>{{Cite web|title=Confectionery Festival|url=http://www.d1.dion.ne.jp/~konchi/kitumoto/menu/kashimaturi.htm|publisher=Kitsumoto Shrine|accessdate=2015-02-14}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|title=Kitsumoto Shrine’s Confectionery Festival|url=https://www.city.kainan.lg.jp/kanko/matsuri/1543928726429.html|publisher=Kainan City|date=2021-03-18|accessdate=2021-07-25}}</ref>
== Deities Enshrined == * {{nihongo|Tajimamori|田道間守}} – Deity of sweets and mikan cultivation * {{nihongo|Kumanoza-no-Ōkami|熊野坐大神}} – Associated with the Kumano faith
== Access == * Located in [[:en:Kainan, Wakayama|Kainan, Wakayama]], accessible via local transportation routes.
