{{Infobox religious building | 名称=橘本神社 | 画像=[[File:Kitsumoto Shrine Entrance.jpg|250px]] | 所在地=[[和歌山県]][[海南市]]下津町橘本779 | 位置={{Coord|34|7|27.6|N|135|11|44.7|E|region:JP-30_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | 祭神=[[田道間守]]、熊野坐大神 | 例祭=[[10月10日]] | 地図 = {{Location map|Japan Wakayama Prefecture|label=橘本神社|float=center|relief=1|width=256}} | image=File:Kitsumoto Shrine Entrance.jpg | prefecture={{ill|Kainan|WD=Q841309}} | coordinates={{Coord|34.12433333333333|N|135.19575|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} }} '''Kitsumoto Shrine (橘本神社)''' is one of the {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|fr|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=99 ōji Shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. It is a Shinto shrine located in {{ill|Kainan, Wakayama|en|Kainan, Wakayama|WD=Q841309|lt=Kainan, Wakayama}}, {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|en|Wakayama Prefecture|WD=Q131314|lt=Wakayama Prefecture}}, Japan. The shrine enshrines {{nihongo|{{ill|Tajimamori|en|Tajimamori|WD=Q11316133|lt=Tajimamori}}|田道間守}} and {{nihongo|Kumanoza-no-Ōkami|熊野坐大神}}, and is revered as the deity of confectionery. It is designated as a {{ill|Prefectural Historic Site|ja|和歌山県指定文化財一覧|1=List of Wakayama Prefecture designated cultural properties|2=ja|3=和歌山県指定文化財一覧|lt=Prefectural Historic Site|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prefectural Historic Site|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11417412}} (since April 9, 1974), as the former site of {{nihongo|Tokosaka Ōji|所坂王子}}, one of the {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}} shrines.<ref name="Kinen">{{Cite web|title=Prefectural Cultural Properties: Historic Sites|url=https://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/prefg/500700/mokuroku/kenkinenbutsu.html|publisher=Wakayama Prefecture Board of Education|accessdate=2021-07-25}}</ref>
== History == According to the ''Kojiki'' and ''Nihon Shoki'', Emperor Suinin ordered Tajimamori to find the legendary fruit of eternal youth. After enduring over ten years of hardship, he returned from the mythical {{nihongo|Tokoyo no Kuni|常世の国}} (often identified with ancient China) with the fruit known as {{nihongo|Tokijiku no Kagunokonomi|非時香菓}}, identified as the {{nihongo|tachibana|橘}}, an ancient citrus fruit. Unfortunately, the emperor had already passed away. In despair, Tajimamori offered the tachibana at the emperor’s tomb and died shortly after.<ref name="Mikan">{{Cite web|title=Mikan Festival|url=http://www.d1.dion.ne.jp/~konchi/kitumoto/menu/mikanmaturi.htm|publisher=Kitsumoto Shrine|accessdate=2015-02-14}}</ref><ref name="Kainanshi">{{Cite web|title=Kitsumoto Shrine (Origin of Mikan, Deity of Confectionery)|url=https://www.city.kainan.lg.jp/kakubusho/machizukuribu/norinsuisangakari/michinoeki/1543927025145.html|publisher=Kainan City|date=2021-03-01|accessdate=2021-07-25}}</ref>
It is said that the tachibana was first planted in Japan at the "Hill of Six Trees," the former site of the shrine to the north. The tachibana is considered the ancestor of modern mandarins, and this region is known as the birthplace of mikan cultivation.<ref name="Mikan"/>
The shrine’s founding date is unknown, but a roof plaque dated 1437 (Eikyō 9) still exists.<ref name="Rekishi">{{Cite web|title=Shrine History|url=http://www.d1.dion.ne.jp/~konchi/kitumoto/menu/framepage.htm|publisher=Kitsumoto Shrine|accessdate=2015-02-14}}</ref><ref name="Nikkeii">{{Cite web|title=Why Is the "Confectionery Deity" in Wakayama? (Mystery Cruise)|url=https://www.nikkei.com/article/DGXLASHD15H02_V10C14A8AA1P00/|publisher=Nikkei|date=2014-08-20|accessdate=2021-07-25}}</ref>
In 1158 (Hōgen 3), Emperor Shirakawa reportedly stayed at the shrine during his pilgrimage to Kumano and composed the poem: ''"Spending a night under the tachibana tree, I gaze at the moon over Mt. Irisa."''<ref name="Rekishi"/>
During the Edo period, Kishu Domain lord {{nihongo|Tokugawa Mitsusada|徳川光貞}} ordered the repair of the shrine in 1687 (Jōkyō 4). However, it later fell into decline, leaving only an old tachibana tree and a small shrine by the early Meiji era. Thanks to local efforts, the shrine was restored, and in November 1907, it was relocated to its current site on the former grounds of Tokosaka Ōji. At this time, two other local Ōji shrines—{{nihongo|Tōge-shita Ōji|搭下王子}} and {{nihongo|Kitsumoto Ōji|橘本王子}}—were also enshrined here.<ref name="Rekishi"/><ref name="Nikkeii"/>
== Festivals == The region is famous for mikan production, and the annual festival on October 10 is known as the ''Mikan Festival,'' commemorating Tajimamori’s introduction of citrus to Japan.<ref name="Mikan"/><ref name="Kainanshi"/>
Additionally, the ''Confectionery Festival'' (''Zenkoku Meika Hōkensai'') is held on the first Sunday of April. This event honors Tajimamori as the deity of sweets, as mikan were historically regarded as confections. Confectioners from across Japan offer their specialty sweets in prayer for prosperity.<ref>{{Cite web|title=Confectionery Festival|url=http://www.d1.dion.ne.jp/~konchi/kitumoto/menu/kashimaturi.htm|publisher=Kitsumoto Shrine|accessdate=2015-02-14}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|title=Kitsumoto Shrine’s Confectionery Festival|url=https://www.city.kainan.lg.jp/kanko/matsuri/1543928726429.html|publisher=Kainan City|date=2021-03-18|accessdate=2021-07-25}}</ref>
== Deities Enshrined == * {{nihongo|Tajimamori|田道間守}} – Deity of sweets and mikan cultivation * {{nihongo|Kumanoza-no-Ōkami|熊野坐大神}} – Associated with the Kumano faith
== Access == * Located in {{ill|Kainan, Wakayama|en|Kainan, Wakayama|WD=Q841309|lt=Kainan, Wakayama}}, accessible via local transportation routes.
== Gallery == <gallery> File:Kitsumoto Shrine Entrance.jpg|Entrance to Kitsumoto Shrine </gallery>
== See Also == * {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines (Kainan)|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines (海南市)|1=List of Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines (Kainan City)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines (Kainan)|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}}

{{Infobox religious building | 名称=橘本神社 | 画像=[[File:Kitsumoto Shrine Entrance.jpg|250px]] | 所在地=[[和歌山県]][[海南市]]下津町橘本779 | 位置={{Coord|34|7|27.6|N|135|11|44.7|E|region:JP-30_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | 祭神=[[田道間守]]、熊野坐大神 | 例祭=[[10月10日]] | 地図 = {{Location map|Japan Wakayama Prefecture|label=橘本神社|float=center|relief=1|width=256}} | image=File:Kitsumoto Shrine Entrance.jpg | prefecture={{ill|Kainan|WD=Q841309}} | coordinates={{Coord|34.12433333333333|N|135.19575|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} }} '''Kitsumoto Shrine (橘本神社)''' is one of the {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|fr|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=99 ōji Shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. It is a Shinto shrine located in {{ill|Kainan, Wakayama|en|Kainan, Wakayama|WD=Q841309|lt=Kainan, Wakayama}}, {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|en|Wakayama Prefecture|WD=Q131314|lt=Wakayama Prefecture}}, Japan. The shrine enshrines {{nihongo|{{ill|Tajimamori|en|Tajimamori|WD=Q11316133|lt=Tajimamori}}|田道間守}} and {{nihongo|Kumanoza-no-Ōkami|熊野坐大神}}, and is revered as the deity of confectionery. It is designated as a {{ill|Prefectural Historic Site|ja|和歌山県指定文化財一覧|1=List of Wakayama Prefecture designated cultural properties|2=ja|3=和歌山県指定文化財一覧|lt=Prefectural Historic Site|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prefectural Historic Site|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11417412}} (since April 9, 1974), as the former site of {{nihongo|Tokosaka Ōji|所坂王子}}, one of the {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}} shrines.<ref name="Kinen">{{Cite web|title=Prefectural Cultural Properties: Historic Sites|url=https://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/prefg/500700/mokuroku/kenkinenbutsu.html|publisher=Wakayama Prefecture Board of Education|accessdate=2021-07-25}}</ref>
== History == According to the ''Kojiki'' and ''Nihon Shoki'', Emperor Suinin ordered Tajimamori to find the legendary fruit of eternal youth. After enduring over ten years of hardship, he returned from the mythical {{nihongo|Tokoyo no Kuni|常世の国}} (often identified with ancient China) with the fruit known as {{nihongo|Tokijiku no Kagunokonomi|非時香菓}}, identified as the {{nihongo|tachibana|橘}}, an ancient citrus fruit. Unfortunately, the emperor had already passed away. In despair, Tajimamori offered the tachibana at the emperor’s tomb and died shortly after.<ref name="Mikan">{{Cite web|title=Mikan Festival|url=http://www.d1.dion.ne.jp/~konchi/kitumoto/menu/mikanmaturi.htm|publisher=Kitsumoto Shrine|accessdate=2015-02-14}}</ref><ref name="Kainanshi">{{Cite web|title=Kitsumoto Shrine (Origin of Mikan, Deity of Confectionery)|url=https://www.city.kainan.lg.jp/kakubusho/machizukuribu/norinsuisangakari/michinoeki/1543927025145.html|publisher=Kainan City|date=2021-03-01|accessdate=2021-07-25}}</ref>
It is said that the tachibana was first planted in Japan at the "Hill of Six Trees," the former site of the shrine to the north. The tachibana is considered the ancestor of modern mandarins, and this region is known as the birthplace of mikan cultivation.<ref name="Mikan"/>
The shrine’s founding date is unknown, but a roof plaque dated 1437 (Eikyō 9) still exists.<ref name="Rekishi">{{Cite web|title=Shrine History|url=http://www.d1.dion.ne.jp/~konchi/kitumoto/menu/framepage.htm|publisher=Kitsumoto Shrine|accessdate=2015-02-14}}</ref><ref name="Nikkeii">{{Cite web|title=Why Is the "Confectionery Deity" in Wakayama? (Mystery Cruise)|url=https://www.nikkei.com/article/DGXLASHD15H02_V10C14A8AA1P00/|publisher=Nikkei|date=2014-08-20|accessdate=2021-07-25}}</ref>
In 1158 (Hōgen 3), Emperor Shirakawa reportedly stayed at the shrine during his pilgrimage to Kumano and composed the poem: ''"Spending a night under the tachibana tree, I gaze at the moon over Mt. Irisa."''<ref name="Rekishi"/>
During the Edo period, Kishu Domain lord {{nihongo|Tokugawa Mitsusada|徳川光貞}} ordered the repair of the shrine in 1687 (Jōkyō 4). However, it later fell into decline, leaving only an old tachibana tree and a small shrine by the early Meiji era. Thanks to local efforts, the shrine was restored, and in November 1907, it was relocated to its current site on the former grounds of Tokosaka Ōji. At this time, two other local Ōji shrines—{{nihongo|Tōge-shita Ōji|搭下王子}} and {{nihongo|Kitsumoto Ōji|橘本王子}}—were also enshrined here.<ref name="Rekishi"/><ref name="Nikkeii"/>
== Festivals == The region is famous for mikan production, and the annual festival on October 10 is known as the ''Mikan Festival,'' commemorating Tajimamori’s introduction of citrus to Japan.<ref name="Mikan"/><ref name="Kainanshi"/>
Additionally, the ''Confectionery Festival'' (''Zenkoku Meika Hōkensai'') is held on the first Sunday of April. This event honors Tajimamori as the deity of sweets, as mikan were historically regarded as confections. Confectioners from across Japan offer their specialty sweets in prayer for prosperity.<ref>{{Cite web|title=Confectionery Festival|url=http://www.d1.dion.ne.jp/~konchi/kitumoto/menu/kashimaturi.htm|publisher=Kitsumoto Shrine|accessdate=2015-02-14}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|title=Kitsumoto Shrine’s Confectionery Festival|url=https://www.city.kainan.lg.jp/kanko/matsuri/1543928726429.html|publisher=Kainan City|date=2021-03-18|accessdate=2021-07-25}}</ref>
== Deities Enshrined == * {{nihongo|Tajimamori|田道間守}} – Deity of sweets and mikan cultivation * {{nihongo|Kumanoza-no-Ōkami|熊野坐大神}} – Associated with the Kumano faith
== Access == * Located in {{ill|Kainan, Wakayama|en|Kainan, Wakayama|WD=Q841309|lt=Kainan, Wakayama}}, accessible via local transportation routes.
== Gallery == <gallery> File:Kitsumoto Shrine Entrance.jpg|Entrance to Kitsumoto Shrine </gallery>
== See Also == * {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines (Kainan)|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines (海南市)|1=List of Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines (Kainan City)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines (Kainan)|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}}

{{Infobox religious building | 名称=橘本神社 | 画像=[[File:Kitsumoto Shrine Entrance.jpg|250px]] | 所在地=[[和歌山県]][[海南市]]下津町橘本779 | 位置={{Coord|34|7|27.6|N|135|11|44.7|E|region:JP-30_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | 祭神=[[田道間守]]、熊野坐大神 | 例祭=[[10月10日]] | 地図 = {{Location map|Japan Wakayama Prefecture|label=橘本神社|float=center|relief=1|width=256}} | image=File:Kitsumoto Shrine Entrance.jpg | prefecture={{ill|Kainan|WD=Q841309}} | coordinates={{Coord|34.12433333333333|N|135.19575|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} }} '''Kitsumoto Shrine (橘本神社)''' is one of the {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|fr|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=99 ōji Shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. It is a Shinto shrine located in {{ill|Kainan, Wakayama|en|Kainan, Wakayama|WD=Q841309|lt=Kainan, Wakayama}}, {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|en|Wakayama Prefecture|WD=Q131314|lt=Wakayama Prefecture}}, Japan. The shrine enshrines {{nihongo|{{ill|Tajimamori|en|Tajimamori|WD=Q11316133|lt=Tajimamori}}|田道間守}} and {{nihongo|Kumanoza-no-Ōkami|熊野坐大神}}, and is revered as the deity of confectionery. It is designated as a {{ill|Prefectural Historic Site|ja|和歌山県指定文化財一覧|1=List of Wakayama Prefecture designated cultural properties|2=ja|3=和歌山県指定文化財一覧|lt=Prefectural Historic Site|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prefectural Historic Site|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11417412}} (since April 9, 1974), as the former site of {{nihongo|Tokosaka Ōji|所坂王子}}, one of the {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}} shrines.<ref name="Kinen">{{Cite web|title=Prefectural Cultural Properties: Historic Sites|url=https://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/prefg/500700/mokuroku/kenkinenbutsu.html|publisher=Wakayama Prefecture Board of Education|accessdate=2021-07-25}}</ref>
== History == According to the ''Kojiki'' and ''Nihon Shoki'', Emperor Suinin ordered Tajimamori to find the legendary fruit of eternal youth. After enduring over ten years of hardship, he returned from the mythical {{nihongo|Tokoyo no Kuni|常世の国}} (often identified with ancient China) with the fruit known as {{nihongo|Tokijiku no Kagunokonomi|非時香菓}}, identified as the {{nihongo|tachibana|橘}}, an ancient citrus fruit. Unfortunately, the emperor had already passed away. In despair, Tajimamori offered the tachibana at the emperor’s tomb and died shortly after.<ref name="Mikan">{{Cite web|title=Mikan Festival|url=http://www.d1.dion.ne.jp/~konchi/kitumoto/menu/mikanmaturi.htm|publisher=Kitsumoto Shrine|accessdate=2015-02-14}}</ref><ref name="Kainanshi">{{Cite web|title=Kitsumoto Shrine (Origin of Mikan, Deity of Confectionery)|url=https://www.city.kainan.lg.jp/kakubusho/machizukuribu/norinsuisangakari/michinoeki/1543927025145.html|publisher=Kainan City|date=2021-03-01|accessdate=2021-07-25}}</ref>
It is said that the tachibana was first planted in Japan at the "Hill of Six Trees," the former site of the shrine to the north. The tachibana is considered the ancestor of modern mandarins, and this region is known as the birthplace of mikan cultivation.<ref name="Mikan"/>
The shrine’s founding date is unknown, but a roof plaque dated 1437 (Eikyō 9) still exists.<ref name="Rekishi">{{Cite web|title=Shrine History|url=http://www.d1.dion.ne.jp/~konchi/kitumoto/menu/framepage.htm|publisher=Kitsumoto Shrine|accessdate=2015-02-14}}</ref><ref name="Nikkeii">{{Cite web|title=Why Is the "Confectionery Deity" in Wakayama? (Mystery Cruise)|url=https://www.nikkei.com/article/DGXLASHD15H02_V10C14A8AA1P00/|publisher=Nikkei|date=2014-08-20|accessdate=2021-07-25}}</ref>
In 1158 (Hōgen 3), Emperor Shirakawa reportedly stayed at the shrine during his pilgrimage to Kumano and composed the poem: ''"Spending a night under the tachibana tree, I gaze at the moon over Mt. Irisa."''<ref name="Rekishi"/>
During the Edo period, Kishu Domain lord {{nihongo|Tokugawa Mitsusada|徳川光貞}} ordered the repair of the shrine in 1687 (Jōkyō 4). However, it later fell into decline, leaving only an old tachibana tree and a small shrine by the early Meiji era. Thanks to local efforts, the shrine was restored, and in November 1907, it was relocated to its current site on the former grounds of Tokosaka Ōji. At this time, two other local Ōji shrines—{{nihongo|Tōge-shita Ōji|搭下王子}} and {{nihongo|Kitsumoto Ōji|橘本王子}}—were also enshrined here.<ref name="Rekishi"/><ref name="Nikkeii"/>
== Festivals == The region is famous for mikan production, and the annual festival on October 10 is known as the ''Mikan Festival,'' commemorating Tajimamori’s introduction of citrus to Japan.<ref name="Mikan"/><ref name="Kainanshi"/>
Additionally, the ''Confectionery Festival'' (''Zenkoku Meika Hōkensai'') is held on the first Sunday of April. This event honors Tajimamori as the deity of sweets, as mikan were historically regarded as confections. Confectioners from across Japan offer their specialty sweets in prayer for prosperity.<ref>{{Cite web|title=Confectionery Festival|url=http://www.d1.dion.ne.jp/~konchi/kitumoto/menu/kashimaturi.htm|publisher=Kitsumoto Shrine|accessdate=2015-02-14}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|title=Kitsumoto Shrine’s Confectionery Festival|url=https://www.city.kainan.lg.jp/kanko/matsuri/1543928726429.html|publisher=Kainan City|date=2021-03-18|accessdate=2021-07-25}}</ref>
== Deities Enshrined == * {{nihongo|Tajimamori|田道間守}} – Deity of sweets and mikan cultivation * {{nihongo|Kumanoza-no-Ōkami|熊野坐大神}} – Associated with the Kumano faith
== Access == * Located in {{ill|Kainan, Wakayama|en|Kainan, Wakayama|WD=Q841309|lt=Kainan, Wakayama}}, accessible via local transportation routes.
== Gallery == <gallery> File:Kitsumoto Shrine Entrance.jpg|Entrance to Kitsumoto Shrine </gallery>
== See Also == * {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines (Kainan)|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines (海南市)|1=List of Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines (Kainan City)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines (Kainan)|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}}

{{Infobox religious building | 名称=橘本神社 | 画像=[[File:Kitsumoto Shrine Entrance.jpg|250px]] | 所在地=[[和歌山県]][[海南市]]下津町橘本779 | 位置={{Coord|34|7|27.6|N|135|11|44.7|E|region:JP-30_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | 祭神=[[田道間守]]、熊野坐大神 | 例祭=[[10月10日]] | 地図 = {{Location map|Japan Wakayama Prefecture|label=橘本神社|float=center|relief=1|width=256}} | image=File:Kitsumoto Shrine Entrance.jpg | prefecture={{ill|Kainan|WD=Q841309}} | coordinates={{Coord|34.12433333333333|N|135.19575|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} }} '''Kitsumoto Shrine (橘本神社)''' is one of the {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|fr|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=99 ōji Shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. It is a Shinto shrine located in {{ill|Kainan, Wakayama|en|Kainan, Wakayama|WD=Q841309|lt=Kainan, Wakayama}}, {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|en|Wakayama Prefecture|WD=Q131314|lt=Wakayama Prefecture}}, Japan. The shrine enshrines {{nihongo|{{ill|Tajimamori|en|Tajimamori|WD=Q11316133|lt=Tajimamori}}|田道間守}} and {{nihongo|Kumanoza-no-Ōkami|熊野坐大神}}, and is revered as the deity of confectionery. It is designated as a {{ill|Prefectural Historic Site|ja|和歌山県指定文化財一覧|1=List of Wakayama Prefecture designated cultural properties|2=ja|3=和歌山県指定文化財一覧|lt=Prefectural Historic Site|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prefectural Historic Site|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11417412}} (since April 9, 1974), as the former site of {{nihongo|Tokosaka Ōji|所坂王子}}, one of the {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}} shrines.<ref name="Kinen">{{Cite web|title=Prefectural Cultural Properties: Historic Sites|url=https://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/prefg/500700/mokuroku/kenkinenbutsu.html|publisher=Wakayama Prefecture Board of Education|accessdate=2021-07-25}}</ref>
== History == According to the ''Kojiki'' and ''Nihon Shoki'', Emperor Suinin ordered Tajimamori to find the legendary fruit of eternal youth. After enduring over ten years of hardship, he returned from the mythical {{nihongo|Tokoyo no Kuni|常世の国}} (often identified with ancient China) with the fruit known as {{nihongo|Tokijiku no Kagunokonomi|非時香菓}}, identified as the {{nihongo|tachibana|橘}}, an ancient citrus fruit. Unfortunately, the emperor had already passed away. In despair, Tajimamori offered the tachibana at the emperor’s tomb and died shortly after.<ref name="Mikan">{{Cite web|title=Mikan Festival|url=http://www.d1.dion.ne.jp/~konchi/kitumoto/menu/mikanmaturi.htm|publisher=Kitsumoto Shrine|accessdate=2015-02-14}}</ref><ref name="Kainanshi">{{Cite web|title=Kitsumoto Shrine (Origin of Mikan, Deity of Confectionery)|url=https://www.city.kainan.lg.jp/kakubusho/machizukuribu/norinsuisangakari/michinoeki/1543927025145.html|publisher=Kainan City|date=2021-03-01|accessdate=2021-07-25}}</ref>
It is said that the tachibana was first planted in Japan at the "Hill of Six Trees," the former site of the shrine to the north. The tachibana is considered the ancestor of modern mandarins, and this region is known as the birthplace of mikan cultivation.<ref name="Mikan"/>
The shrine’s founding date is unknown, but a roof plaque dated 1437 (Eikyō 9) still exists.<ref name="Rekishi">{{Cite web|title=Shrine History|url=http://www.d1.dion.ne.jp/~konchi/kitumoto/menu/framepage.htm|publisher=Kitsumoto Shrine|accessdate=2015-02-14}}</ref><ref name="Nikkeii">{{Cite web|title=Why Is the "Confectionery Deity" in Wakayama? (Mystery Cruise)|url=https://www.nikkei.com/article/DGXLASHD15H02_V10C14A8AA1P00/|publisher=Nikkei|date=2014-08-20|accessdate=2021-07-25}}</ref>
In 1158 (Hōgen 3), Emperor Shirakawa reportedly stayed at the shrine during his pilgrimage to Kumano and composed the poem: ''"Spending a night under the tachibana tree, I gaze at the moon over Mt. Irisa."''<ref name="Rekishi"/>
During the Edo period, Kishu Domain lord {{nihongo|Tokugawa Mitsusada|徳川光貞}} ordered the repair of the shrine in 1687 (Jōkyō 4). However, it later fell into decline, leaving only an old tachibana tree and a small shrine by the early Meiji era. Thanks to local efforts, the shrine was restored, and in November 1907, it was relocated to its current site on the former grounds of Tokosaka Ōji. At this time, two other local Ōji shrines—{{nihongo|Tōge-shita Ōji|搭下王子}} and {{nihongo|Kitsumoto Ōji|橘本王子}}—were also enshrined here.<ref name="Rekishi"/><ref name="Nikkeii"/>
== Festivals == The region is famous for mikan production, and the annual festival on October 10 is known as the ''Mikan Festival,'' commemorating Tajimamori’s introduction of citrus to Japan.<ref name="Mikan"/><ref name="Kainanshi"/>
Additionally, the ''Confectionery Festival'' (''Zenkoku Meika Hōkensai'') is held on the first Sunday of April. This event honors Tajimamori as the deity of sweets, as mikan were historically regarded as confections. Confectioners from across Japan offer their specialty sweets in prayer for prosperity.<ref>{{Cite web|title=Confectionery Festival|url=http://www.d1.dion.ne.jp/~konchi/kitumoto/menu/kashimaturi.htm|publisher=Kitsumoto Shrine|accessdate=2015-02-14}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|title=Kitsumoto Shrine’s Confectionery Festival|url=https://www.city.kainan.lg.jp/kanko/matsuri/1543928726429.html|publisher=Kainan City|date=2021-03-18|accessdate=2021-07-25}}</ref>
== Deities Enshrined == * {{nihongo|Tajimamori|田道間守}} – Deity of sweets and mikan cultivation * {{nihongo|Kumanoza-no-Ōkami|熊野坐大神}} – Associated with the Kumano faith
== Access == * Located in {{ill|Kainan, Wakayama|en|Kainan, Wakayama|WD=Q841309|lt=Kainan, Wakayama}}, accessible via local transportation routes.
== Gallery == <gallery> File:Kitsumoto Shrine Entrance.jpg|Entrance to Kitsumoto Shrine </gallery>
== See Also == * {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines (Kainan)|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines (海南市)|1=List of Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines (Kainan City)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines (Kainan)|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}}

{{Infobox religious building | 名称=橘本神社 | 画像=[[File:Kitsumoto Shrine Entrance.jpg|250px]] | 所在地=[[和歌山県]][[海南市]]下津町橘本779 | 位置={{Coord|34|7|27.6|N|135|11|44.7|E|region:JP-30_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | 祭神=[[田道間守]]、熊野坐大神 | 例祭=[[10月10日]] | 地図 = {{Location map|Japan Wakayama Prefecture|label=橘本神社|float=center|relief=1|width=256}} | image=File:Kitsumoto Shrine Entrance.jpg | prefecture={{ill|Kainan|WD=Q841309|1=Kainan, Wakayama}} | coordinates={{Coord|34.12433333333333|N|135.19575|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} }} '''Kitsumoto Shrine (橘本神社)''' is one of the {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|fr|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=99 ōji Shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. It is a Shinto shrine located in {{ill|Kainan, Wakayama|en|Kainan, Wakayama|WD=Q841309|lt=Kainan, Wakayama|1=Kainan, Wakayama}}, {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|en|Wakayama Prefecture|WD=Q131314|lt=Wakayama Prefecture|1=Wakayama Prefecture}}, Japan. The shrine enshrines {{nihongo|{{ill|Tajimamori|en|Tajimamori|WD=Q11316133|lt=Tajimamori|1=Tajimamori}}|田道間守}} and {{nihongo|Kumanoza-no-Ōkami|熊野坐大神}}, and is revered as the deity of confectionery. It is designated as a {{ill|Prefectural Historic Site|ja|和歌山県指定文化財一覧|1=list of Wakayama Prefecture designated cultural properties|2=ja|3=和歌山県指定文化財一覧|lt=Prefectural Historic Site|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prefectural Historic Site|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11417412}} (since April 9, 1974), as the former site of {{nihongo|Tokosaka Ōji|所坂王子}}, one of the {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}} shrines.<ref name="Kinen">{{Cite web|title=Prefectural Cultural Properties: Historic Sites|url=https://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/prefg/500700/mokuroku/kenkinenbutsu.html|publisher=Wakayama Prefecture Board of Education|accessdate=2021-07-25}}</ref>
== History == According to the ''Kojiki'' and ''Nihon Shoki'', Emperor Suinin ordered Tajimamori to find the legendary fruit of eternal youth. After enduring over ten years of hardship, he returned from the mythical {{nihongo|Tokoyo no Kuni|常世の国}} (often identified with ancient China) with the fruit known as {{nihongo|Tokijiku no Kagunokonomi|非時香菓}}, identified as the {{nihongo|tachibana|橘}}, an ancient citrus fruit. Unfortunately, the emperor had already passed away. In despair, Tajimamori offered the tachibana at the emperor’s tomb and died shortly after.<ref name="Mikan">{{Cite web|title=Mikan Festival|url=http://www.d1.dion.ne.jp/~konchi/kitumoto/menu/mikanmaturi.htm|publisher=Kitsumoto Shrine|accessdate=2015-02-14}}</ref><ref name="Kainanshi">{{Cite web|title=Kitsumoto Shrine (Origin of Mikan, Deity of Confectionery)|url=https://www.city.kainan.lg.jp/kakubusho/machizukuribu/norinsuisangakari/michinoeki/1543927025145.html|publisher=Kainan City|date=2021-03-01|accessdate=2021-07-25}}</ref>
It is said that the tachibana was first planted in Japan at the "Hill of Six Trees," the former site of the shrine to the north. The tachibana is considered the ancestor of modern mandarins, and this region is known as the birthplace of mikan cultivation.<ref name="Mikan"/>
The shrine’s founding date is unknown, but a roof plaque dated 1437 (Eikyō 9) still exists.<ref name="Rekishi">{{Cite web|title=Shrine History|url=http://www.d1.dion.ne.jp/~konchi/kitumoto/menu/framepage.htm|publisher=Kitsumoto Shrine|accessdate=2015-02-14}}</ref><ref name="Nikkeii">{{Cite web|title=Why Is the "Confectionery Deity" in Wakayama? (Mystery Cruise)|url=https://www.nikkei.com/article/DGXLASHD15H02_V10C14A8AA1P00/|publisher=Nikkei|date=2014-08-20|accessdate=2021-07-25}}</ref>
In 1158 (Hōgen 3), Emperor Shirakawa reportedly stayed at the shrine during his pilgrimage to Kumano and composed the poem: ''"Spending a night under the tachibana tree, I gaze at the moon over Mt. Irisa."''<ref name="Rekishi"/>
During the Edo period, Kishu Domain lord {{nihongo|Tokugawa Mitsusada|徳川光貞}} ordered the repair of the shrine in 1687 (Jōkyō 4). However, it later fell into decline, leaving only an old tachibana tree and a small shrine by the early Meiji era. Thanks to local efforts, the shrine was restored, and in November 1907, it was relocated to its current site on the former grounds of Tokosaka Ōji. At this time, two other local Ōji shrines—{{nihongo|Tōge-shita Ōji|搭下王子}} and {{nihongo|Kitsumoto Ōji|橘本王子}}—were also enshrined here.<ref name="Rekishi"/><ref name="Nikkeii"/>
== Festivals == The region is famous for mikan production, and the annual festival on October 10 is known as the ''Mikan Festival,'' commemorating Tajimamori’s introduction of citrus to Japan.<ref name="Mikan"/><ref name="Kainanshi"/>
Additionally, the ''Confectionery Festival'' (''Zenkoku Meika Hōkensai'') is held on the first Sunday of April. This event honors Tajimamori as the deity of sweets, as mikan were historically regarded as confections. Confectioners from across Japan offer their specialty sweets in prayer for prosperity.<ref>{{Cite web|title=Confectionery Festival|url=http://www.d1.dion.ne.jp/~konchi/kitumoto/menu/kashimaturi.htm|publisher=Kitsumoto Shrine|accessdate=2015-02-14}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|title=Kitsumoto Shrine’s Confectionery Festival|url=https://www.city.kainan.lg.jp/kanko/matsuri/1543928726429.html|publisher=Kainan City|date=2021-03-18|accessdate=2021-07-25}}</ref>
== Deities Enshrined == * {{nihongo|Tajimamori|田道間守}} – Deity of sweets and mikan cultivation * {{nihongo|Kumanoza-no-Ōkami|熊野坐大神}} – Associated with the Kumano faith
== Access == * Located in {{ill|Kainan, Wakayama|en|Kainan, Wakayama|WD=Q841309|lt=Kainan, Wakayama|1=Kainan, Wakayama}}, accessible via local transportation routes.
== Gallery == <gallery> File:Kitsumoto Shrine Entrance.jpg|Entrance to Kitsumoto Shrine </gallery>
== See Also == * {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines (Kainan)|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines (海南市)|1=List of Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines (Kainan City)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines (Kainan)|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}}

{{Infobox religious building | 名称=橘本神社 | 画像=[[File:Kitsumoto Shrine Entrance.jpg|250px]] | 所在地=[[和歌山県]][[海南市]]下津町橘本779 | 位置={{Coord|34|7|27.6|N|135|11|44.7|E|region:JP-30_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | 祭神=[[田道間守]]、熊野坐大神 | 例祭=[[10月10日]] | 地図 = {{Location map|Japan Wakayama Prefecture|label=橘本神社|float=center|relief=1|width=256}} | image=File:Kitsumoto Shrine Entrance.jpg | prefecture={{ill|Kainan|WD=Q841309|1=Kainan, Wakayama}} | coordinates={{Coord|34.12433333333333|N|135.19575|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} }} '''Kitsumoto Shrine (橘本神社)''' is one of the {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|fr|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=99 ōji Shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. It is a Shinto shrine located in {{ill|Kainan, Wakayama|en|Kainan, Wakayama|WD=Q841309|lt=Kainan, Wakayama|1=Kainan, Wakayama}}, {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|en|Wakayama Prefecture|WD=Q131314|lt=Wakayama Prefecture|1=Wakayama Prefecture}}, Japan. The shrine enshrines {{nihongo|{{ill|Tajimamori|en|Tajimamori|WD=Q11316133|lt=Tajimamori|1=Tajimamori}}|田道間守}} and {{nihongo|Kumanoza-no-Ōkami|熊野坐大神}}, and is revered as the deity of confectionery. It is designated as a {{ill|Prefectural Historic Site|ja|和歌山県指定文化財一覧|1=list of Wakayama Prefecture designated cultural properties|2=ja|3=和歌山県指定文化財一覧|lt=Prefectural Historic Site|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prefectural Historic Site|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11417412}} (since April 9, 1974), as the former site of {{nihongo|Tokosaka Ōji|所坂王子}}, one of the {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}} shrines.<ref name="Kinen">{{Cite web|title=Prefectural Cultural Properties: Historic Sites|url=https://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/prefg/500700/mokuroku/kenkinenbutsu.html|publisher=Wakayama Prefecture Board of Education|accessdate=2021-07-25}}</ref>
== History == According to the ''Kojiki'' and ''Nihon Shoki'', Emperor Suinin ordered Tajimamori to find the legendary fruit of eternal youth. After enduring over ten years of hardship, he returned from the mythical {{nihongo|Tokoyo no Kuni|常世の国}} (often identified with ancient China) with the fruit known as {{nihongo|Tokijiku no Kagunokonomi|非時香菓}}, identified as the {{nihongo|tachibana|橘}}, an ancient citrus fruit. Unfortunately, the emperor had already passed away. In despair, Tajimamori offered the tachibana at the emperor’s tomb and died shortly after.<ref name="Mikan">{{Cite web|title=Mikan Festival|url=http://www.d1.dion.ne.jp/~konchi/kitumoto/menu/mikanmaturi.htm|publisher=Kitsumoto Shrine|accessdate=2015-02-14}}</ref><ref name="Kainanshi">{{Cite web|title=Kitsumoto Shrine (Origin of Mikan, Deity of Confectionery)|url=https://www.city.kainan.lg.jp/kakubusho/machizukuribu/norinsuisangakari/michinoeki/1543927025145.html|publisher=Kainan City|date=2021-03-01|accessdate=2021-07-25}}</ref>
It is said that the tachibana was first planted in Japan at the "Hill of Six Trees," the former site of the shrine to the north. The tachibana is considered the ancestor of modern mandarins, and this region is known as the birthplace of mikan cultivation.<ref name="Mikan"/>
The shrine’s founding date is unknown, but a roof plaque dated 1437 (Eikyō 9) still exists.<ref name="Rekishi">{{Cite web|title=Shrine History|url=http://www.d1.dion.ne.jp/~konchi/kitumoto/menu/framepage.htm|publisher=Kitsumoto Shrine|accessdate=2015-02-14}}</ref><ref name="Nikkeii">{{Cite web|title=Why Is the "Confectionery Deity" in Wakayama? (Mystery Cruise)|url=https://www.nikkei.com/article/DGXLASHD15H02_V10C14A8AA1P00/|publisher=Nikkei|date=2014-08-20|accessdate=2021-07-25}}</ref>
In 1158 (Hōgen 3), Emperor Shirakawa reportedly stayed at the shrine during his pilgrimage to Kumano and composed the poem: ''"Spending a night under the tachibana tree, I gaze at the moon over Mt. Irisa."''<ref name="Rekishi"/>
During the Edo period, Kishu Domain lord {{nihongo|Tokugawa Mitsusada|徳川光貞}} ordered the repair of the shrine in 1687 (Jōkyō 4). However, it later fell into decline, leaving only an old tachibana tree and a small shrine by the early Meiji era. Thanks to local efforts, the shrine was restored, and in November 1907, it was relocated to its current site on the former grounds of Tokosaka Ōji. At this time, two other local Ōji shrines—{{nihongo|Tōge-shita Ōji|搭下王子}} and {{nihongo|Kitsumoto Ōji|橘本王子}}—were also enshrined here.<ref name="Rekishi"/><ref name="Nikkeii"/>
== Festivals == The region is famous for mikan production, and the annual festival on October 10 is known as the ''Mikan Festival,'' commemorating Tajimamori’s introduction of citrus to Japan.<ref name="Mikan"/><ref name="Kainanshi"/>
Additionally, the ''Confectionery Festival'' (''Zenkoku Meika Hōkensai'') is held on the first Sunday of April. This event honors Tajimamori as the deity of sweets, as mikan were historically regarded as confections. Confectioners from across Japan offer their specialty sweets in prayer for prosperity.<ref>{{Cite web|title=Confectionery Festival|url=http://www.d1.dion.ne.jp/~konchi/kitumoto/menu/kashimaturi.htm|publisher=Kitsumoto Shrine|accessdate=2015-02-14}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|title=Kitsumoto Shrine’s Confectionery Festival|url=https://www.city.kainan.lg.jp/kanko/matsuri/1543928726429.html|publisher=Kainan City|date=2021-03-18|accessdate=2021-07-25}}</ref>
== Deities Enshrined == * {{nihongo|Tajimamori|田道間守}} – Deity of sweets and mikan cultivation * {{nihongo|Kumanoza-no-Ōkami|熊野坐大神}} – Associated with the Kumano faith
== Access == * Located in {{ill|Kainan, Wakayama|en|Kainan, Wakayama|WD=Q841309|lt=Kainan, Wakayama|1=Kainan, Wakayama}}, accessible via local transportation routes.
== Gallery == <gallery> File:Kitsumoto Shrine Entrance.jpg|Entrance to Kitsumoto Shrine </gallery>
== See Also == * {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines (Kainan)|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines (海南市)|1=List of Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines (Kainan City)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines (Kainan)|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}}

{{Infobox religious building | 名称=橘本神社 | 画像=[[File:Kitsumoto Shrine Entrance.jpg|250px]] | 所在地=[[和歌山県]][[海南市]]下津町橘本779 | 位置={{Coord|34|7|27.6|N|135|11|44.7|E|region:JP-30_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | 祭神=[[田道間守]]、熊野坐大神 | 例祭=[[10月10日]] | 地図 = {{Location map|Japan Wakayama Prefecture|label=橘本神社|float=center|relief=1|width=256}} | image=File:Kitsumoto Shrine Entrance.jpg | prefecture={{ill|Kainan|WD=Q841309|1=Kainan, Wakayama}} | coordinates={{Coord|34.12433333333333|N|135.19575|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} }} '''Kitsumoto Shrine (橘本神社)''' is one of the {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|fr|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=99 ōji Shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. It is a Shinto shrine located in {{ill|Kainan, Wakayama|en|Kainan, Wakayama|WD=Q841309|lt=Kainan, Wakayama|1=Kainan, Wakayama}}, {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|en|Wakayama Prefecture|WD=Q131314|lt=Wakayama Prefecture|1=Wakayama Prefecture}}, Japan. The shrine enshrines {{nihongo|{{ill|Tajimamori|en|Tajimamori|WD=Q11316133|lt=Tajimamori|1=Tajimamori}}|田道間守}} and {{nihongo|Kumanoza-no-Ōkami|熊野坐大神}}, and is revered as the deity of confectionery. It is designated as a {{ill|Prefectural Historic Site|ja|和歌山県指定文化財一覧|1=list of Wakayama Prefecture designated cultural properties|2=ja|3=和歌山県指定文化財一覧|lt=Prefectural Historic Site|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prefectural Historic Site|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11417412}} (since April 9, 1974), as the former site of {{nihongo|Tokosaka Ōji|所坂王子}}, one of the {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}} shrines.<ref name="Kinen">{{Cite web|title=Prefectural Cultural Properties: Historic Sites|url=https://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/prefg/500700/mokuroku/kenkinenbutsu.html|publisher=Wakayama Prefecture Board of Education|accessdate=2021-07-25}}</ref>
== History == According to the ''Kojiki'' and ''Nihon Shoki'', Emperor Suinin ordered Tajimamori to find the legendary fruit of eternal youth. After enduring over ten years of hardship, he returned from the mythical {{nihongo|Tokoyo no Kuni|常世の国}} (often identified with ancient China) with the fruit known as {{nihongo|Tokijiku no Kagunokonomi|非時香菓}}, identified as the {{nihongo|tachibana|橘}}, an ancient citrus fruit. Unfortunately, the emperor had already passed away. In despair, Tajimamori offered the tachibana at the emperor’s tomb and died shortly after.<ref name="Mikan">{{Cite web|title=Mikan Festival|url=http://www.d1.dion.ne.jp/~konchi/kitumoto/menu/mikanmaturi.htm|publisher=Kitsumoto Shrine|accessdate=2015-02-14}}</ref><ref name="Kainanshi">{{Cite web|title=Kitsumoto Shrine (Origin of Mikan, Deity of Confectionery)|url=https://www.city.kainan.lg.jp/kakubusho/machizukuribu/norinsuisangakari/michinoeki/1543927025145.html|publisher=Kainan City|date=2021-03-01|accessdate=2021-07-25}}</ref>
It is said that the tachibana was first planted in Japan at the "Hill of Six Trees," the former site of the shrine to the north. The tachibana is considered the ancestor of modern mandarins, and this region is known as the birthplace of mikan cultivation.<ref name="Mikan"/>
The shrine’s founding date is unknown, but a roof plaque dated 1437 (Eikyō 9) still exists.<ref name="Rekishi">{{Cite web|title=Shrine History|url=http://www.d1.dion.ne.jp/~konchi/kitumoto/menu/framepage.htm|publisher=Kitsumoto Shrine|accessdate=2015-02-14}}</ref><ref name="Nikkeii">{{Cite web|title=Why Is the "Confectionery Deity" in Wakayama? (Mystery Cruise)|url=https://www.nikkei.com/article/DGXLASHD15H02_V10C14A8AA1P00/|publisher=Nikkei|date=2014-08-20|accessdate=2021-07-25}}</ref>
In 1158 (Hōgen 3), Emperor Shirakawa reportedly stayed at the shrine during his pilgrimage to Kumano and composed the poem: ''"Spending a night under the tachibana tree, I gaze at the moon over Mt. Irisa."''<ref name="Rekishi"/>
During the Edo period, Kishu Domain lord {{nihongo|Tokugawa Mitsusada|徳川光貞}} ordered the repair of the shrine in 1687 (Jōkyō 4). However, it later fell into decline, leaving only an old tachibana tree and a small shrine by the early Meiji era. Thanks to local efforts, the shrine was restored, and in November 1907, it was relocated to its current site on the former grounds of Tokosaka Ōji. At this time, two other local Ōji shrines—{{nihongo|Tōge-shita Ōji|搭下王子}} and {{nihongo|Kitsumoto Ōji|橘本王子}}—were also enshrined here.<ref name="Rekishi"/><ref name="Nikkeii"/>
== Festivals == The region is famous for mikan production, and the annual festival on October 10 is known as the ''Mikan Festival,'' commemorating Tajimamori’s introduction of citrus to Japan.<ref name="Mikan"/><ref name="Kainanshi"/>
Additionally, the ''Confectionery Festival'' (''Zenkoku Meika Hōkensai'') is held on the first Sunday of April. This event honors Tajimamori as the deity of sweets, as mikan were historically regarded as confections. Confectioners from across Japan offer their specialty sweets in prayer for prosperity.<ref>{{Cite web|title=Confectionery Festival|url=http://www.d1.dion.ne.jp/~konchi/kitumoto/menu/kashimaturi.htm|publisher=Kitsumoto Shrine|accessdate=2015-02-14}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|title=Kitsumoto Shrine’s Confectionery Festival|url=https://www.city.kainan.lg.jp/kanko/matsuri/1543928726429.html|publisher=Kainan City|date=2021-03-18|accessdate=2021-07-25}}</ref>
== Deities Enshrined == * {{nihongo|Tajimamori|田道間守}} – Deity of sweets and mikan cultivation * {{nihongo|Kumanoza-no-Ōkami|熊野坐大神}} – Associated with the Kumano faith
== Access == * Located in {{ill|Kainan, Wakayama|en|Kainan, Wakayama|WD=Q841309|lt=Kainan, Wakayama|1=Kainan, Wakayama}}, accessible via local transportation routes.
== Gallery == <gallery> File:Kitsumoto Shrine Entrance.jpg|Entrance to Kitsumoto Shrine </gallery>
== See Also == * {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines (Kainan)|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines (海南市)|1=List of Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines (Kainan City)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines (Kainan)|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}}

{{Infobox religious building | 名称=橘本神社 | 画像=[[File:Kitsumoto Shrine Entrance.jpg|250px]] | 所在地=[[和歌山県]][[海南市]]下津町橘本779 | 位置={{Coord|34|7|27.6|N|135|11|44.7|E|region:JP-30_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | 祭神=[[田道間守]]、熊野坐大神 | 例祭=[[10月10日]] | 地図 = {{Location map|Japan Wakayama Prefecture|label=橘本神社|float=center|relief=1|width=256}} | image=File:Kitsumoto Shrine Entrance.jpg | prefecture={{ill|Kainan|WD=Q841309|1=Kainan, Wakayama}} | coordinates={{Coord|34.12433333333333|N|135.19575|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} }} '''Kitsumoto Shrine (橘本神社)''' is one of the {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|fr|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=99 ōji Shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. It is a Shinto shrine located in {{ill|Kainan, Wakayama|en|Kainan, Wakayama|WD=Q841309|lt=Kainan, Wakayama|1=Kainan, Wakayama}}, {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|en|Wakayama Prefecture|WD=Q131314|lt=Wakayama Prefecture|1=Wakayama Prefecture}}, Japan. The shrine enshrines {{nihongo|{{ill|Tajimamori|en|Tajimamori|WD=Q11316133|lt=Tajimamori|1=Tajimamori}}|田道間守}} and {{nihongo|Kumanoza-no-Ōkami|熊野坐大神}}, and is revered as the deity of confectionery. It is designated as a {{ill|Prefectural Historic Site|ja|和歌山県指定文化財一覧|1=list of Wakayama Prefecture designated cultural properties|2=ja|3=和歌山県指定文化財一覧|lt=Prefectural Historic Site|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prefectural Historic Site|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11417412}} (since April 9, 1974), as the former site of {{nihongo|Tokosaka Ōji|所坂王子}}, one of the {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}} shrines.<ref name="Kinen">{{Cite web|title=Prefectural Cultural Properties: Historic Sites|url=https://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/prefg/500700/mokuroku/kenkinenbutsu.html|publisher=Wakayama Prefecture Board of Education|accessdate=2021-07-25}}</ref>
== History == According to the ''Kojiki'' and ''Nihon Shoki'', Emperor Suinin ordered Tajimamori to find the legendary fruit of eternal youth. After enduring over ten years of hardship, he returned from the mythical {{nihongo|Tokoyo no Kuni|常世の国}} (often identified with ancient China) with the fruit known as {{nihongo|Tokijiku no Kagunokonomi|非時香菓}}, identified as the {{nihongo|tachibana|橘}}, an ancient citrus fruit. Unfortunately, the emperor had already passed away. In despair, Tajimamori offered the tachibana at the emperor’s tomb and died shortly after.<ref name="Mikan">{{Cite web|title=Mikan Festival|url=http://www.d1.dion.ne.jp/~konchi/kitumoto/menu/mikanmaturi.htm|publisher=Kitsumoto Shrine|accessdate=2015-02-14}}</ref><ref name="Kainanshi">{{Cite web|title=Kitsumoto Shrine (Origin of Mikan, Deity of Confectionery)|url=https://www.city.kainan.lg.jp/kakubusho/machizukuribu/norinsuisangakari/michinoeki/1543927025145.html|publisher=Kainan City|date=2021-03-01|accessdate=2021-07-25}}</ref>
It is said that the tachibana was first planted in Japan at the "Hill of Six Trees," the former site of the shrine to the north. The tachibana is considered the ancestor of modern mandarins, and this region is known as the birthplace of mikan cultivation.<ref name="Mikan"/>
The shrine’s founding date is unknown, but a roof plaque dated 1437 (Eikyō 9) still exists.<ref name="Rekishi">{{Cite web|title=Shrine History|url=http://www.d1.dion.ne.jp/~konchi/kitumoto/menu/framepage.htm|publisher=Kitsumoto Shrine|accessdate=2015-02-14}}</ref><ref name="Nikkeii">{{Cite web|title=Why Is the "Confectionery Deity" in Wakayama? (Mystery Cruise)|url=https://www.nikkei.com/article/DGXLASHD15H02_V10C14A8AA1P00/|publisher=Nikkei|date=2014-08-20|accessdate=2021-07-25}}</ref>
In 1158 (Hōgen 3), Emperor Shirakawa reportedly stayed at the shrine during his pilgrimage to Kumano and composed the poem: ''"Spending a night under the tachibana tree, I gaze at the moon over Mt. Irisa."''<ref name="Rekishi"/>
During the Edo period, Kishu Domain lord {{nihongo|Tokugawa Mitsusada|徳川光貞}} ordered the repair of the shrine in 1687 (Jōkyō 4). However, it later fell into decline, leaving only an old tachibana tree and a small shrine by the early Meiji era. Thanks to local efforts, the shrine was restored, and in November 1907, it was relocated to its current site on the former grounds of Tokosaka Ōji. At this time, two other local Ōji shrines—{{nihongo|Tōge-shita Ōji|搭下王子}} and {{nihongo|Kitsumoto Ōji|橘本王子}}—were also enshrined here.<ref name="Rekishi"/><ref name="Nikkeii"/>
== Festivals == The region is famous for mikan production, and the annual festival on October 10 is known as the ''Mikan Festival,'' commemorating Tajimamori’s introduction of citrus to Japan.<ref name="Mikan"/><ref name="Kainanshi"/>
Additionally, the ''Confectionery Festival'' (''Zenkoku Meika Hōkensai'') is held on the first Sunday of April. This event honors Tajimamori as the deity of sweets, as mikan were historically regarded as confections. Confectioners from across Japan offer their specialty sweets in prayer for prosperity.<ref>{{Cite web|title=Confectionery Festival|url=http://www.d1.dion.ne.jp/~konchi/kitumoto/menu/kashimaturi.htm|publisher=Kitsumoto Shrine|accessdate=2015-02-14}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|title=Kitsumoto Shrine’s Confectionery Festival|url=https://www.city.kainan.lg.jp/kanko/matsuri/1543928726429.html|publisher=Kainan City|date=2021-03-18|accessdate=2021-07-25}}</ref>
== Deities Enshrined == * {{nihongo|Tajimamori|田道間守}} – Deity of sweets and mikan cultivation * {{nihongo|Kumanoza-no-Ōkami|熊野坐大神}} – Associated with the Kumano faith
== Access == * Located in {{ill|Kainan, Wakayama|en|Kainan, Wakayama|WD=Q841309|lt=Kainan, Wakayama|1=Kainan, Wakayama}}, accessible via local transportation routes.
== Gallery == <gallery> File:Kitsumoto Shrine Entrance.jpg|Entrance to Kitsumoto Shrine </gallery>
== See Also == * {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines (Kainan)|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines (海南市)|1=List of Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines (Kainan City)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines (Kainan)|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}}

{{Infobox religious building | 名称=橘本神社 | 画像=[[File:Kitsumoto Shrine Entrance.jpg|250px]] | 所在地=[[和歌山県]][[海南市]]下津町橘本779 | 位置={{Coord|34|7|27.6|N|135|11|44.7|E|region:JP-30_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | 祭神=[[田道間守]]、熊野坐大神 | 例祭=[[10月10日]] | 地図 = {{Location map|Japan Wakayama Prefecture|label=橘本神社|float=center|relief=1|width=256}} | image=File:Kitsumoto Shrine Entrance.jpg | prefecture={{ill|Kainan|qid=Q841309|1=Kainan, Wakayama}} | coordinates={{Coord|34.12433333333333|N|135.19575|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} }} '''Kitsumoto Shrine (橘本神社)''' is one of the {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|fr|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=99 ōji Shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. It is a Shinto shrine located in {{ill|Kainan, Wakayama|en|Kainan, Wakayama|qid=Q841309|lt=Kainan, Wakayama|1=Kainan, Wakayama}}, {{ill|Wakayama Prefecture|en|Wakayama Prefecture|qid=Q131314|lt=Wakayama Prefecture|1=Wakayama Prefecture}}, Japan. The shrine enshrines {{nihongo|{{ill|Tajimamori|en|Tajimamori|qid=Q11316133|lt=Tajimamori|1=Tajimamori}}|田道間守}} and {{nihongo|Kumanoza-no-Ōkami|熊野坐大神}}, and is revered as the deity of confectionery. It is designated as a {{ill|Prefectural Historic Site|ja|和歌山県指定文化財一覧|1=list of Wakayama Prefecture designated cultural properties|2=ja|3=和歌山県指定文化財一覧|lt=Prefectural Historic Site|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prefectural Historic Site|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11417412}} (since April 9, 1974), as the former site of {{nihongo|Tokosaka Ōji|所坂王子}}, one of the {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}} shrines.<ref name="Kinen">{{Cite web|title=Prefectural Cultural Properties: Historic Sites|url=https://www.pref.wakayama.lg.jp/prefg/500700/mokuroku/kenkinenbutsu.html|publisher=Wakayama Prefecture Board of Education|accessdate=2021-07-25}}</ref>
== History == According to the ''Kojiki'' and ''Nihon Shoki'', Emperor Suinin ordered Tajimamori to find the legendary fruit of eternal youth. After enduring over ten years of hardship, he returned from the mythical {{nihongo|Tokoyo no Kuni|常世の国}} (often identified with ancient China) with the fruit known as {{nihongo|Tokijiku no Kagunokonomi|非時香菓}}, identified as the {{nihongo|tachibana|橘}}, an ancient citrus fruit. Unfortunately, the emperor had already passed away. In despair, Tajimamori offered the tachibana at the emperor’s tomb and died shortly after.<ref name="Mikan">{{Cite web|title=Mikan Festival|url=http://www.d1.dion.ne.jp/~konchi/kitumoto/menu/mikanmaturi.htm|publisher=Kitsumoto Shrine|accessdate=2015-02-14}}</ref><ref name="Kainanshi">{{Cite web|title=Kitsumoto Shrine (Origin of Mikan, Deity of Confectionery)|url=https://www.city.kainan.lg.jp/kakubusho/machizukuribu/norinsuisangakari/michinoeki/1543927025145.html|publisher=Kainan City|date=2021-03-01|accessdate=2021-07-25}}</ref>
It is said that the tachibana was first planted in Japan at the "Hill of Six Trees," the former site of the shrine to the north. The tachibana is considered the ancestor of modern mandarins, and this region is known as the birthplace of mikan cultivation.<ref name="Mikan"/>
The shrine’s founding date is unknown, but a roof plaque dated 1437 (Eikyō 9) still exists.<ref name="Rekishi">{{Cite web|title=Shrine History|url=http://www.d1.dion.ne.jp/~konchi/kitumoto/menu/framepage.htm|publisher=Kitsumoto Shrine|accessdate=2015-02-14}}</ref><ref name="Nikkeii">{{Cite web|title=Why Is the "Confectionery Deity" in Wakayama? (Mystery Cruise)|url=https://www.nikkei.com/article/DGXLASHD15H02_V10C14A8AA1P00/|publisher=Nikkei|date=2014-08-20|accessdate=2021-07-25}}</ref>
In 1158 (Hōgen 3), Emperor Shirakawa reportedly stayed at the shrine during his pilgrimage to Kumano and composed the poem: ''"Spending a night under the tachibana tree, I gaze at the moon over Mt. Irisa."''<ref name="Rekishi"/>
During the Edo period, Kishu Domain lord {{nihongo|Tokugawa Mitsusada|徳川光貞}} ordered the repair of the shrine in 1687 (Jōkyō 4). However, it later fell into decline, leaving only an old tachibana tree and a small shrine by the early Meiji era. Thanks to local efforts, the shrine was restored, and in November 1907, it was relocated to its current site on the former grounds of Tokosaka Ōji. At this time, two other local Ōji shrines—{{nihongo|Tōge-shita Ōji|搭下王子}} and {{nihongo|Kitsumoto Ōji|橘本王子}}—were also enshrined here.<ref name="Rekishi"/><ref name="Nikkeii"/>
== Festivals == The region is famous for mikan production, and the annual festival on October 10 is known as the ''Mikan Festival,'' commemorating Tajimamori’s introduction of citrus to Japan.<ref name="Mikan"/><ref name="Kainanshi"/>
Additionally, the ''Confectionery Festival'' (''Zenkoku Meika Hōkensai'') is held on the first Sunday of April. This event honors Tajimamori as the deity of sweets, as mikan were historically regarded as confections. Confectioners from across Japan offer their specialty sweets in prayer for prosperity.<ref>{{Cite web|title=Confectionery Festival|url=http://www.d1.dion.ne.jp/~konchi/kitumoto/menu/kashimaturi.htm|publisher=Kitsumoto Shrine|accessdate=2015-02-14}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|title=Kitsumoto Shrine’s Confectionery Festival|url=https://www.city.kainan.lg.jp/kanko/matsuri/1543928726429.html|publisher=Kainan City|date=2021-03-18|accessdate=2021-07-25}}</ref>
== Deities Enshrined == * {{nihongo|Tajimamori|田道間守}} – Deity of sweets and mikan cultivation * {{nihongo|Kumanoza-no-Ōkami|熊野坐大神}} – Associated with the Kumano faith
== Access == * Located in {{ill|Kainan, Wakayama|en|Kainan, Wakayama|qid=Q841309|lt=Kainan, Wakayama|1=Kainan, Wakayama}}, accessible via local transportation routes.
== Gallery == <gallery> File:Kitsumoto Shrine Entrance.jpg|Entrance to Kitsumoto Shrine </gallery>
== See Also == * {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines (Kainan)|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines (海南市)|1=List of Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines (Kainan City)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines (Kainan)|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}}
